SAP Portfolio Briefing | May 2026 · FY2026 |
YTD Consumed $39.0M YTD Budget $44.6M YTD ACV $50.7M | Proj Consumed $93.7M Proj Budget $108.3M Proj ACV $121.6M
AbbVie Inc
Life sciences and healthcare
YTD Consumed: $1.4M
YTD Budget: $1.8M
YTD ACV: $1.9M
Projected Consumed: $3.3M
Projected Budget: $4.5M
Projected ACV: $4.6M
EA Insights and Actions
AbbVie has contracted a full SAP estate spanning Data & AI, Technology Foundation, Finance, Spend, and Supply Chain, but BTP — the connective platform underpinning all of them — sits at $0 across every tenant (BDC Public $20K, Database Cloud $20.7K, Enterprise Agreement ~$82.8K YTD unconsumed), meaning every domain is operating as an island and the unified data, AI, and integration narrative the architecture was sized for cannot be delivered.
The customer's consumption pattern is architecturally inverted across the enterprise — high-touch transactional layers (Concur T&E at 115–135%, Ariba B&I at 100%+, SAC BI, Traceability Hub) are over- or fully consuming while the foundational layers beneath them (BTP, SSO at $0, SAP Build at $54, Batch Release Hub at $0, Ariba SLP declining to 81%) are dormant, signalling AbbVie is running production workloads without the governance, identity, extensibility, and closed-loop backbones that make those workloads compliant and scalable.
For a regulated life sciences manufacturer, the combination of a dormant Batch Release Hub, half-scoped Traceability Hub, absent SSO across Concur/Ariba, and zero BTP integration fabric means AbbVie's GxP closed-loop, identity assurance, and auditable data lineage stories are architecturally broken at exactly the layers regulators and internal audit examine first.
Across a combined contracted envelope where roughly $145K+ of BTP and platform entitlements are at $0 while SAC BI, SAC Planning, and Traceability Hub each show six-figure YTD shortfalls, AbbVie is on a trajectory to forfeit well over $1M of FY2026 value not from individual adoption failures but from one shared root cause — the platform layer that links them was never activated.
Convene an enterprise architecture review with AbbVie's CIO, Chief Data Officer, and Head of Digital Manufacturing to designate a single accountable owner for BTP activation across Data & AI, Technology Foundation, Finance, and Supply Chain, treating BTP as the shared dependency rather than four separate domain conversations.
Launch a cross-domain integration workshop that sequences a first production BTP workload connecting SAC BI/Planning, Concur/Ariba, and Traceability Hub data flows, so a single activation unlocks attainment lift in multiple stalled domains simultaneously.
Initiate a GxP and identity architecture assessment that pairs Batch Release Hub activation with SSO rollout across Concur and Ariba, closing the two highest-risk compliance gaps in the estate before they surface in audit or regulatory inspection.
Run a portfolio-level commercial realignment that trues up over-consuming Concur and Ariba B&I capacity against the dormant BTP, SSO, Batch Release Hub, and SAP Build entitlements, rebalancing the contract envelope toward where AbbVie is actually deriving and will derive architectural value.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
48.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$160K
YTD Budget$332K
YTD ACV: $334K
Projected Consumed: $385K · Projected Budget: $839K · Projected ACV: $802K
Solution Insights and Actions
AbbVie's Data & AI foundation is architecturally inverted — the analytics consumption layer (SAC BI) is live while the underlying BTP platform that should feed it with integrated data, AI services, and extensions sits at $0 across both BDC Public and Database Cloud tenants, meaning the LoB is consuming dashboards on islands of data without the agentic AI, integration, and data fabric capabilities BTP was contracted to provide.
SAC BI adoption has plateaued at 41–46% attainment ($30K against a $66K monthly budget, $160K YTD vs $331K YTD budget) precisely because the planning, Joule AI, and unified data scenarios that justify the full license are gated by the dormant BTP layer — AbbVie is using SAC BI as a static reporting tool rather than the unified analytics-and-planning capability it was licensed for.
Two parallel BTP Enterprise Agreements (BDC Public $20,031 and Database Cloud $20,722) are both cold-start shelfware with zero consumption over four months, indicating no integration, extension, or data pipeline workloads have been onboarded — the architectural backbone for connecting SAP and non-SAP data into AbbVie's analytics estate is contracted but absent.
The combined picture is a Data & AI line of business with visualization but no platform — without BTP activation, the 'trusted AI,' 'business context,' and 'unified planning' capabilities promised by SAC BI cannot be delivered, and AbbVie is on track to forfeit roughly $454K of combined SAC BI and BTP value this fiscal year.
Activate both BTP Enterprise Agreements by standing up a first production workload — a data pipeline or integration flow that feeds SAC BI — to break the $0 baseline on $40,753 of dual-tenant BTP budget and unlock the platform dependency blocking SAC BI expansion.
Run a joint SAC BI and BTP use-case workshop with AbbVie analytics and platform owners to define 2–3 net-new planning models or AI-enabled dashboards that consume BTP data services, lifting SAC BI off its $30K monthly plateau toward the $66K budget line.
Convert dormant SAC BI named licenses and ad-hoc Excel reporting workflows onto governed SAC stories and planning models to close the $171K YTD attainment gap and shift the LoB from steady-state reporting into the unified analytics-and-planning posture the contract was sized for.
Establish a single Data & AI consumption governance cadence covering SAC BI and both BTP tenants, with named workload owners accountable for monthly burn against the combined $839K annual ceiling and explicit milestones for BTP service enablement.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
19.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$109
YTD Budget$547
YTD ACV: $405
Projected Consumed: $261 · Projected Budget: $84K · Projected ACV: $971
Solution Insights and Actions
AbbVie's entire BTP Enterprise Agreement foundation is dormant across all three architectural pillars — Joule Studio (AI/agentic development), Integration Suite (process integration), and broader Technology Foundation services — meaning the platform contracted to unify AI, integration, and extensibility for the LoB is delivering none of its intended connective tissue between SAP and non-SAP systems.
SAP Build registered only $54 of early activity before flat-lining, indicating a single pilot team experimented with low-code app development but the citizen-developer and fusion-team model never scaled — leaving AbbVie without the extension layer that BTP is architecturally designed to feed into.
The combined dormancy of BTP Enterprise Agreement and stalled SAP Build means AbbVie has no active path to extend, automate, or AI-enable core SAP processes, so any custom app, workflow, or integration need is likely being met by shadow tooling outside the SAP estate — eroding the strategic rationale for the Technology Foundation investment.
With ~$82,800 of cumulative BTP entitlement unconsumed YTD against the full annual envelope and only a fraction of the fiscal year remaining, the architectural risk is no longer underutilization — it is contract value evaporation without a single production workload anchored on the platform.
Stand up a joint BTP activation workshop with AbbVie platform, integration, and AI leads to designate a named technical owner and select one initial workload per pillar — an Integration Suite flow, a Joule Studio agent or extension, and a Technology Foundation data/runtime service.
Re-engage the original SAP Build sponsor to diagnose the post-February stall and re-anchor Build as the low-code front door to BTP by sequencing two to three workflow or app use cases that consume Integration Suite and Joule Studio services downstream.
Establish a monthly consumption and architecture governance checkpoint covering all three BTP line items and SAP Build, tying each activated workload to a specific LoB capability outcome rather than tracking burn-down in isolation.
Build a prioritized BTP use-case roadmap for AbbVie covering integration, extension, automation, and AI-with-context scenarios so the Enterprise Agreement transitions from dormant entitlement to a coordinated foundation supporting Technology Foundation outcomes.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
97.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$1.1M
YTD Budget$1.1M
YTD ACV: $1.3M
Projected Consumed: $2.6M · Projected Budget: $2.7M · Projected ACV: $3.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
AbbVie's Travel & Expense stack (Concur Expense, ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, Reporting, TripLink, Direct Travel, Company Bill, Connectors, Preferred Success, WalkMe) is uniformly over-consuming budget at 115–135% attainment — the entire T&E architecture is operating as a tightly integrated, production-embedded suite, but the commercial envelope was sized below actual business demand, putting the LoB at risk of capacity throttling across mission-critical travel and expense workflows.
Procurement is architecturally bifurcated — Ariba Buying & Invoicing is over-consuming at 100%+ attainment while Ariba SLP is declining toward 81%, meaning AbbVie is transacting purchases and invoices at scale without a matching cadence of supplier qualification and performance governance, eroding the upstream control that makes downstream P2P spend compliant.
The Financial Planning & Analysis domain is stalled at the platform layer — SAC Planning is locked in a flat $35K/month single-workload pattern at 57% attainment while the BTP Enterprise Agreement sits at $0 consumption, so the LoB has neither expanded planning scenarios nor activated the extensibility platform needed to integrate planning with operational data.
Identity and access foundation is absent — Single Sign-on is fully dormant at $0 against $17 budget, meaning AbbVie's heavily-adopted Concur and Ariba user base is authenticating without the consolidated SSO layer that was contracted to secure and streamline access across the Finance and Spend Management estate.
Initiate a portfolio-level true-up across the Concur suite (Expense, ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, Reporting Services, TripLink, Direct Travel, Company Bill Statement, Connectors, Preferred Success, WalkMe Premium) to realign ACV ceilings with the demonstrated ~$190K aggregate projected overage before throughput is throttled.
Rebalance the Procurement architecture by auditing stalled supplier qualification workflows in Ariba SLP and tying renewed supplier onboarding cadence to the high-volume Ariba Buying & Invoicing transaction flow so spend governance keeps pace with spend execution.
Convene a joint FP&A and BTP activation workshop to expand SAC Planning beyond its single static workload into additional finance and workforce planning models, using BTP as the integration and extensibility fabric to close both the $311K SAC shortfall and the $4K dormant BTP entitlement.
Launch a Single Sign-on activation engagement with AbbVie's identity and security stakeholders to integrate SSO with the highest-traffic Concur and Ariba applications, converting the dormant entitlement into the authentication backbone for the active Spend Management estate.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
32.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$122K
YTD Budget$379K
YTD ACV: $269K
Projected Consumed: $292K · Projected Budget: $867K · Projected ACV: $645K
Solution Insights and Actions
AbbVie's Make & Deliver compliance architecture is structurally incomplete — Traceability Hub is operational at roughly half its intended scope while Batch Release Hub remains entirely dormant, meaning serialization data is being captured for some flows but the GxP electronic batch release process it's supposed to feed is still running on legacy/paper rails, breaking the intended closed-loop quality-to-release workflow.
Traceability Hub adoption has plateaued at a fixed serialization footprint with Jan–Apr consumption flat at ~$40K against rising $72K–$76K monthly budgets, indicating AbbVie has onboarded an initial regulatory scope (likely DSCSA or EU FMD core SKUs) but has not extended multi-ingredient traceability, recall streamlining, or sustainability measurement to additional sites, products, or markets the contract was sized for.
The May -$40,034 negative consumption posting on Traceability Hub is a data integrity event that invalidates current attainment reporting and must be resolved before the $574,939 remaining gap can be credibly reforecast or expansion ROI defended internally.
Batch Release Hub's $0 YTD against a $480 annual contract signals a pre-adoption posture rather than failed adoption, but with 7 months left the window to realize digitized release decision dashboards, lead-time reduction, and compliance cost takeout in FY2026 is rapidly closing.
Reconcile the -$40,034 May Traceability Hub entry with AbbVie finance and SAP billing to restore reporting integrity before any expansion or renewal conversation proceeds.
Stand up a Batch Release Hub activation plan with AbbVie's quality and manufacturing release owners targeting a first pilot site or product line to generate initial consumption and validate the integration path into existing GxP systems.
Run a Traceability Hub scope expansion workshop with AbbVie supply chain and serialization leads to identify untapped SKUs, manufacturing sites, and regulatory markets that can lift monthly consumption beyond the $40K plateau.
Architect the integration between Traceability Hub serialization data and Batch Release Hub release workflows so that AbbVie realizes the intended closed-loop value of track-and-trace feeding electronic batch release decisions rather than operating them as disconnected investments.
Abbott Laboratories
Life sciences and healthcare
YTD Consumed: $1.5M
YTD Budget: $1.8M
YTD ACV: $2.1M
Projected Consumed: $3.7M
Projected Budget: $4.3M
Projected ACV: $5.1M
EA Insights and Actions
Abbott's enterprise architecture exhibits a consistent 'plan-but-don't-execute' pattern across every L1 domain — IBP plans without execution-layer fulfillment, Ariba Sourcing decisions without Buying/Invoicing or Supplier Risk enforcement, SAC Planning without rolling forecast operationalization, and Incentive payouts without Cloud for Customer pipeline data — meaning Abbott has invested ~$3M+ in upstream decision systems whose outputs are handed off to non-SAP or manual downstream processes, breaking the closed-loop value chain that justifies an integrated SAP estate.
Integration Suite running at 106% with a $44K overage while HANA Cloud sits at 65% and SAP Build at 44% reveals that Abbott is wiring systems together faster than it is building the data fabric or extension surfaces those integrations should feed — the nervous system is scaling without a brain or hands, which for a regulated life-sciences manufacturer means integration spend is accruing as middleware cost rather than enabling AI-driven quality, supply, or commercial outcomes.
The front-to-back office data chain is architecturally broken — Cloud for Customer at 53–63% starves Incentive Management of trustworthy opportunity data, Supplier Risk at 26% starves Ariba Sourcing of risk signals, and Data Intelligence Cloud's flat run-rate starves HANA Cloud and SAC of new data products — indicating that across CX, Procurement, and Data & AI, Abbott has bought systems of record that are not being fed as systems of record, undermining the single-source-of-truth posture required for FDA, ESG, and financial reporting integrity.
Across a contracted footprint exceeding ~$5M ACV, Abbott is simultaneously over-consuming the operational layers (Integration Suite +$44K, SAC BI +$4K, Preferred Success Ariba +$71K projected, IBP at 100% with zero headroom) and under-consuming the strategic platforms (HANA Cloud -$63K, SAP Build, Cloud for Customer -$70K, Supplier Risk, Data Intelligence) — a commercial signature of a customer paying for transformation capacity while only operating legacy-equivalent workloads, putting the next renewal cycle at risk of downward rebaselining unless strategic capabilities are activated.
Commission an enterprise-wide closed-loop architecture review that maps each L1 planning/decision system (IBP, Ariba Sourcing, SAC Planning, Incentive Management) to its required SAP execution and data-feedback counterpart, exposing where non-SAP handoffs are breaking the integrated value chain.
Launch a BTP foundation activation program — pairing Integration Suite's active iFlows with concrete SAP Build extensions, HANA Cloud workload migration, and Data Intelligence pipeline expansion — to convert Abbott's integration overage into a coherent data-and-extension fabric rather than isolated middleware spend.
Establish a cross-LoB data integrity workstream that mandates Cloud for Customer as the opportunity system of record feeding Incentive Management, and Supplier Risk as the mandatory gate feeding Ariba Sourcing, to restore the system-of-record discipline required for regulated reporting.
Open a portfolio-level commercial rebalancing conversation that redirects unconsumed ACV from dormant strategic capabilities (HANA Cloud, Build, Cloud for Customer, Supplier Risk) toward the overruns in Integration Suite, SAC BI, IBP, and Preferred Success Ariba, protecting renewal value while funding activation of the dormant strategic layer.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
74.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$66K
YTD Budget$89K
YTD ACV: $113K
Projected Consumed: $159K · Projected Budget: $219K · Projected ACV: $272K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation at Abbott is architecturally fragmented — HANA Cloud (the business data fabric meant to anchor AI and analytical workloads) is running at 65% attainment with flat $8K–$9.6K monthly consumption while SAC BI is saturated at 112%, meaning the visualization layer is hitting a ceiling on a data platform that is materially underutilized.
Data Intelligence Cloud is operating as a steady-state pipeline runner rather than the orchestration backbone it is designed to be — flat $1,700–$2,000 monthly consumption tracking exactly to plan indicates no new data integrations or ML scenarios are being onboarded, which starves both HANA Cloud and SAC BI of net-new data products to analyze.
The Data & AI stack is delivering reporting but not the agentic AI and unified planning outcomes it was contracted for — HANA Cloud's vector/multi-model and business data fabric capabilities are dormant against a $63K projected annual shortfall, while SAC BI's planning and Joule-driven decisioning capabilities are constrained by an undersized entitlement.
Abbott has an inverted capacity posture across the analytics tier — the consumption layer (SAC BI) is over-subscribed by ~15% while the data layer (HANA Cloud) is under-consumed by ~38%, signaling demand is concentrated in dashboards on existing datasets rather than expanding the underlying data estate.
Re-baseline SAP Analytics Cloud BI entitlement to absorb the $4,153 projected overrun and profile the saturating user groups and dashboards to justify the capacity true-up before throttling impacts business reporting.
Launch a HANA Cloud workload expansion review with Abbott's data platform owners to migrate latent on-prem HANA workloads and activate vector and multi-model capabilities against the $23,659 YTD and $63,335 projected annual budget gap.
Commission a Data Intelligence Cloud use-case discovery with Abbott's data engineering lead to identify two new pipeline or ML orchestration scenarios that feed HANA Cloud, converting the flat run rate into expansion consumption against the remaining $13,147 budget.
Establish an architectural alignment review across Data Intelligence Cloud, HANA Cloud, and SAC BI to ensure the data fabric, ingestion, and consumption layers are scaled coherently rather than independently throttled or over-provisioned.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
106.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$137K
YTD Budget$129K
YTD ACV: $135K
Projected Consumed: $328K · Projected Budget: $286K · Projected ACV: $323K
Solution Insights and Actions
Abbott's Technology Foundation is architecturally lopsided — Integration Suite is running hot at 106% YTD attainment with a projected $44K overage on a $282K budget, while SAP Build sits at 44% projected consumption, meaning the LoB is heavily wiring systems together but barely extending them with custom apps or automations that would multiply the integration investment.
The citizen developer capability is effectively dormant — SAP Build's flatlined $112–$144 monthly consumption against a $258 monthly budget target indicates no builder community or use-case pipeline has formed, so process automation and app extension scenarios that should ride on top of the active integration backbone are being left to manual workarounds or shadow IT.
Integration Suite's sustained 7%+ monthly overage signals it has become the de facto nervous system for Abbott's SAP estate, but without a consumption deep-dive the LoB cannot distinguish strategic API/iFlow growth (AI agents, expanding integration footprint) from inefficient message patterns that will silently consume contracted capacity before year-end.
Convene a Technology Foundation architecture review with Abbott to connect Integration Suite's active iFlows and APIs to concrete SAP Build app and workflow scenarios, turning integration endpoints into business-facing extensions that justify both platforms.
Stand up an SAP Build center of enablement and run a use-case expansion workshop to identify 3–5 automation or app scenarios that close the $935 YTD Build gap and activate the dormant low-code capability.
Initiate a consumption deep-dive on Integration Suite's iFlows, APIs, and message volumes to classify the overage as strategic vs inefficient before opening a capacity expansion or commercial restructuring conversation to align ACV with the $326K run-rate.
Establish a joint Build + Integration Suite governance forum with Abbott so new integrations are paired with low-code consumers, preventing the current pattern where integration capacity scales while extension capacity stalls.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
59.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$198K
YTD Budget$332K
YTD ACV: $384K
Projected Consumed: $476K · Projected Budget: $799K · Projected ACV: $923K
Solution Insights and Actions
Abbott's Customer Experience architecture is operating with an inverted adoption pattern — Incentive Management (the back-office comp engine) ran at near-100% of plan through April while Cloud for Customer (the front-office system of engagement) is stuck at 53-63% attainment, meaning sales reps are being paid on outcomes that aren't being captured, governed, or pipelined in the CRM of record.
Cloud for Customer is architecturally positioned as the lead-to-cash front door but is functioning as a partial deployment — the persistent ~40% attainment gap and $70K projected under-consumption indicate dormant sales and service user populations, leaving opportunity management, case routing, and customer 360 capabilities unrealized and weakening the data feed into Incentive Management's quota and attainment calculations.
The May -$51,754 entry in Incentive Management is a billing artifact rather than an adoption signal, but it masks the fact that the underlying commission, dispute, and payee workflows have been the only reliably adopted CX capability at Abbott — making reconciliation urgent to preserve confidence in the one stable pillar of this LoB's architecture.
Reconcile the May Incentive Management reversal with finance to restate true consumption and confirm that comp plan calculation, dispute, and payee transaction workflows remain operational at the ~$52K monthly run-rate.
Launch a Cloud for Customer activation program focused on dormant sales and service licenses, mandating CRM pipeline and case management as the system of record so that opportunity and quota data flowing into Incentive Management is complete and trustworthy.
Convene an executive business review with Abbott to rebaseline the $70K unconsumed Cloud for Customer ACV against two anchor use cases — lead-to-opportunity and service ticket routing — with named business owners accountable for monthly active user growth.
Establish an integration checkpoint between Cloud for Customer opportunity data and Incentive Management attainment calculations to close the architectural loop between front-office selling behavior and back-office compensation outcomes.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
77.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$516K
YTD Budget$670K
YTD ACV: $883K
Projected Consumed: $1.2M · Projected Budget: $1.6M · Projected ACV: $2.1M
Solution Insights and Actions
Abbott's Source-to-Pay architecture is structurally incomplete: Ariba Sourcing runs at ~89% attainment while Buying and Invoicing stalls at 56% and Supplier Risk at 26% — meaning sourcing decisions are being made but the downstream invoicing, compliance, and risk-monitoring layers that convert those decisions into governed, de-risked spend are dormant, leaving Abbott with negotiated savings that aren't being enforced at the transaction layer.
The Finance plane mirrors the same pattern as Procurement — SAC Planning is stuck at ~70% of plan with May regressing to 63%, indicating Abbott has operationalized a narrow slice of financial planning models but has not unified financial and operational planning across workforce, OpEx, or rolling forecast, so the LoB has planning infrastructure without an enterprise planning rhythm.
Preferred Success Ariba is the only Procurement asset running hot at 120% YTD attainment against saturated monthly ceilings, which signals Abbott is leaning on guided success engagements to compensate for the underlying adoption gaps in Buying/Invoicing and Supplier Risk rather than driving native platform self-sufficiency.
Ariba Supplier Risk's flat ~$700/month consumption against a $39.5K annual envelope means risk signals are not flowing into sourcing or supplier lifecycle decisions, breaking the architectural intent of embedded risk-aware procurement and exposing Abbott's tier-1/tier-2 supply base to unmonitored financial, ESG, and geopolitical exposure.
Close the Procure-to-Pay execution gap by running a buying-channel and invoice-routing audit on Ariba Buying and Invoicing to redirect off-platform PO and invoice volume and lift attainment above the 60% ceiling.
Embed Ariba Supplier Risk into the sourcing and supplier onboarding workflow by enrolling tier-1/tier-2 suppliers into continuous monitoring and activating risk alerts, engagement projects, and control-based screening.
Expand SAC Planning beyond its current financial-planning footprint into workforce, OpEx, and rolling forecast models to convert the tool from a static planning repository into a recurring close-and-forecast rhythm.
Rebalance the Preferred Success Ariba engagement toward enablement that builds Abbott's self-sufficiency on Buying/Invoicing and Supplier Risk, and open a contract expansion conversation to cover the projected $71K overrun before capacity is exhausted.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
100.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$618K
YTD Budget$618K
YTD ACV: $618K
Projected Consumed: $1.5M · Projected Budget: $1.5M · Projected ACV: $1.5M
Solution Insights and Actions
Abbott's Supply Chain Management architecture is single-pillared on IBP planning with no visible execution-layer adoption signals — the LoB has institutionalized forward-looking demand, supply, and S&OP orchestration but the absence of complementary fulfillment, response, or control-tower products means planning decisions are handed off to non-SAP execution systems, limiting closed-loop visibility from plan to delivery.
The textbook-linear $123,616/month IBP burn confirms a mature, embedded S&OP cadence rather than experimental usage — Abbott has operationalized at least the core planning modules (demand, supply, S&OP), but the perfectly flat consumption pattern signals a steady-state deployment with no evidence of advanced capabilities like AI-powered demand sensing, inventory optimization, or scenario simulation being activated within the current envelope.
At 100% YTD attainment with $865K of ACV remaining against 7 months, Abbott will fully consume the $1.48M IBP contract with zero headroom — architecturally this means any new planning use case, additional planning area, or business unit onboarding cannot be absorbed without a commercial expansion, capping the LoB's ability to broaden planning maturity mid-cycle.
Validate with Abbott's supply chain planning leads which IBP modules (demand, supply, S&OP, inventory, response) are driving the steady burn and which remain dormant, to expose untapped capabilities like AI-powered demand sensing and multilevel supply planning already covered under the existing contract.
Initiate a discovery on extending the SCM architecture beyond planning into execution and response — surfacing whether Abbott has a control-tower or order-promising gap that prevents IBP plans from being actioned in fulfillment.
Open a capacity-expansion conversation tied to specific new use cases (scenario planning, what-if simulation, inventory optimization, or additional business units) since the current ACV trajectory leaves no room for adoption growth.
Document the operational planning processes anchored on the $123,616/month consumption to build a defensible renewal justification and protect IBP as the system of record for S&OP at Abbott.
Border States Industries, Inc.
Wholesale distribution
YTD Consumed: $410K
YTD Budget: $454K
YTD ACV: $399K
Projected Consumed: $984K
Projected Budget: $1.1M
Projected ACV: $957K
EA Insights and Actions
BTP is the connective tissue underpinning every L1 domain at Border States — Data & AI, T&E, HCM, and Commerce all depend on Integration Suite for downstream flows — yet the foundation layer is structurally undersized ($139K projected vs. $102K budget) while Commerce Cloud sits two-thirds dormant and SF Compensation runs at $0, meaning the one platform that must scale is overrunning while the domains it should be activating are starved.
The customer has assembled architecturally complete suites in HCM (EC + Payroll live) and Finance/T&E (full capture-to-pay-to-audit chain consuming) but the cross-domain integration nodes — SF Compensation bridging Performance→Payroll, and Concur Expense feeding Finance posting — are either dark or behaviorally broken (May $507 cliff), meaning Border States owns end-to-end process suites that are not yet executing as end-to-end processes.
Border States' overall contract posture is bifurcated — foundation and operational layers (BTP Integration, Joule Studio, T&E capture/audit) are over-consuming by ~25–110% while strategic activation layers (Commerce Cloud at 32%, Talent suite at 41–94%, SF Compensation at 0%) sit idle — signaling a portfolio where the customer pays for transformation capacity but operates a transactional footprint, undermining the digital distributor growth narrative typical of an electrical wholesale leader.
The Data & AI domain runs entirely through the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement with no named Datasphere, SAC, or AI Core SKUs, while Joule Studio is overrunning 210% on the foundation tier — meaning AI demand is real and growing but the customer has no governed data product layer to anchor it, leaving generative and analytical AI workloads architecturally rootless across HCM, Finance, and Commerce data.
Convene a cross-domain integration architecture review focused on the three broken bridges — SF Compensation↔Payroll, Concur Expense↔Finance posting, and Commerce Cloud↔Customer/Order data — to convert disconnected suites into closed-loop business processes.
Open a consolidated CPEA re-baselining conversation that simultaneously right-sizes the over-consuming foundation and T&E layers against the demonstrably dormant Commerce Cloud, Talent, and SF Compensation entitlements rather than negotiating each domain in isolation.
Commission a BTP service-mix decomposition across Integration Suite, Joule Studio, and the CPEA-funded Data & AI envelope to confirm which named services (Datasphere, AI Core, SAC) are actually carrying workload and establish a governed Data & AI reference architecture before Joule expansion outpaces data foundations.
Run a clean-core and capability-activation workshop with Border States' CIO and LoB owners to reframe the ~$120K of combined dormant entitlement (Commerce, Talent, Compensation) as funded runway for the distributor's omnichannel and workforce growth agenda rather than as commercial overhang.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
93.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$81K
YTD Budget$87K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $195K · Projected Budget: $216K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
Border States' Data & AI footprint in BAIP rests on a single foundational contract — the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement — meaning every data, analytics, and AI workload the LoB runs is consolidated under one consumption envelope, with no specialized Data & AI SKUs layered on top to differentiate governed data, ML, or generative AI workloads.
The 93.2% YTD attainment with three months over 100% indicates the LoB has operationalized BTP-based data and AI capabilities into recurring business processes rather than treating them as experimental, but the absence of named Data & AI products in the portfolio means we cannot confirm whether consumption is flowing to high-value services (e.g., Datasphere, SAC, AI Core) versus low-leverage compute.
At the current run rate (~$195K vs. $215,815 ceiling), Border States will leave ~$20K of pre-committed Data & AI capacity unconsumed, representing latent architectural headroom that could underwrite expansion into governed data products or AI scenarios without incremental procurement.
Decompose Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement consumption by underlying BTP service to confirm whether Data & AI workloads (Datasphere, SAC, AI Core, HANA Cloud) are the actual drivers, and identify which Data & AI capabilities are dormant despite being entitled.
Position the projected ~$20K unconsumed ceiling as funded runway for activating an adjacent Data & AI capability — such as Joule, AI Core, or Datasphere replication flows — that complements existing workloads rather than adding net-new spend.
Validate with the LoB whether the recurring monthly overages reflect production-grade Data & AI usage that warrants rebalancing the monthly budget against the remaining $134K of contract value, or whether they signal an unmanaged consumption pattern that needs governance.

Projected Consumed: $139K · Projected Budget: $102K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
BTP is the integration and extension backbone for Border States' SAP estate, and the dual-track overconsumption on Integration Suite (117% YTD, steady $7K/month) plus Joule Studio (~210% YTD) signals that both enterprise integration flows and AI-assisted extension development have moved from pilot into embedded production use — the technology foundation is actively powering LoB workloads, not sitting idle.
The tight, non-volatile monthly burn pattern on Integration Suite indicates structural iPaaS dependency for steady-state interface traffic, meaning any ceiling breach or throttling risk translates directly into business process disruption across the LoBs that rely on those integrations.
Joule Studio's outsized overrun against a comparatively small budget reveals the AI/Joule extensibility capability was under-provisioned at contract time relative to actual developer and business demand, leaving generative AI enablement architecturally available but commercially constrained.
With combined projected consumption (~$139K) materially exceeding combined budget (~$102K), the LPR868 enterprise agreement is undersized for the foundation tier overall, putting downstream LoB roadmaps dependent on BTP integration and AI extensions at financial governance risk rather than technical risk.
Open a consolidated CPEA true-up conversation covering both Integration Suite and Joule Studio to re-baseline the LPR868 ceiling against the ~$139K projected run-rate and remove overage exposure on the foundation layer.
Commission a BTP service-mix and workload audit on Integration Suite to identify the top consuming iflows and evaluate reserved capacity, message packaging, or architectural consolidation as unit-cost levers.
Validate the Joule Studio use cases driving the $4K+/month burn to confirm they map to sanctioned AI extension scenarios and establish a governance model before consumption scales further unchecked.
Map the BTP integration and Joule-built extensions to the specific LoB processes they support so that the commercial right-sizing conversation is framed in terms of business capability continuity, not just platform spend.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
32.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$17K
YTD Budget$54K
YTD ACV: $94K
Projected Consumed: $42K · Projected Budget: $132K · Projected ACV: $225K
Solution Insights and Actions
Border States' Customer Experience architecture is anchored on a single SAP Commerce Cloud deployment running one static workload, meaning the platform's multi-storefront, B2B/B2C/B2B2C, and catalog personalization capabilities are architecturally available but operationally dormant — the LoB has bought a unified commerce backbone and is using it as a single-channel storefront.
The flat $3,463 monthly burn against a $10,921 budget baseline indicates that AI innovation, omnichannel expansion, and buyer segmentation capabilities are licensed but unactivated, leaving Border States without the cross-channel sales growth and personalization outcomes Commerce Cloud is designed to deliver.
With projected annual consumption at $41,563 against a $131,635 budget, roughly two-thirds of the contracted Commerce footprint is generating no business value, signaling a mismatch between the commercial commitment and the LoB's current digital commerce ambition.
Run a Commerce Cloud capability activation workshop with Border States' digital commerce owner to map dormant capabilities — additional B2B portals, distributor storefronts, and segmented catalogs — against the $90K annual consumption gap.
Engage Border States to scope an AI and personalization activation track on Commerce Cloud, converting unused entitlement into measurable conversion, AOV, and buyer engagement outcomes.
Diagnose why Commerce Cloud consumption has been frozen in a $3,378–$3,554 band for five months and determine whether the constraint is traffic, catalog scope, or organizational ownership of digital commerce.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
116.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$67K
YTD Budget$57K
YTD ACV: $68K
Projected Consumed: $160K · Projected Budget: $137K · Projected ACV: $162K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Travel & Expense stack is architecturally complete and operationally embedded — Expense capture (ExpenseIt), submission (Expense), compliance enforcement (Intelligent Audit), reimbursement (Expense Pay), and analytics (Reporting Services) are all consuming against contract, meaning Border States is realizing the full end-to-end T&E value chain rather than running partial automation.
Four of five T&E products are structurally over-consuming (ExpenseIt 137%, Intelligent Audit 127%, Reporting Services 132%, Expense pre-May 127%+), indicating the contracted capacity envelope was sized below the LoB's actual operational footprint and the business is on track for a combined ~$22K annual overage exposure.
The May collapse in Concur Expense from $6,159 to $507 while Expense Pay, ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, and Reporting Services all continued at normal volume is architecturally inconsistent — downstream payment, audit, and reporting flows depend on submitted expense reports, so either a submission/approval workflow has broken upstream or the May signal is a data/reporting artifact masking continued usage.
Expense Pay is the only T&E component tracking on-plan with $155 of headroom, signaling that reimbursement automation is sized correctly but is the narrowest pipe in a system where capture and audit volumes are growing 30%+ faster than provisioned, creating a future bottleneck if employee population or expense types expand.
Diagnose the May Concur Expense consumption cliff with Border States AP/Finance to determine whether expense submission, approval routing, SSO, or reporting telemetry has been disrupted, since downstream Expense Pay and Intelligent Audit volumes contradict a true usage stoppage.
Consolidate a single true-up conversation across ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, and Reporting Services to right-size the T&E contract envelope to the demonstrated ~$87K run rate rather than negotiating three separate overage remediations.
Conduct a T&E policy and configuration audit to confirm the sustained 125–138% attainment across capture, audit, and reporting reflects legitimate business volume growth rather than duplicate submissions, misconfigured audit rules, or unmanaged report proliferation.
Position Expense Pay's $155 of unused capacity as a lever to broaden reimbursement coverage to additional entities or expense categories, aligning the payment layer's footprint with the higher-volume capture and audit layers upstream.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
87.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$187K
YTD Budget$214K
YTD ACV: $237K
Projected Consumed: $448K · Projected Budget: $517K · Projected ACV: $570K
Solution Insights and Actions
The HCM foundation is architecturally sound — Employee Central and HXM Operations both hit 100% May attainment, meaning the core HR system of record and cross-functional workforce processes are fully operationalized and ready to serve as the trusted data spine for downstream talent, payroll, and learning modules.
The talent suite is contracted but architecturally stranded — Performance and Goals, Recruiting, Onboarding, and Career and Talent Development all sit in flat, capped consumption bands (41–94% attainment) while Employee Central runs at 100%, meaning Border States has a healthy HR system of record feeding a talent layer that is only partially wired into the workforce, leaving career pathing, performance-to-comp linkage, and hire-to-productive journeys structurally incomplete.
The pay-for-performance loop is broken at the compensation node — SF Compensation shows $0 consumed against its $550 budget while Performance and Goals and Employee Central Payroll are both live, meaning performance data is being captured and payroll is executing pay, but there is no automated merit/bonus planning bridge between them, forcing comp decisions to happen outside the integrated HXM architecture.
Payroll is operationally stable but scope-constrained — Employee Central Payroll oscillates around 96% attainment with a projected $3,378 annual shortfall, signaling the gross-to-net engine runs reliably for the licensed population but secondary capabilities like retroactive processing, off-cycle workbench, and finance posting are dormant, leaving regulatory and finance-integration value unrealized.
Activate SF Compensation by configuring comp plan templates, eligibility rules, and worksheets so performance ratings from SF Performance and Goals flow into merit and bonus planning and then into Employee Central Payroll, closing the pay-for-performance architectural gap.
Run a talent-suite utilization audit across Career and Talent Development, Onboarding, Recruiting, and Performance and Goals to identify which sub-modules (career worksheets, crossboarding, offer management, calibration, 360s) are dormant and convert the ~$30K+ combined annual ACV gap into active workflows tied to the Employee Central population.
Expand Employee Central Payroll scope into retroactive processing, off-cycle workbench, and posting to SAP Finance to absorb the projected $3,378 annual shortfall and complete the HR-to-finance integration the cloud payroll architecture is designed to deliver.
Convert Employee Central's 100% attainment into strategic depth by activating position management, workflows, and reporting dashboards so the system of record becomes a governance and analytics layer rather than just a transactional HR database.
CHS Inc.
Agribusiness
YTD Consumed: $1.6M
YTD Budget: $2.1M
YTD ACV: $2.3M
Projected Consumed: $3.9M
Projected Budget: $5.2M
Projected ACV: $5.4M
EA Insights and Actions
CHS has contracted ~$3M+ across SAC BI/Planning, SuccessFactors, IBP, and Signavio but the integrating fabric is failing in opposite directions — Integration Suite is 125% over-consumed and projecting a $23.9K overrun while the parallel BTP CPEA pools for extension, data, and AI sit at or near zero, meaning the LoB is funding an integrated enterprise architecture but operationalizing only the pipes, leaving every domain to run as an island.
The descriptive-to-predictive transition is blocked enterprise-wide — SAC BI runs at 108% on reporting while SAC Planning sits at 7.6%, IBP's AI demand sensing and Control Tower are dormant, Signavio has no Process Intelligence companion, and Joule capabilities across BDC and Joule Studio are untapped, meaning CHS has bought the forward-looking decisioning layer across Finance, Supply Chain, and Process domains but is operating the entire estate as a rear-view-mirror reporting business — a critical gap for an ag-cooperative where margin depends on demand, commodity, and supply foresight.
The talent-to-spend-to-plan value chain is architecturally severed — SuccessFactors Employee Central is healthy at 96.5% but Compensation, Performance, and Career are eroding; Fieldglass Contingent (39%) and Services Procurement (24%) are bypassed; and SAC Planning at 7.6% cannot ingest either signal — so CHS has no integrated view of total workforce cost (permanent + contingent) flowing into financial plans despite owning every module required to produce one.
Identity and access governance is structurally regressing while the SAP cloud footprint expands — Cloud IAG is flat and SSO is declining toward abandonment even as BDC, Integration Suite, Joule, and SuccessFactors capacity grows, meaning every newly provisioned tenant lands outside federated authentication and SoD coverage, creating a compounding compliance exposure for a regulated cooperative handling member financial and commodity data.
Convene an enterprise integration and BTP portfolio review to rebalance the dormant Technology Foundation, Database Cloud, and Finance CPEA pools against the saturated Integration Suite and BDC Public ceilings, establishing one cross-tenant consumption governance view before renewal.
Charter a cross-domain 'predictive activation' program that sequences SAC Planning (Finance), IBP Inventory/Response/Control Tower (Supply Chain), Signavio Process Intelligence (Transformation), and Joule (Data & AI) as one integrated decisioning roadmap rather than four independent module activations.
Run a total-workforce-cost architecture workshop linking SuccessFactors EC, Fieldglass Contingent and Services Procurement, and SAC Planning to re-route off-platform contingent and SOW spend back through the VMS and into the financial planning cycle.
Launch an IAG and SSO scope-expansion initiative to federate and govern all newly provisioned BTP, BDC, Joule, and SuccessFactors tenants before the access and SoD gap compounds further.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
110.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$321K
YTD Budget$290K
YTD ACV: $96K
Projected Consumed: $770K · Projected Budget: $773K · Projected ACV: $230K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI architecture for CHS is operating as a reporting-centric stack — SAC BI is embedded in active workflows at 108% of plan while the broader BDC Public Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement runs at 114% YTD, confirming analytics consumption is pulling platform spend but the unified planning, predictive, and Joule-driven decisioning layers of SAC remain largely untapped.
The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is bifurcated across BDC Public and Database Cloud with divergent adoption profiles — BDC Public is on track to exhaust its $512K ceiling with only $27,905 of headroom against $334K of back-half demand, while Database Cloud sits at 89% YTD attainment yet still projects a $1,400 overage, indicating the data foundation is under-leveraged even as the analytics surface is over-consuming.
The cooling attainment curve on SAC BI (112% in Q1 down to 103% in May) signals that net-new use case adoption has plateaued at descriptive reporting, leaving the LoB without the forward-looking planning and AI-driven decision capabilities the SAC investment was architected to deliver.
Run a joint capacity and use case review on the BDC Public Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement to map the $334K back-half demand against the $27,905 headroom and decide between true-forward, workload rationalization, or contract expansion before ceiling breach.
Launch a SAC use case discovery workshop with CHS focused on activating planning, predictive, and Joule capabilities so the platform evolves from BI reporting into unified financial and operational decisioning.
Reconcile the Database Cloud Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement consumption against the projected $1,400 overage and run an enablement sprint on underutilized database services to convert dormant entitlement into governed data foundation workloads supporting the analytics layer.
Establish a cross-tenant consumption governance view spanning BDC Public and Database Cloud so CHS can manage the Data & AI architecture as one integrated stack rather than two independently drifting contracts.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
106.7%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$112K
YTD Budget$105K
YTD ACV: $1K
Projected Consumed: $268K · Projected Budget: $248K · Projected ACV: $3K
Solution Insights and Actions
CHS's Technology Foundation is architecturally bifurcated — Integration Suite is the dominant workload consuming $77,797 (125% of plan) while a parallel CPEA pool earmarked for broader BTP services sits at $0, meaning the LoB is funding a platform strategy but only operationalizing the integration layer, leaving extension, data, and AI capabilities dormant.
SAP Build is technically live but strategically stalling — three consecutive months of declining consumption ($132→$114) against a $1,474 envelope indicate the low-code capability is not scaling into a citizen-developer practice, so the LoB's ability to extend SAP processes with custom apps and workflows is contracting rather than compounding.
Joule Studio's CPEA pool is tracking to a $2,225 annual overage with April spiking to 101.3%, signaling that AI and developer tooling workloads are embedded in active business use and approaching capacity limits that will constrain further Joule and generative AI adoption if left ungoverned.
Integration Suite's $23,917 projected overrun against a $162,795 ceiling shows the LoB is over-relying on integration as its primary BTP value driver while the decelerating attainment curve (132%→108%) suggests workload growth is plateauing rather than diversifying into the unused Technology Foundation pool.
Convene a BTP portfolio rebalancing review with CHS to redirect the dormant $4,394 Technology Foundation CPEA budget toward extension, data, or AI workloads that complement the saturated Integration Suite footprint.
Launch a Build re-engagement play tied to specific Integration Suite use cases, using the $841 unconsumed budget to fund low-code front-ends or workflow extensions on top of the integrations already in production.
Establish a unified CPEA governance checkpoint covering all three consumption pools to address the combined $26,142 projected overrun on active pools while activating the idle pool before renewal.
Run a Joule Studio workload review to diagnose the April spike and confirm whether AI-assisted development is scaling into a repeatable pattern that justifies expanding the commitment ceiling.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
30.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$9K
YTD Budget$30K
YTD ACV: $94K
Projected Consumed: $22K · Projected Budget: $73K · Projected ACV: $225K
Solution Insights and Actions
Business Transformation Management at CHS is architecturally a single-product footprint — Signavio Process Management is the only lever for process discovery, modeling, and governance, meaning the LoB has no companion capabilities (Process Intelligence, Process Governance, Process Insights) to close the loop from modeled processes to mined execution data, leaving transformation work descriptive rather than evidence-based.
The 5x ACV expansion from $9.4K to $46.9K signals CHS leadership committed to scale process transformation, but the declining consumption curve indicates the operating model to consume that capacity — process owners, modeling standards, governance rituals — was not stood up alongside the contract, so the investment is funding shelfware rather than transformation throughput.
At 30.8% YTD attainment and a trajectory toward ~$22K of $73K consumed, roughly two-thirds of contracted process management capacity is dormant, which means the LoB is paying for enterprise-wide process governance while operating at departmental-pilot intensity.
Anchor Signavio Process Management adoption to one end-to-end value stream (procure-to-pay or order-to-cash) with named process owners accountable for modeled, published, and governed process inventory tied to measurable transformation outcomes.
Establish a Process CoE cadence with recurring modeling, review, and publication rituals so consumption is driven by governance discipline rather than ad-hoc user activity.
Evaluate extending the Signavio footprint with Process Intelligence to pair modeled processes with execution mining, closing the architectural gap between as-designed and as-run that single-product Process Management cannot address alone.
Solution Area

34.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$200K
YTD Budget$578K
YTD ACV: $902K
Projected Consumed: $479K · Projected Budget: $1.5M · Projected ACV: $2.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
CHS's Finance and Spend Management architecture is broadly contracted but operationally inert — Fieldglass (Contingent + Services), SAC Planning, and Cloud Platform EA all show declining or zero consumption, meaning the LoB has purchased an integrated external workforce, planning, and extensibility stack but is executing spend and planning processes outside it.
The external workforce capability is architecturally complete yet operationally bypassed — both Fieldglass Contingent Workforce (~39% projected attainment) and Services Procurement (~24% projected attainment) are declining in parallel, indicating contingent labor and SOW spend at CHS is routing around the VMS and forfeiting the Ariba/SuccessFactors spend visibility the integration was designed to deliver.
Enterprise planning is functionally dormant — SAC Planning sits at 7.6% projected annual attainment with consumption shrinking into planning season, leaving CHS with a financial planning platform that cannot unify FP&A, workforce, and operational plans despite the $798K commitment, while the parallel Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement zero-consumption pattern signals the extensibility layer needed to integrate planning into business processes is also unactivated.
Identity and access governance is locked at a baseline footprint — Cloud IAG consumption is flat at $24-25K while SSO declines toward abandonment, meaning IAG is governing a fixed set of systems while the authentication layer beneath it erodes, leaving newly provisioned SAP cloud capacity outside both federated access and SoD risk coverage.
Run a joint Procurement-HR off-platform spend audit to re-route contingent labor and SOW requisitions back through Fieldglass Contingent Workforce and Services Procurement, restoring the Ariba and SuccessFactors integration path for total external workforce spend visibility.
Convene an FP&A activation workshop to stand up SAC Planning on CHS's core financial planning cycle with driver-based forecasts and workforce plans, and pair it with a Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement first-use-case scoping session so planning extensions and integrations have a live runtime.
Launch an IAG scope-expansion initiative to onboard additional connected systems, activate access certification and SoD risk analysis on unmonitored roles, and federate the remaining application landscape under Single Sign-on to halt the authentication-layer decline.
Escalate the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement zero-consumption pattern to the CHS account executive and technical architect to diagnose whether entitlement provisioning, skills, or sponsoring project absence is blocking activation before further budget lapses.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
88.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$275K
YTD Budget$311K
YTD ACV: $357K
Projected Consumed: $659K · Projected Budget: $753K · Projected ACV: $857K
Solution Insights and Actions
CHS has deployed the full SuccessFactors HCM suite with Employee Central as the system of record running at 96.5% attainment, but every downstream talent module (Compensation, Performance & Goals, Career Development, Learning) is underutilized or declining — the core HR foundation is healthy while the talent value layer it was meant to feed is architecturally stranded.
The Performance → Compensation → Career Development chain is broken in practice: Performance and Goals consumption is eroding monthly (81% → 77%), Compensation is actively declining (-$345 since Jan with a projected 19.5% shortfall), and Career Development is flatlined at 33% attainment — meaning skills, ratings, and pay decisions are not flowing through the integrated talent architecture SuccessFactors was designed to deliver.
Recruiting Management (94% attainment) and Onboarding (66% attainment) are out of sync architecturally — requisitions are being processed in the system but a third of new hires are bypassing the integrated onboarding workflow, fragmenting the hire-to-productive-employee journey that EC + Recruiting + Onboarding are contracted to deliver as one process.
Preferred Success is burning a flat $13,008/month at 120% of plan while the SuccessFactors modules it is meant to accelerate are stagnating or declining — the customer is over-consuming advisory entitlement without translating it into module adoption, indicating guided services are not being directed at the talent suite's weakest links.
Redirect Preferred Success engagements at CHS toward activating the dormant talent chain — specifically Performance & Goals calibration, Compensation worksheet adoption, and Career/Development planning — to convert the $24K projected overage into measurable adoption lift on the underperforming modules.
Run a talent process architecture review with CHS HR to re-anchor merit, bonus, and performance cycles inside SF Compensation and Performance & Goals, reversing the multi-month consumption decline and restoring the EC-to-pay data flow the suite was contracted to deliver.
Close the Recruiting-to-Onboarding handoff gap by auditing which business units and requisition types skip SF Onboarding after using Recruiting Management, and route those new hires through the integrated workflow to recover the $18,722 onboarding shortfall.
Launch a Career and Talent Development activation campaign tied to SF Learning content expansion, using EC skills data to drive role-based development plans and break both modules out of their flat consumption ceilings.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
88.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$692K
YTD Budget$785K
YTD ACV: $817K
Projected Consumed: $1.7M · Projected Budget: $1.9M · Projected ACV: $2.0M
Solution Insights and Actions
CHS's Supply Chain Management footprint is architecturally single-threaded on IBP, meaning demand sensing, inventory optimization, S&OP, and supply response all depend on one platform being used continuously — and the alternating consumption pattern shows the LoB is treating it as a periodic planning tool rather than the unified collaborative process it is designed to be.
The cyclical $132K–$147K consumption rhythm indicates only one or two IBP modules (likely S&OP or Demand) are running in batch cadence while Inventory Optimization, Response & Supply Planning, and Control Tower capabilities appear dormant, leaving CHS without the cross-functional balancing between commercial forecasts and supply constraints that justifies the IBP investment.
With YTD consumption of $691,761 against an $785,118 budget and an annual trajectory $253,523 short of the $1,913,747 ACV ceiling, the LoB is contractually entitled to roughly 13% more planning capability than it is operationalizing — a gap that represents unactivated planner cohorts or planning areas rather than overspend risk.
The sub-85% attainment in Jan, Mar, and May signals that AI-powered demand prediction and multilevel supply planning are not embedded in the monthly planning rhythm, so CHS is making supply decisions on stale or siloed forecasts during half of every quarter.
Activate dormant IBP modules — specifically Inventory Optimization and Response & Supply Planning — to convert IBP from a forecasting tool into the end-to-end S&OP backbone it is licensed to be.
Shift IBP from batch planning cycles to continuous forecast collaboration by onboarding additional demand and supply planner cohorts into the active planning areas.
Audit the off-month planning cadence (Jan, Mar, May) to identify which planning roles or business units are absent from the IBP process and drive their activation to stabilize attainment above 95%.
Enable Control Tower and AI-powered demand sensing capabilities to close the architectural gap between commercial signals and supply response, ensuring the full IBP value chain is operational rather than just the S&OP layer.
CLEVELAND-CLIFFS INC.
Mining
YTD Consumed: $119K
YTD Budget: $162K
YTD ACV: $215K
Projected Consumed: $285K
Projected Budget: $392K
Projected ACV: $516K
EA Insights and Actions
Cleveland-Cliffs has contracted four L1 domains (Data & AI, Integration, CX, Supply Chain) but every one is operating as a single-product island with no cross-domain connective tissue — IBP plans cannot flow to execution, SAC dashboards cannot consume governed APIs, and C4C cannot enrich customer 360 with supply or service signals, meaning the ~$390K annual enterprise commitment is funding four disconnected point tools rather than an integrated steel-industry value chain.
Integration Suite's dormancy at 18.8% attainment ($26/month static iFlow) is the architectural root cause that simultaneously caps SAC BI (no trusted data pipelines for analytics), IBP (no execution handoff for forecasts), and C4C (no order-to-customer or service-to-supply flows) — the connective platform underpinning all three LoBs is the lowest-adopted asset in the portfolio, structurally blocking every cross-domain AI and automation outcome.
IBP at $19K/month flat consumption and 64% attainment represents the only domain with meaningful spend yet operates as a standalone S&OP engine — for a steel manufacturer whose competitive position depends on demand volatility response and mill-to-customer orchestration, planning without connected manufacturing execution, logistics network, or CX demand signals leaves the largest SAP investment delivering siloed forecasts instead of supply chain resilience.
Across all four domains the adoption signature is identical — single product, flat consumption, low attainment, no platform extensions — indicating Cleveland-Cliffs has no enterprise architecture governance forcing capability activation against contracted value, with a combined YTD shortfall trajectory that will leave six-figure commitments unrealized and no AI/Joule-ready foundation in place for the next planning cycle.
Convene a cross-domain enterprise architecture review with Cleveland-Cliffs IT and LoB leadership to map the integration dependencies between IBP, SAC, C4C, and Integration Suite and produce a single sequenced activation roadmap rather than four parallel domain plans.
Prioritize Integration Suite activation as the enterprise unlock — stand up API Management, Event Mesh, and Trading Partner Management with iFlows that explicitly connect IBP outputs to execution systems, SAC to governed data sources, and C4C to order/service backends.
Commission a contract-to-architecture reconciliation across the ~$390K annual footprint to identify which dormant capabilities (SAC planning, IBP demand sensing/inventory, C4C service depth, BTP integration modules) should be activated, rescoped, or consolidated before renewal conversations.
Establish a steel-industry value-chain reference architecture session positioning Digital Manufacturing, Business Network, and Joule as the missing execution and AI layers that would make the existing planning, analytics, and CX investments operational at enterprise scale.

Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI footprint at Cleveland-Cliffs is architecturally singular — SAC BI is the sole analytics capability contracted, meaning the LoB has visualization and dashboarding but no integrated planning, AI-driven decisioning, or Joule-enabled analytics narrative working alongside it to deliver the full unified analytics-and-planning value proposition.
SAC BI is technically provisioned but functionally dormant at 17.4% YTD attainment, with 82% of consumption concentrated in a single April workload — this indicates a proof-of-concept pattern rather than embedded business intelligence, leaving operational and financial decision-makers without the trusted, context-rich analytics the platform is designed to deliver.
The 90% May collapse to $20 confirms there is no recurring dashboard cadence or persistent user base — without sustained consumption, the LoB cannot realize the 'faster decisions with trusted AI' outcome and risks SAC BI becoming shelfware against the $2,862 remaining annual commitment.
Diagnose the April $199 workload to identify the business question it answered, then templatize and scale that use case across Finance and Operations to convert isolated activity into a recurring analytics pattern.
Establish a governed content lifecycle in SAC BI — recurring dashboards, named business owners, and a published catalog — to move the LoB from ad-hoc visualization to embedded business intelligence.
Evaluate extending the Data & AI architecture with planning and Joule capabilities so SAC BI is connected to forward-looking financial and operational planning rather than operating as a standalone reporting tool.

Solution Insights and Actions
Integration Suite is deployed as a single-purpose pipe rather than an enterprise integration backbone — the flat $26/month consumption locked across five months indicates one static iFlow in production, leaving Cleveland-Cliffs' manufacturing and supply chain landscape without the API governance, event-driven orchestration, and AI-ready integration fabric the platform was contracted to deliver.
The 18.8% projected attainment against a $1,706 budget is not a sizing problem but an architectural dormancy signal — API Management, Trading Partner Management, and Event Mesh capabilities sit unused, meaning Cleveland-Cliffs cannot expose secure APIs for AI agent consumption, govern B2B partner exchanges with steel mill suppliers, or react to real-time production events.
Without an active integration layer expanding beyond the single flow, every downstream SAP investment that depends on cross-system data flow — analytics, planning, agentic AI — is architecturally constrained, since Integration Suite is the connective tissue that makes those capabilities operational at LoB scale.
Run an integration use-case discovery workshop with Cleveland-Cliffs integration architects and manufacturing IT to identify 3-5 net-new iFlows spanning plant-to-ERP, supplier EDI, and shop-floor event scenarios.
Activate API Management and publish a governed API catalog so Cleveland-Cliffs can expose ERP and manufacturing data to AI agents and partner ecosystems rather than relying on point-to-point integration.
Stand up Trading Partner Management for steel supply chain B2B onboarding and Event Mesh for real-time production and logistics events to convert dormant contracted capability into measurable operational adoption.
Establish an integration center of excellence engagement model with Cleveland-Cliffs to govern iFlow expansion, track capability activation against the $1,386 budget gap, and align integration roadmap to manufacturing and supply chain priorities.
Solution Area

Solution Insights and Actions
Cleveland-Cliffs' Customer Experience architecture rests on a single pillar — Cloud for Customer — and that pillar is operating at 22.3% of planned utilization, meaning the LoB's entire front-office capability for sales account management, opportunity pipeline, and service ticketing is functionally underpowered against the $9,030 YTD plan.
The flat $294–$521 monthly consumption band signals a static, isolated user community rather than a CX platform of record — Cleveland-Cliffs' sales and service teams are largely transacting outside C4C, leaving customer 360, opportunity governance, and case management as dormant capabilities despite contractual deployment.
With no complementary CX products (Sales Cloud, Service Cloud, CDC, or Emarsys) in the adoption footprint, C4C is carrying the full weight of customer engagement alone — there is no architectural depth for marketing orchestration, commerce, or identity, so the LoB cannot evolve beyond basic CRM record-keeping even if C4C adoption improved.
Run a Customer Experience seat and territory audit to map which Cleveland-Cliffs sales and service organizations are transacting outside Cloud for Customer and bring them onto the platform to close the $7,017 YTD and $15,464 ACV gap.
Mandate Cloud for Customer as the system of record for opportunity, account, and service ticket lifecycle so core CX processes stop bypassing the contracted platform.
Initiate a CX architecture review to determine whether Cleveland-Cliffs requires adjacent capabilities (service ticketing depth, marketing, or commerce) or whether the C4C footprint should be right-sized to reflect actual sales/service scope.

Projected Consumed: $280K · Projected Budget: $366K · Projected ACV: $466K
Solution Insights and Actions
Cleveland-Cliffs' Supply Chain Management architecture is single-product thin — IBP is the only active capability, meaning the LoB has a planning engine but no connected execution, sourcing, or logistics fabric to act on its forecasts, which structurally limits supply chain value to siloed S&OP runs.
Within IBP itself, the flat $19,419 monthly consumption signature indicates only one planning module (likely baseline S&OP or demand) is live while inventory optimization, demand sensing, and response & supply planning remain dormant — the LoB is paying for a unified planning platform but operating it as a static forecasting tool.
The degrading budget attainment (64.9% → 63.9%) and projected $86,840 annual shortfall against the $366,478 commitment confirm IBP adoption has plateaued rather than scaled, signaling that planner enablement and use-case rollout stalled after initial go-live and the platform is not absorbing new supply chain scenarios.
Activate IBP's dormant demand sensing and inventory optimization modules with a planner-led workshop to convert the static monthly workload into multi-scenario planning runs and reclaim the projected $86,840 consumption gap.
Audit which IBP planning processes (S&OP, demand, response, inventory) are actually executing behind the recurring $19,419 baseline and build a module-by-module enablement roadmap tied to Cleveland-Cliffs' steel supply chain volatility use cases.
Open an architectural conversation on extending Supply Chain Management beyond planning — specifically into execution (e.g., Digital Manufacturing, Business Network for logistics) — so IBP forecasts can drive operational outcomes rather than terminate at the plan layer.
Cardinal Health Inc.
Life sciences and healthcare
YTD Consumed: $1.0M
YTD Budget: $1.4M
YTD ACV: $1.7M
Projected Consumed: $2.5M
Projected Budget: $3.5M
Projected ACV: $4.2M
EA Insights and Actions
Cardinal Health's enterprise architecture shows a consistent pattern of single-product anchoring per domain — Build alone in Technology Foundation, Commerce alone in CX, Ariba Buying/Invoicing dominant in Spend — meaning each L1 is operating as an island transaction system without the surrounding governance, data, and extension layers, which collectively blocks the integrated source-to-pay-to-serve-to-extend story that justified the multi-domain SAP investment.
The architectural intent of BTP as the connective tissue across Commerce, Ariba, and Concur is unrealized — Build's 68% YTD attainment with no Integration Suite, Joule, or automation activity means the over-consuming Concur stack, dormant Ariba Contracts/SLP, and stalled Commerce storefront have no shared extensibility or integration fabric, so every cross-domain process gap (supplier onboarding to commerce, expense to AP, contract to storefront pricing) is being solved off-platform or in core, accumulating technical debt ahead of any S/4 modernization.
Cardinal Health is structurally mis-sized at the enterprise contract level — uniformly over-consuming T&E (107–136%) and Digital Payments (127%) signal demand the commercial envelope cannot absorb, while Commerce ($953K unconsumed of $982K) and Ariba SLP/Contracts (13–65%) carry roughly $1M+ of stranded value, indicating the FY2026 commitment portfolio reflects an outdated architectural plan rather than current business consumption reality.
The procurement, treasury, and commerce value chains are each broken at their governance hinge — Ariba Sourcing without Contracts/SLP, Commerce without CDC/Service, and Build without a CoE — meaning Cardinal Health as a regulated healthcare distributor cannot evidence supplier qualification, customer data lineage, or extension governance across its SAP estate, which is a direct compliance and audit-readiness exposure given DSCSA and FDA traceability obligations.
Convene a cross-domain EA review with Cardinal Health spanning Spend, CX, and Technology Foundation leaders to rebalance the FY2026 commitment portfolio — redirecting stranded Commerce and Ariba Contracts/SLP value toward the over-consuming T&E and Payments stacks and aligning ACV to actual architectural consumption shape.
Run an integration architecture workshop mapping the source-to-pay-to-settle chain (Ariba Sourcing → Contracts → SLP → Buying/Invoicing → Digital Payments) and the storefront-to-customer chain (Commerce → CDC → Service) onto Integration Suite and Build, exposing the off-platform integrations Cardinal Health is silently accumulating.
Stand up a BTP Center of Excellence for Cardinal Health anchored on Build, Integration Suite, and Joule Studio with a governed backlog of cross-domain extensions (supplier onboarding, expense-to-AP reconciliation, B2B commerce pricing) to convert project-by-project consumption into a compounding platform capability.
Commission a healthcare-distribution compliance architecture assessment that traces DSCSA, supplier qualification, and customer data governance requirements through the current Ariba SLP, Commerce, and Build gaps to quantify regulatory exposure created by the dormant governance layers.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
68.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$242
YTD Budget$354
YTD ACV: $642
Projected Consumed: $580 · Projected Budget: $807 · Projected ACV: $2K
Solution Insights and Actions
Cardinal Health's Technology Foundation has SAP Build as its sole low-code lever, and its sporadic, project-driven consumption pattern indicates the LoB is treating Build as a tactical tool rather than the strategic extensibility layer for BTP — meaning custom apps, workflow automation, and citizen-developer extensions to core SAP processes remain ad-hoc rather than an embedded capability.
With Build at 68% YTD attainment ($241 of $354) and May decelerating to 44% against a $132 ACV target, the architectural intent of a continuous extension fabric on BTP is not being realized — Cardinal Health is likely defaulting to off-platform tools or custom code for process gaps that Build was contracted to absorb.
Build is the only Technology Foundation product showing activity, which signals the broader BTP foundation (integration, automation, AI extensibility via Joule Studio) is under-leveraged — without a Center of Excellence anchoring Build to a reusable app and workflow portfolio, each project resets rather than compounds platform value.
Stand up a Build-anchored extensibility CoE for Cardinal Health that catalogs SAP process gaps (approvals, supplier onboarding, finance close tasks) and converts them into a governed backlog of low-code apps and workflows, shifting Build from project tool to foundational capability.
Run a Joule Studio and Build Process Automation enablement track with Cardinal Health's app dev and automation teams to activate the AI-assisted and workflow dimensions of Build that are currently dormant in the consumption pattern.
Tie Build consumption to named citizen-developer pods with quarterly app delivery commitments to eliminate the swing between 111% and sub-45% monthly attainment and absorb the $466 remaining budget through predictable, outcome-linked usage.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
8.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$29K
YTD Budget$360K
YTD ACV: $659K
Projected Consumed: $69K · Projected Budget: $982K · Projected ACV: $1.6M
Solution Insights and Actions
Cardinal Health's Customer Experience architecture is anchored on a single product — SAP Commerce Cloud — with no surrounding CX stack (no signals from Sales, Service, Marketing, or CDC), meaning the LoB has commerce transaction capability but lacks the customer data, marketing orchestration, and service continuity layers needed to deliver a true unified experience.
The flat $7K monthly Commerce consumption pattern indicates a single storefront or B2B site is live in steady-state while the platform's catalog management, promotions engine, and B2B account capabilities remain architecturally available but operationally dormant — the LoB is paying for an enterprise commerce platform but running it as a point solution.
With only $28,705 consumed against a $982,178 annual commitment (under 3% YTD attainment with 7 months left), the Commerce investment is fundamentally stalled and the LoB will forfeit roughly $953K in contracted value, signaling either a delayed rollout program or an architecture decision that never progressed past pilot.
Without complementary CX products like Emarsys, Sales/Service Cloud, or Customer Data Platform alongside Commerce, Cardinal Health cannot close the loop between storefront behavior, customer profile, and downstream engagement — limiting Commerce to an order-capture tool rather than a growth engine.
