Launch a Commerce Cloud adoption review with Cardinal Health's digital and B2B commerce owners to map which catalogs, storefronts, and B2B account sites were contracted but never launched, and build an activation roadmap against the $953K unconsumed value.
Position a CX architecture assessment that evaluates the gap between Commerce Cloud and the missing customer data, marketing, and service layers required for the LoB to realize unified commerce outcomes rather than isolated transactions.
Run a technical enablement workshop on Commerce Cloud headless APIs, promotion engine, and B2B account management to expand the active workload footprint beyond the single low-volume storefront driving current consumption.
Engage the Cardinal Health sponsor to confirm whether the Commerce deployment is delayed, descoped, or architecturally blocked, and align on whether the FY2026 commitment reflects current business intent or needs renegotiation.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
97.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$1.0M
YTD Budget$1.0M
YTD ACV: $1.1M
Projected Consumed: $2.4M · Projected Budget: $2.5M · Projected ACV: $2.6M
Solution Insights and Actions
The procure-to-pay spine is architecturally fragmented at Cardinal Health — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is over-consuming at 108% while Ariba Contracts (65% attainment) and Ariba SLP (13% attainment) are dormant, meaning purchases are flowing through the platform without contract governance or qualified supplier controls, which structurally defeats the savings and compliance purpose of the Ariba suite.
The Travel and Expense stack is uniformly over-consuming across every component (Expense 109%, ExpenseIt 123%, Analytics 123%, Reporting 128%, Expense Pay 107%, Direct Travel 123%, Support Desk 136%, Preferred Success 124%) — this is not adoption success but a structurally under-sized commercial envelope where the entire T&E architecture will hit ACV ceilings and throttle expense processing before fiscal year end.
The sourcing-to-contract-to-supplier value chain is broken — Ariba Sourcing runs in event-driven bursts (52% to 127% swings) while Contracts and SLP sit dormant, so negotiated sourcing outcomes are not being codified into contracts or tied to qualified suppliers, leaving savings unrealized and supplier risk ungoverned.
Treasury integration is the smallest but healthiest signal in the estate — SAP Digital Payments crossed an inflection at 127% attainment with sustained April–May growth, indicating payment centralization is taking hold and is ready to be tied back to the over-consuming AP flow in Ariba Buying and Invoicing to complete the source-to-pay-to-settle architecture.
Activate Ariba Contracts and Ariba SLP as the governance layer beneath Ariba Buying and Invoicing by migrating Cardinal Health's active supplier base into SLP qualification and routing contract authoring/renewals through Ariba Contracts to close the $11,299 and $13,508 YTD gaps and enforce contracted-spend compliance.
Initiate a consolidated T&E true-up across Concur Expense, ExpenseIt, Analytics, Reporting, Expense Pay, Direct Travel, User Support Desk, and Preferred Success Concur to right-size ACV against the combined projected overrun and prevent ceiling-driven throttling of expense processing.
Establish a recurring sourcing event cadence in Ariba Sourcing with mandatory hand-off into Ariba Contracts for award codification, converting episodic RFx usage into a continuous sourcing-to-contract pipeline that feeds supplier records in SLP.
Launch a Concur User Support Desk ticket-deflection program leveraging Preferred Success Concur advisory capacity to convert the 136% support consumption into self-sufficiency through knowledge base, in-app guidance, and admin enablement.
Cargill, Incorporated
Agribusiness
YTD Consumed: $4.1M
YTD Budget: $4.1M
YTD ACV: $4.0M
Projected Consumed: $9.7M
Projected Budget: $9.8M
Projected ACV: $9.5M
EA Insights and Actions
BTP is dormant or under-activated across Finance ($0 of $230 EA), Customer Experience, and Supply Chain while Joule Studio and BDC Public are simultaneously overrunning in BAIP — Cargill is consuming AI and data services aggressively in one corner of the estate while leaving the extension, integration, and analytics fabric that would connect Ariba, Concur, Commerce, and IBP completely unbuilt, which structurally prevents the closed-loop, AI-augmented enterprise the platform investments imply.
Every L1 domain except Technology Foundation is in overrun (BAIP Data & AI ~$38.5K, Commerce ~$14.3K, Concur/Oversight ~$162K, IBP ~$297K) while procurement source-to-contract sits at 33–87% and SAP Build declines to 70% — Cargill is paying premium for the systems of record that capture transactions but under-consuming the systems of governance and extension that convert those transactions into managed business outcomes.
Cargill's CX is a single-pillar Commerce storefront with no Customer Data, Marketing, or Service, and IBP plans have no architecturally visible execution or analytics downstream — for a commodity-driven B2B business, this means neither the demand signal from buyers nor the supply plan from planners closes the loop into a unified customer or operational view, capping the ROI on both the CX and SCM investments.
Ariba Contracts at 33% attainment while Buying & Invoicing and Concur run hot reveals a procurement architecture where transactions execute without contractual governance upstream, meaning negotiated savings, compliance terms, and supplier performance are not enforced at the point of spend — a material control gap for a regulated, global agribusiness.
Convene a cross-domain BTP activation review that targets the dormant Finance, CX, and SCM entitlements with named first workloads (Ariba–S/4 integration, Commerce-to-customer-data unification, IBP-to-execution feedback) and rebalances unused Technology Foundation capacity to cover the Joule Studio overrun.
Initiate a consolidated enterprise commercial true-up across the ~$512K combined overrun in BAIP Data & AI, Commerce, Concur/Oversight, and IBP, paired with a reallocation from the underperforming Ariba source-to-contract and SAP Build entitlements to reset the EA envelope against actual adoption shape.
Run an end-to-end integration architecture workshop tracing Commerce → Customer Data → IBP → Ariba → Concur → BAIP to surface every broken handoff and define the BTP-hosted integration and data services required to make the suite operate as one enterprise rather than five.
Stand up an enterprise FinOps and consumption governance cadence with Cargill that establishes subaccount budgets, attainment alerts, and quarterly architecture reviews so domains scale as managed capabilities instead of oscillating between overrun and administrative throttling.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
135.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$63K
YTD Budget$47K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $152K · Projected Budget: $114K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
Cargill's BAIP Data & AI footprint on Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is running hot across both BDC Public and Database Cloud subaccounts, indicating the LoB has moved past experimentation into production-grade data and AI workloads — the architectural intent of the platform is being realized, but without the commercial envelope to sustain it.
The BDC Public subaccount at 139% attainment ($58,634 vs $42,094) is the dominant overrun and likely hosts the data foundation and AI services powering analytics use cases, meaning any hard-stop here would cascade into downstream BAIP consumption patterns rather than a single isolated workload.
Database Cloud's shift from a Jan–Feb shortfall to three consecutive months of 102–119% attainment shows persistent data services have crossed into steady-state operation, which architecturally signals the LoB now depends on this tier for live workloads rather than development sandboxes.
With a combined projected overage approaching $38.5K against a remaining budget of roughly $44.6K, Cargill is on a trajectory to exhaust the Data & AI platform contract roughly five months early, putting the entire BAIP capability stack at risk of a mid-cycle throttling event.
Run a joint workload review across the BDC Public and Database Cloud subaccounts to attribute the overage to specific Data & AI services and distinguish unmanaged sprawl from intentional production scale-out.
Initiate a commercial true-up or capacity expansion conversation on LPR868 sized to the combined ~$38.5K projected shortfall to protect continuity of the BAIP Data & AI capabilities Cargill is now operationally dependent on.
Establish consumption governance with Cargill — subaccount-level budgets, alerting thresholds, and an FinOps cadence — so the Data & AI platform scales as a managed capability rather than a reactive overrun.
Validate with Cargill's architecture team whether the BDC Public growth reflects planned AI/ML expansion and, if so, position a right-sized multi-year commitment that aligns commercial terms with the LoB's adoption trajectory.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
107.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$87K
YTD Budget$81K
YTD ACV: $6K
Projected Consumed: $209K · Projected Budget: $195K · Projected ACV: $15K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation is architecturally bifurcated — Integration Suite is the dominant, production-grade workload pacing at 107% of YTD budget while Joule Studio is overrunning at 118% and the generic BTP tenant sits at zero, indicating Cargill has operationalized integration as the backbone but is consuming AI/agent capabilities faster than governed and has not activated the broader BTP extensibility surface.
Joule Studio's $9,601 projected overage signals that Cargill is scaling generative AI and agent workloads on BTP ahead of commercial capacity, which puts the LoB's ability to continue building Joule-based experiences at risk of a hard stop unless the EA is rebalanced from the dormant Technology Foundation pool.
SAP Build is architecturally the citizen-development and extension layer that should ride on top of Integration Suite's APIs, but its three-month consumption decline to 70% attainment shows the low-code fabric is disengaging just as integration capacity matures — meaning Cargill is building pipes without building the apps and workflows that monetize them.
The Integration Suite attainment curve declining from 125% to 90% alongside a fully dormant generic BTP entitlement indicates the initial wave of integrations has stabilized with no second-wave use cases queued, leaving the LoB exposed to a flat-lined platform with no expansion roadmap into extensions, data, or AI services.
Rebalance the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement by shifting unused capacity from the dormant Technology Foundation bucket to cover the Joule Studio overage, and open a commercial true-up conversation on the $9,601 AI workload gap.
Run a joint workload review on Integration Suite to classify the 125%-to-90% decline as either optimization or stalled onboarding, and pair it with a use-case pipeline tied to Build and Joule to convert integration assets into business-facing applications.
Launch a SAP Build reactivation workshop with Cargill's app dev and process automation owners to land two to three low-code extensions on top of existing Integration Suite APIs and reverse the citizen-development disengagement.
Stand up an activation plan for the unallocated Technology Foundation BTP entitlement targeting specific services (e.g., data, AI, extension runtimes) so the $634 annual budget translates into a concrete capability footprint rather than shelfware.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
106.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$128K
YTD Budget$121K
YTD ACV: $131K
Projected Consumed: $308K · Projected Budget: $294K · Projected ACV: $314K
Solution Insights and Actions
Cargill's Customer Experience architecture is single-pillar — only SAP Commerce Cloud is active, meaning the storefront and catalog engine is live but the surrounding CX fabric (Customer Data, Marketing, Sales, Service) is absent, leaving Cargill with transactional commerce but no unified customer profile, journey orchestration, or post-sale service continuity.
Commerce Cloud is operationally embedded but architecturally isolated — the $14,297 (4.9%) projected budget overrun confirms it is a load-bearing system, yet without adjacent CX products the 'seamless unified commerce experience' and 'AI innovation' capabilities of the platform are structurally unreachable for Cargill's B2B buyers.
The April–May $1,084 consumption dip against an otherwise overrun trajectory signals a workload anomaly rather than a demand decline, indicating storefront/catalog utilization is volatile and capacity planning is reactive rather than governed by a forecasted commerce roadmap.
Reconcile the $14,297 projected Commerce Cloud overrun with Cargill by right-sizing storefront and catalog workloads and aligning contracted ACV to actual B2B transaction volume patterns.
Diagnose the April consumption drop by tracing it to a specific catalog publish, campaign, or integration event to determine whether it reflects a tuning opportunity or a degradation in buyer engagement.
Open an architectural conversation with Cargill on extending the CX footprint with SAP Emarsys, Sales Cloud, or Service Cloud so Commerce Cloud's catalog and order data can drive personalization, lead conversion, and post-order service rather than terminating at checkout.
Establish a unified customer data foundation discussion to address the absence of identity and consent management underneath Commerce Cloud, which currently blocks the AI and personalization capabilities Cargill is already paying for.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
90.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$2.0M
YTD Budget$2.2M
YTD ACV: $2.2M
Projected Consumed: $4.8M · Projected Budget: $5.3M · Projected ACV: $5.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
Cargill's Procurement suite is architecturally complete but operationally fragmented — Buying & Invoicing, Catalog, Sourcing, Contracts, and SIPM are all contracted yet each runs 33–87% of plan, meaning the source-to-pay chain executes transactions without the upstream sourcing discipline, contract governance, or supplier performance feedback that justify the integrated investment.
The Travel & Expense stack is the inverse problem — Concur Expense, ExpenseIt, Analytics, Company Bill Statement, and Oversight Detect are all running 124–147% of plan, confirming T&E is the most deeply embedded Finance capability at Cargill but is structurally under-sized, putting the LoB at risk of ACV breach and service disruption mid-year.
Contract lifecycle management is the weakest link in the procurement architecture — Ariba Contracts at 33% May attainment and a projected 47% annual landing means sourcing events in Ariba Sourcing are not flowing into a governed contract repository, so negotiated savings and compliance clauses are not being enforced downstream in Buying & Invoicing.
BTP is entirely dormant with $0 consumed against the $230 Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement, leaving Cargill without the extension, integration, and analytics fabric needed to connect Ariba, Concur, and Oversight — a foundational gap that limits cross-product automation and data unification for Finance and Spend Management.
Right-size the Concur and Oversight Detect contracts through a consolidated true-up covering the projected ~$162K combined overrun across Expense, ExpenseIt, Analytics, Company Bill Statement, and Oversight Detect to prevent ACV exhaustion in the back half of the year.
Launch a coordinated Source-to-Contract recovery program that links Ariba Sourcing event expansion, Ariba Contracts repository migration, and Catalog-enabled supplier onboarding to restore the integrated procurement flow and close the combined ~$580K projected procurement shortfall.
Trigger a BTP activation workshop to identify the first production workload — prioritizing Ariba-to-S/4 integration or Concur analytics extensions — and convert the $0 Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement baseline into measurable consumption tied to named application owners.
Run a Buying & Invoicing transaction audit at Cargill to identify off-platform PO and invoice flows and route them through Ariba, directly attacking the $209K YTD gap and re-anchoring AP automation as the system of record.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
107.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$1.8M
YTD Budget$1.7M
YTD ACV: $1.7M
Projected Consumed: $4.3M · Projected Budget: $4.0M · Projected ACV: $4.0M
Solution Insights and Actions
IBP is the sole contracted pillar of Cargill's Supply Chain planning architecture, and its Q1 overrun followed by forced normalization signals that demand sensing, S&OP, and supply response are running hot against a ceiling that was not sized for the LoB's actual planning intensity — the risk is not cost, it is that planners will self-throttle collaborative S&OP cycles to stay within budget, degrading the very forecasting and multilevel supply planning capability the platform was bought to deliver.
The April–May snap to exactly 100% attainment is architecturally suspicious — it reflects administrative governance rather than organic workload, meaning IBP consumption is now being managed to the contract rather than to the business, which masks whether AI-powered demand prediction and response planning are being used at the intensity Cargill's commodity-driven supply chain requires.
With $1.78M YTD consumed against a $1.65M YTD budget and a projected $297K annual overrun, the LoB is on a trajectory where IBP capacity becomes the binding constraint on planning maturity — every incremental S&OP scenario, demand sensing run, or supply response simulation now competes for a fixed envelope, blocking the collaborative planning outcome IBP was architected to enable.
IBP operates here without visible adjacent Supply Chain execution or analytics signals in the LoB portfolio, meaning plans generated in IBP have no architecturally confirmed downstream path into execution or performance feedback — the LoB is investing in forward-looking planning intelligence without a closed loop back to actuals, which limits the ROI of the planning platform itself.
Run a process-level decomposition of IBP consumption across demand, S&OP, supply, and response modules to determine whether the Q1 burn reflects legitimate planning intensity or uncontrolled scenario proliferation, and use that to justify either a capacity uplift or a governance model.
Validate whether the April–May normalization is the result of planner throttling or genuine optimization by reviewing planning run frequency and user activity, because suppressed usage of demand sensing and S&OP directly erodes Cargill's supply responsiveness.
Open a capacity expansion conversation framed around the $297K projected overrun and Cargill's commodity volatility exposure, positioning the uplift as protection of S&OP cadence rather than a cost increase.
Assess the integration architecture between IBP and Cargill's execution and analytics layers to ensure planning outputs are consumed downstream, since IBP value collapses if forecasts and supply plans do not flow into operational decisions.
Conagra Brands, Inc.
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $533K
YTD Budget: $548K
YTD ACV: $481K
Projected Consumed: $1.3M
Projected Budget: $1.5M
Projected ACV: $1.2M
EA Insights and Actions
Conagra's entire SAP estate is converging on a single load-bearing dependency — Integration Suite at 133%+ and BDC Public BTP at 145%+ are absorbing all strategic workloads while the Technology Foundation tenant sits at 0%, Database Cloud BTP sits sub-32%, and the Finance Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement sits at $0, meaning every cross-domain story (Ariba-to-S/4, PaPM-to-SAC, Joule extensions) is running on two over-consumed contracts with no activated platform substrate beneath them, putting the entire integrated enterprise at risk of a mid-year consumption freeze.
Conagra has built data and process production capacity but no governed consumption surface — BDC Public BTP and Integration Suite are over-consuming while SAC BI ($101/mo, 41% YTD), SAC Planning (declining $13.2K→$12.4K), and Ariba Spend Analysis (collapsed to $0) are all decaying simultaneously, breaking the analyze-plan-act loop that a CPG operator needs for margin defense, trade promotion optimization, and supplier rationalization.
The S2C and FP&A spines are structurally complete on paper but operationally inverted — Sourcing executes at 94%, PaPM spiked to 357%, and SSO runs at 136%, yet the upstream governance layers (Supplier Risk stuck at 70%, SLP sliding 99%→90%, Spend Analysis at $0, SAC Planning declining) are eroding in parallel, meaning Conagra is scaling transaction throughput and identity access faster than the risk, planning, and analytics controls that should govern them.
Joule Studio's 10x ramp to $2,512/mo demonstrates Conagra is committing to AI extensibility, but with the Technology Foundation tenant dormant and BTP extension/runtime services unactivated across Finance and Data domains, every Joule agent and AI workload is being built without the platform substrate needed to scale them as enterprise capabilities rather than isolated experiments.
Convene a single cross-domain BTP architecture review covering LPR868 (Integration Suite + Joule), the BDC Public tenant, Database Cloud, and the Finance Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement to consolidate true-up, rebalance ~$30K+ of dormant entitlement against ~$85K+ of projected overage, and prevent a hard stop on production integrations.
Stand up an analytics reactivation workshop that re-anchors SAC BI, SAC Planning, and Ariba Spend Analysis to live outputs from BDC Public, PaPM, and Integration Suite — restoring the data-to-decision loop and protecting the $169K planning renewal and the $3,536 SAC BI envelope.
Commission a clean-core extensibility reference architecture that formalizes Joule Studio as the AI extension pattern, lands first workloads on the dormant Technology Foundation and Database Cloud tenants, and gives Finance/Procurement a governed BTP path for Ariba–S/4 integration and SSO-fronted custom apps.
Launch a supplier governance and risk uplift play that expands Supplier Risk coverage, restores SLP scorecard cadence, and ties both back to a reactivated Spend Analysis category pipeline — closing the S2C governance gap before SLP and Supplier Risk become renewal-cycle shelfware.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
162.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$110K
YTD Budget$68K
YTD ACV: $2K
Projected Consumed: $264K · Projected Budget: $234K · Projected ACV: $5K
Solution Insights and Actions
Conagra's Data & AI footprint is architecturally bifurcated — the BDC Public BTP tenant is running 145%+ hot on a $212K budget while the Database Cloud BTP tenant sits flat at sub-32% attainment, meaning one platform instance is absorbing all the strategic data and AI workloads while the other is stranded as a narrow, single-purpose deployment.
The analytics layer is deployed but disconnected from the data platform driving the overconsumption — SAC BI is trending down to $101/month (41% YTD attainment) while BDC Public BTP services are being consumed 2x over plan, indicating Conagra is building and expanding data pipelines and AI services without surfacing the outputs through SAP's intended visualization and decision layer.
SAC BI's declining run rate signals the planning and AI-driven decision capabilities (Joule, unified financial/operational planning) are dormant, leaving the LoB with a data platform that generates insight but no governed business consumption surface — projected $1,433 against $3,536 budget means roughly one-third of the analytics contract is at risk of going unused.
The Database Cloud BTP entitlement is functioning as a fixed-workload hosting contract rather than an enterprise data platform — the tight $558–$649 monthly band against a $17,804 budget projects ~$10,560 of unused capacity, meaning Conagra is paying for a data & AI expansion runway it has not architecturally activated.
Run a joint BTP consumption review across both Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreements to map which BDC Public services are driving the overage and determine whether Database Cloud workloads should be consolidated or repositioned to rebalance the two contracts.
Restructure the BDC Public Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement commercial envelope to reflect the sustained 145%+ attainment, and pair the expansion with governance on which Data & AI services are strategic vs uncontrolled consumption.
Anchor a SAC BI adoption play on a finance or supply chain use case that consumes data from the active BDC Public workloads, closing the architectural gap between data production and business consumption while lifting attainment toward the $295/month budget pace.
Launch a Database Cloud BTP service discovery workshop to identify 2–3 net-new data or integration workloads that can absorb the $1,366/month budget gap and convert the entitlement from a single-workload host into a broader data platform.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
111.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$65K
YTD Budget$58K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $156K · Projected Budget: $143K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
Conagra's BTP footprint is bifurcated into a runaway Integration Suite and a dormant general-purpose tenant — Integration Suite is pacing to 133% of annual budget while the Technology Foundation entitlement sits at 0% consumption, signaling that integration is carrying the entire platform value while extension, data, and runtime services that should surround it remain unbuilt.
Joule Studio's 10x consumption ramp from $227 to $2,512 confirms the AI extensibility layer has moved from sandbox to active build, but with YTD attainment at only 27% the LoB is architecturally positioned to operationalize Joule agents yet under-utilizing the runtime needed to scale them across business units.
Integration Suite's sustained 150%+ monthly over-attainment and $49,743 cumulative overrun indicate the platform has become the de facto backbone for Conagra's process integration, but without a true-up the LoB faces a mid-year hard stop that will halt every downstream business process dependent on BTP-mediated flows.
The combination of an over-consumed Integration Suite, an emerging Joule Studio practice, and an unactivated general BTP tenant reveals a foundation that is load-bearing in one dimension and structurally hollow in others — Conagra is integrating and experimenting with AI but has no platform substrate for the extensions, data services, or custom apps those investments will eventually require.
Initiate a true-up on LPR868 sized to the $35,950 projected Integration Suite overage and rebalance unconsumed Technology Foundation entitlement into the same contract envelope to prevent a consumption freeze on production integrations.
Run a workload discovery on the dormant Technology Foundation tenant to land first use cases in extension suite, data services, or application runtime that complement the active Integration Suite and Joule Studio footprints.
Formalize the Joule Studio build pattern into a reference architecture and extend it to adjacent Conagra business units to sustain the $2,500+/month trajectory and convert experimentation into enterprise AI capability.
Conduct a service-level decomposition of Integration Suite consumption to confirm whether the 150%+ attainment reflects strategic process expansion or untuned interfaces, and use the findings to right-size the contract against actual architectural demand.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
84.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$358K
YTD Budget$422K
YTD ACV: $479K
Projected Consumed: $860K · Projected Budget: $1.1M · Projected ACV: $1.1M
Solution Insights and Actions
Conagra's source-to-contract spine is architecturally intact but operationally fragmenting — Ariba Sourcing runs steady-state at ~94% (never breaching ACV), SLP attainment is sliding three months straight (99%→90%), and Supplier Risk is stuck at ~70% — meaning sourcing events are still executing but the upstream supplier qualification, performance, and risk signals that should govern those events are weakening, eroding the integrated S2C value case.
The Procurement analytics loop is broken — Ariba Spend Analysis collapsed to $0 in April and May after a three-month decline, so Conagra is now running sourcing, SLP, and Supplier Risk without the spend visibility layer that identifies categories to source, suppliers to rationalize, and risk exposure to monitor, leaving the entire Ariba suite operating blind to consolidated spend patterns.
FP&A's decision architecture is inverted — Profitability and Performance Management spiked to 357% in May (a batch/go-live signature, not steady modeling) while SAP Analytics Cloud Planning has declined every month from $13.2K to $12.4K, meaning Conagra is generating granular profitability outputs with no corresponding uplift in planning consumption to act on them, breaking the model-to-plan feedback loop.
The platform extension layer is dormant while identity consumption surges — Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement sits at $0 through five months while Single Sign-on is tracking 6% over ACV at 136% May attainment, indicating Conagra is scaling user access to existing SAP services but has not activated the BTP foundation needed to extend, integrate, or build around them.
Reactivate Ariba Spend Analysis as the analytical input to Sourcing, SLP, and Supplier Risk by restoring category spend dashboards and tying them to a quarterly sourcing pipeline and supplier rationalization review with Conagra procurement leadership.
Stand up a BTP activation workshop on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement anchored to a concrete Finance or Procurement extension use case (e.g., Ariba-to-S/4HANA integration, SAC custom data models, or SSO-governed custom apps) to convert the $2,581 dormant ceiling into live platform consumption.
Run a joint PaPM–SAC Planning operating model review to convert the May profitability spike into a recurring allocation cadence whose outputs feed driver-based forecasts in SAC Planning, reversing the SAC consumption decline and protecting the $169K planning renewal case.
Open a Supplier Risk and SLP coverage expansion play with Conagra — broadening monitored supplier segments, ESG/financial risk categories, and performance scorecard cycles — to lift Supplier Risk off its ~70% ceiling and reverse the SLP three-month attainment slide before the gaps compound into renewal-cycle shelfware.
Coop Regions Of Organic Producer
Agribusiness
YTD Consumed: $119K
YTD Budget: $127K
YTD ACV: $135K
Projected Consumed: $286K
Projected Budget: $306K
Projected ACV: $325K
EA Insights and Actions
Coop Regions has built a textbook modern data architecture on paper — Datasphere as the semantic layer, SAC for BI and Planning, IBP for supply chain planning — but each domain is operating as an island with no evidence of Datasphere feeding SAC Planning or IBP outputs flowing into financial plans, meaning the cooperative is paying for an integrated intelligent-enterprise stack while running it as four disconnected point solutions, blocking the unified organic supply chain traceability and driver-based FP&A story that justifies the combined investment.
Integration Suite is over-consuming at 107% while every capability it was architected to connect — Joule Studio ($0), SAP Build ($14/mo ceiling), SAC BI (flat $150-230/mo), and FSM operational sources into SAC Planning — is dormant or narrow, revealing that the integration backbone is load-bearing for a single legacy use case rather than serving as the connective tissue for AI, extensibility, analytics, and planning, which leaves the cooperative's clean-core and AI-ready ambitions architecturally unsupported.
The contract shape is structurally misaligned with consumption reality across the enterprise — IBP at 100% ($56.5K YTD of $135.6K), Integration Suite over-ceiling, SAC Planning recovering to 111%, but Datasphere eroded to 69.2%, SAC BI dormant against $6,915 annual, and Joule Studio at $0 of $174 — meaning the cooperative is heading into renewal with ~$10K+ of shelfware on the intelligence and extensibility layers while the integration layer is under-sized, a posture that will lock in the wrong architecture for an organic producer needing AI-driven yield, perishability, and traceability outcomes.
IBP and SAC Planning are both live planning brains operating without their execution and operational-data feeds — IBP has no visible downstream supply execution or visibility layer and SAC Planning has no connected spend, procurement, or AP signals — so the cooperative is generating plans in isolation from actuals, undermining the closed-loop planning-to-execution outcome that is the defining value proposition of an organic producer cooperative managing yield volatility and member settlements.
Convene a cross-domain enterprise architecture review to redesign the Datasphere → SAC BI/Planning → IBP data and planning chain as a single integrated flow anchored on organic supply chain traceability and driver-based FP&A, and use the output to rebalance CPEA allocation away from dormant Joule Studio and underused SAC BI toward the over-running Integration Suite ahead of renewal.
Stand up a closed-loop planning workshop that connects IBP supply and demand signals into SAC Planning financial models and back into operational spend and procurement sources, eliminating the planning-in-isolation pattern visible across both Finance and Supply Chain domains.
Launch a Joule Studio and SAP Build activation initiative scoped to a single organic producer use case (e.g., supplier traceability agent or yield variance workflow) that consumes existing Integration Suite APIs and Datasphere models, converting three dormant or ceiling-bound investments into one connected AI-and-extensibility capability.
Run a portfolio-level contract reshape exercise quantifying the ~$10K+ YTD shelfware on Datasphere, SAC BI, and Joule Studio against the over-ceiling Integration Suite and CPEA-Integration positions to align the renewal commitment shape with the cooperative's actual operating pattern and stated AI ambitions.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
70.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$18K
YTD Budget$26K
YTD ACV: $33K
Projected Consumed: $44K · Projected Budget: $64K · Projected ACV: $80K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation is architecturally intact but commercially eroding — Datasphere is provisioned as the semantic data layer yet attainment has slipped from 81.7% to 69.2%, meaning the LoB's AI-ready, business-context-preserving fabric is contracting just as it should be scaling to feed downstream analytics and AI workloads.
The semantic layer is feeding a starved consumption tier — Datasphere is running at roughly 5x the spend of SAC BI ($17,340 vs $984 YTD), indicating Coop Regions is investing in modeled, trusted data but failing to operationalize it into dashboards, planning, or decision workflows where business users realize value.
SAC BI is deployed but dormant as a decision platform — flat $150–$230 monthly consumption against a $6,915 annual budget shows the unified analytics and planning capability is technically live but not embedded in recurring finance, supply chain, or organic producer reporting cadences, leaving the LoB with a reporting tool rather than a planning backbone.
The Datasphere-to-SAC architectural pairing is underutilized for the organic supply chain use case — with a combined ~$10.5K YTD gap and ~27% of Datasphere value tracking unused, the LoB is paying for an integrated data-and-analytics stack while operating it as disconnected components, blocking supplier analytics and organic traceability outcomes.
Run a joint Datasphere + SAC BI use-case workshop anchored on organic supply chain and supplier analytics to convert the modeled data layer into consumed dashboards and planning scenarios.
Launch a Datasphere enablement sprint covering space provisioning, replication flows, and analytic model creation to reverse the month-over-month attainment decline and absorb the $5,934 cumulative gap.
Execute a SAC BI named-user audit to reclaim inactive licenses and re-onboard business users onto Datasphere-backed models, breaking the flat consumption ceiling.
Activate Joule-powered analytics and planning scenarios on SAC BI to extend usage beyond static reporting into AI-assisted decisioning grounded in the existing Datasphere semantic layer.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
104.3%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$26K
YTD Budget$25K
YTD ACV: $21K
Projected Consumed: $62K · Projected Budget: $60K · Projected ACV: $50K
Solution Insights and Actions
The integration backbone is the only fully active layer of the Technology Foundation — SAP Integration Suite is running at 107% YTD with CPEA-Integration also over-ceiling at 112%, meaning Coop Regions is genuinely landing API and iFlow workloads to connect core processes, but the May deceleration on Integration Suite signals this single load-bearing capability may be plateauing on one narrow use case rather than scaling.
The extensibility layer is architecturally orphaned — SAP Build sits at a flat $14/month consumption ceiling indicating a single fixed team, while the CPEA-Joule Studio contract is at $0 across five months, so the LoB has integration pipes but no citizen-developer or AI-agent extension surface consuming them, defeating the purpose of pairing Build and Joule Studio with Integration Suite.
The AI-ready foundation contracted under Joule Studio CPEA is entirely dormant — zero consumption against a $174 annual plan means none of the agent, Joule, or AI-API capabilities Integration Suite is designed to feed are actually being stood up, leaving Coop Regions paying for integration optimized for AI consumption with no AI consumer attached.
Capacity sizing across the foundation is misaligned with reality — Integration Suite and its CPEA are structurally over-ceiling while Joule Studio CPEA and Build are structurally under-consumed, indicating the LoB bought a balanced platform but is operating it as an integration-only stack heading into renewal with the wrong shape of commitment.
Convene a Technology Foundation architecture review with Coop Regions to rebalance CPEA allocation away from the dormant Joule Studio entitlement toward the over-running Integration Suite footprint ahead of renewal.
Activate the Joule Studio CPEA by scoping a first AI-agent or Joule extension use case that consumes existing Integration Suite APIs, turning the dormant contract into a connected capability rather than shelfware.
Break the SAP Build $14/month ceiling by onboarding a second wave of business users onto a low-code workflow that extends an already-integrated process, converting Build from a one-team tool into the extensibility layer for the Integration Suite investment.
Investigate the May Integration Suite dip with the integration lead to confirm whether a key iFlow was paused or decommissioned, and expand to a second scenario such as supplier B2B/EDI to make adoption resilient beyond a single workload.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
95.3%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$18K
YTD Budget$19K
YTD ACV: $25K
Projected Consumed: $44K · Projected Budget: $46K · Projected ACV: $59K
Solution Insights and Actions
Finance & Spend Management adoption at Coop Regions Of Organic Producer is architecturally narrow — SAP Analytics Cloud Planning is the only active capability, meaning the LoB has unified planning analytics but no connected spend, procurement, or financial close products to feed it with operational data, limiting plans to financial inputs rather than driver-based cross-process planning.
SAC Planning has just crossed into healthy territory at 111% May attainment after four months below plan, confirming the platform is finally moving from deployment to active planner usage — but the $6,471 YTD ACV gap and projected $2,519 annual shortfall show the April capacity expansion outpaced onboarding, so entitlements exist without planners to consume them.
Because SAC Planning operates without companion FSM products (e.g., spend, AP, treasury signals), the 'unify financial and operational planning' capability is architecturally underused — the LoB is doing financial planning in isolation rather than the integrated FP&A the product is designed to deliver.
Scale the May use case (likely quarterly forecast or workforce plan) to additional cost centers and planning models in SAC Planning to convert the inflection into sustained ≥100% attainment and absorb the expanded April entitlement.
Run a targeted enablement sprint for the newly entitled SAC Planning user cohort focused on driver-based planning and data actions to close the $2,098 May ACV gap created by the capacity step-up.
Open a discovery on connecting SAC Planning to operational spend and procurement data sources so the planning models reflect real commitments and actuals, unlocking the unified financial-and-operational planning the architecture promises.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
100.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$56K
YTD Budget$56K
YTD ACV: $56K
Projected Consumed: $136K · Projected Budget: $136K · Projected ACV: $136K
Solution Insights and Actions
Coop Regions Of Organic Producer has operationalized IBP as its supply chain planning backbone, with five consecutive months of perfect budget attainment ($11,299/month, $56,495 YTD of $135,593 annual) signaling that demand and supply planning cycles are embedded in monthly rhythm rather than experimental — the LoB is realizing the core S&OP value the platform was architected to deliver.
However, the flat, predictable burn pattern suggests the LoB is exercising a narrow slice of IBP's architectural surface — likely core S&OP and forecasting — while adjacent modules such as demand sensing, inventory optimization, and response/supply planning appear dormant, leaving AI-driven demand prediction and multilevel supply orchestration as untapped capabilities for an organic producer cooperative where shelf-life and yield volatility make these features especially high-value.
With IBP as the only Supply Chain product showing adoption signal, the cooperative has a planning brain but no visible execution or visibility layer connected to it in this LoB's footprint, meaning forward-looking plans risk being disconnected from downstream supply execution and inventory actualization.
Document the specific planning use cases driving the steady $11,299/month consumption and package them into a value realization narrative to anchor renewal and justify expansion beyond the current $135,593 ceiling.
Run a discovery workshop with the planning team on demand sensing and inventory optimization modules, framed around organic produce volatility (seasonality, perishability, yield variance) to convert baseline adoption into an uplift opportunity.
Map IBP's planning outputs to the cooperative's supply execution and visibility tooling to ensure planned demand and supply signals are operationalized downstream rather than stopping at the plan.
Corteva Agriscience LLC
Agribusiness
YTD Consumed: $3.0M
YTD Budget: $3.0M
YTD ACV: $3.7M
Projected Consumed: $7.3M
Projected Budget: $7.2M
Projected ACV: $9.0M
EA Insights and Actions
BTP is dormant or under-contracted across every L1 domain — Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance all show $0–minimal BTP Enterprise Agreement activation — meaning Corteva has no governed platform plane to host extensions, AI services, or cross-domain integrations, forcing customizations into the core and blocking the clean-core S/4HANA trajectory the contract was designed to enable.
Corteva's architecture is inverted across the stack — heavy raw consumption layers (Database Cloud at 2.2x–2.5x, IBP at 158% of plan, Integration Suite at 1.4–7x) are running hot while the decision and experience layers above them (SAC BI at 0.2%, SAC Planning regressing to 55%, SAP Build at 12%, Datasphere flat at 2%) are dormant, meaning Corteva is paying enterprise-scale infrastructure costs without converting that compute into the unified planning, analytics, and citizen-developer outcomes that justify the investment.
The integration backbone is industrializing system-to-system connectivity (Integration Suite at 143% in May) but the consumer layers it should feed — SAC BI, SAP Build apps, Ariba Spend Analysis, Treasury cash-to-bank, IBP-to-execution flows — are all fragmented or dormant, meaning Corteva is building pipes without endpoints and breaking the closed-loop stories (plan-to-execute, source-to-pay, record-to-report) that depend on integrated downstream consumption.
Across Procurement (Ariba Spend Analysis at $0, Catalog collapsed), Treasury (four disconnected utilities), and Supply Chain (IBP isolated from execution and Business Network), Corteva owns end-to-end suites but operates them as point solutions, blocking the integrated agribusiness operating model — seed-to-shelf planning, supplier-to-payment compliance, demand-to-cash orchestration — that an agriscience leader needs to manage commodity volatility and seasonal working capital.
Convene a cross-domain BTP activation review that consolidates the three dormant Enterprise Agreements (Data & AI, Technology Foundation Other, Finance) into a single platform roadmap with named first workloads for extensions, AI services, and integration governance before overage on CPEA-aligned services compounds.
Commission an enterprise integration coherence workshop that maps Integration Suite iFlows to their intended downstream consumers — SAC BI, SAP Build apps, Ariba catalog/spend, Treasury payment factory, IBP-to-S/4 execution — and prioritizes closing the broken closed-loop chains rather than adding new pipes.
Trigger an enterprise contract re-baselining across IBP (LPR318), Database Cloud EA, and the Integration Suite/Joule CPEAs to align the demonstrated run rates with annual ceilings, while redirecting the dormant entitlements in SAC BI, SAC Planning, SAP Build, Datasphere, and Ariba Spend Analysis toward named LoB use cases.
Stand up a Corteva-specific clean-core and extensibility governance forum to ensure that the next wave of agronomy, supply chain, and finance customizations land on BTP-governed extension patterns rather than in the S/4HANA core, protecting the upgrade path and the AI-ready data foundation.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
28.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$12K
YTD Budget$43K
YTD ACV: $68K
Projected Consumed: $29K · Projected Budget: $105K · Projected ACV: $163K
Solution Insights and Actions
Corteva's Data & AI architecture is inverted — the Database Cloud foundation is running at 2.2x–2.5x its sized capacity while the analytical and semantic layers above it (Datasphere, SAC BI) sit largely dormant, meaning the LoB is paying for heavy raw data processing without converting it into the unified business context and decision-ready insight the stack was designed to deliver.
Datasphere's flat ~$1.4K/month consumption against a $70K envelope indicates a single static pipeline is running rather than the federated knowledge-graph layer Corteva contracted for — without expanded modeling and source federation, SAC BI has no trusted semantic foundation to consume, which structurally explains its 0.2% attainment and blocks the AI-ready data outcome the architecture promises.
SAC BI is provisioned but operationally absent ($26 of $11,935 YTD), meaning Corteva's finance, commercial, and agronomy teams have no unified planning and analytics surface — the LoB is making decisions outside the governed SAP data layer even though the underlying Datasphere and Database Cloud capacity is already being paid for.
The BTP Enterprise Agreement (Public) shows zero activation while the Database Cloud EA is over-consumed, indicating Corteva has no extension, integration, or AI service plane stood up on BTP — workloads are landing directly on the database tier without the governed platform services needed to operationalize Joule, data products, or AI scenarios on top of Datasphere.
Commission a Data & AI architecture realignment between the over-running Database Cloud EA and the dormant Datasphere/SAC BI layers to ensure raw data consumption is feeding governed semantic models and business-facing analytics rather than untracked workloads.
Launch a Datasphere use-case expansion targeting Corteva's agronomy and supply chain domains to federate SAP and non-SAP sources into business-modeled data products that can then power SAC BI stories and planning models.
Activate SAC BI with a named first production workload — a finance planning model or commercial dashboard sourced from Datasphere — to convert the $29K dormant entitlement into recurring business consumption tied to a real LoB decision cadence.
Trigger a BTP Enterprise Agreement activation workshop to identify the first extension, integration, or AI service that establishes the platform plane Corteva's Data & AI stack currently lacks, and right-size the Database Cloud EA against its ~$1,060/month actual run rate.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
184.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$187K
YTD Budget$102K
YTD ACV: $145K
Projected Consumed: $448K · Projected Budget: $284K · Projected ACV: $347K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation is operating as an integration-led architecture rather than a balanced extension platform — Integration Suite and its underlying BTP consumption are running 1.4x–7x over plan while SAP Build sits at 12% attainment, meaning Corteva is industrializing system-to-system connectivity but not building the user-facing apps and workflows that turn those integrations into business outcomes.
BTP consumption is structurally under-contracted across the foundation — the Integration Suite-aligned CPEA envelope is on track for ~$230K against a $78K plan and the Joule Studio-aligned CPEA is pacing to ~$44K against $13K, indicating real workload adoption is outpacing the commercial envelope and exposing Corteva to overage rather than reflecting governed growth.
The low-code extension capability is effectively shelfware — flat ~$900/month SAP Build consumption against a $40.9K budget signals a single static workload and a capped citizen-developer community, leaving Corteva's Integration Suite APIs without a corresponding app/workflow layer to expose them to business users.
A third CPEA entitlement under Technology Foundation Other is fully dormant at $0 consumed, meaning purchased BTP capacity for foundational services (subaccounts, extensions, or platform services) has never been activated and is contributing nothing to the architecture it was meant to underpin.
Initiate a consolidated CPEA true-up and workload-tagging exercise across the Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and Technology Foundation Other allocations to right-size the envelope to the demonstrated ~$280K run rate and establish per-BU chargeback before overages compound.
Launch a SAP Build adoption program anchored on Integration Suite APIs — identify 2–3 citizen-developer app or workflow use cases that consume existing iFlows, and stand up Build Apps / Process Automation enablement to expand beyond the current single-workload footprint.
Run an Integration Suite capacity and use-case review to validate the May 143% spike as governed load, formalize the next wave (B2B, API management, event mesh) with Corteva's integration CoE, and tie new iFlows to Build-based consumer apps.
Trigger an activation diagnostic on the dormant Technology Foundation Other CPEA to identify whether unprovisioned subaccounts, missing enablement, or absent use cases are blocking consumption, and unblock the first billable workload.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
75.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$1.3M
YTD Budget$1.7M
YTD ACV: $2.0M
Projected Consumed: $3.1M · Projected Budget: $4.1M · Projected ACV: $4.7M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Procure-to-Pay architecture is operationally fractured at Corteva — Ariba Buying & Invoicing runs at 80% attainment with volatile monthly swings, Ariba Catalog has collapsed to ~$50/month since January, and Ariba Spend Analysis has flatlined at $0 since February, meaning buyers are transacting without guided catalog compliance and procurement leadership has lost the spend visibility loop needed to close savings.
The Treasury architecture is contracted as an integrated stack but deployed as disconnected fragments — Cash & Treasury (56% attainment), Multi Bank Connect (flat $1,180/month with a May ACV true-down), Digital Payments (74% capped), and S/4HANA Customer Payments (~70%) all show single-use-case plateaus, so Corteva owns an end-to-end cash-to-bank platform but operates it as four narrow utilities with no enterprise liquidity or payment factory outcome.
The FP&A and platform extensibility foundation is effectively unbuilt — BTP Enterprise Agreement sits at $0 consumption while SAC Planning is regressing (May dropped to 55% after three steady months), meaning the analytics-and-extension layer meant to unify financial and operational planning across Finance and Spend domains is delivering neither integration capacity nor forward-looking planning value.
Travel & Expense and Compliance are the only consistently healthy domains — Concur Expense, Reporting, Connectors, and Preferred Success all run at or above plan and DRC holds an 82–89% band — confirming that where Corteva has clear process ownership and embedded workflows, the architecture delivers; the under-adoption elsewhere is an operating-model problem, not a product-fit problem.
Reconstitute the Source-to-Pay value chain by re-activating Ariba Spend Analysis data feeds, rebuilding Ariba Catalog punchout/hosted content with 3–5 high-volume suppliers, and routing the volatile Ariba Buying & Invoicing categories back through catalog-enforced compliance to recover the combined ~$560K projected procurement shortfall.
Convene a Treasury architecture review that connects Cash & Treasury, Multi Bank Connect, Digital Payments, and S/4HANA Customer Payments into a single cash-to-bank operating model — expanding bank connections, payment channels, and AR scenarios rather than treating each product as an isolated workflow.
Launch a BTP activation and SAC Planning re-engagement workstream to land the first production BTP workload and extend SAC Planning into a second use case (workforce or sales planning), converting the dormant $390 BTP entitlement and the $10K SAC gap into a unified Finance planning and extension foundation.
Govern the over-consuming assets — Preferred Success Ariba, Preferred Success Concur, Concur Reporting, and Concur Connectors — with a prioritized engagement plan and a contract right-sizing conversation to protect high-value services before entitlements are exhausted in the back half of the year.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
127.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$1.6M
YTD Budget$1.2M
YTD ACV: $1.6M
Projected Consumed: $3.7M · Projected Budget: $2.8M · Projected ACV: $3.7M
Solution Insights and Actions
Corteva's Supply Chain Management architecture is effectively a single-pillar deployment centered on IBP, and the sustained consumption overrun signals that the LoB has operationalized the full planning stack — demand sensing, S&OP, inventory, and supply response — as its primary decision-making backbone rather than as a pilot or seasonal workload.
With 56.6% of the annual IBP ceiling consumed in 41.7% of the year and May running 58% above plan, the architectural risk is not technical but contractual — the planning platform that now governs Corteva's supply orchestration is on a trajectory to exhaust capacity mid-cycle, which would throttle the very S&OP cadence the LoB depends on.
The $355K/month run rate against a $225K budget indicates IBP workloads have likely expanded beyond the originally scoped planning horizons or scenarios, suggesting Corteva is leaning on advanced capabilities (multilevel supply planning, AI-driven demand prediction, response planning) at production scale rather than the lighter forecasting footprint the original sizing assumed.
Open a true-up and capacity re-baselining conversation for IBP (LPR318) to lift the annual ceiling in line with the demonstrated ~$355K monthly consumption and protect continuity of the S&OP process.
Conduct a workload decomposition review with Corteva's planning team to confirm which IBP modules (demand, supply, S&OP, inventory) are driving the overage and validate that consumption is tied to planning value delivered rather than unoptimized job, data, or scenario volume.
Assess architectural white space around IBP — particularly upstream execution (S/4HANA supply chain) and downstream visibility (Business Network, Analytics Cloud) — to determine whether Corteva's planning decisions are flowing into execution systems or stalling at the plan boundary.
Domino's Pizza LLC
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $803K
YTD Budget: $866K
YTD ACV: $958K
Projected Consumed: $1.9M
Projected Budget: $2.1M
Projected ACV: $2.3M
EA Insights and Actions
Domino's has bought the full closed-loop digital QSR stack — IBP planning, Business Network supplier collaboration, Ariba source-to-pay, HANA Cloud data backbone, and Integration Suite as connective tissue — but only the transactional middles are running while the collaboration, governance, and intelligence layers (Business Network transactions, Ariba Contracts/Sourcing, Datasphere/SAC on HANA, SAP Build extensions) are dormant, leaving a ~$230K+ annualized value gap and an enterprise that transacts but neither governs upstream nor learns from its own data.
Integration Suite is the only platform capability running hot (99% YTD, $1.9K overrun) across an otherwise contracting BTP estate, which means the iFlows that should be carrying IBP plans to the Business Network, Ariba contracts to Buying, and operational data into HANA Cloud exist as pipes without endpoints — the integration backbone is healthy but the cross-domain business processes it was scoped to enable have not been wired up.
HANA Cloud running without Datasphere/SAC while SAP Build declines to 52.7% attainment reveals Domino's has no architectural path from data to decision to action — Concur Analytics' 118% over-consumption proves the LoB will adopt analytics aggressively when it exists, making the dormant Data & AI and low-code layers the single biggest blocker to franchisee-facing intelligence and store-ops differentiation.
The Ariba Sourcing/Contracts decline (66% and falling) coinciding with dormant Business Network collaboration means Domino's procurement governance is eroding at exactly the moment its supply chain needs tighter supplier contracts and forecast sharing for a high-velocity ingredient supply chain — two independent domain weaknesses converging into one enterprise risk around supplier reliability and negotiated savings leakage.
Convene a cross-domain architecture review anchoring IBP, Business Network, Ariba Contracts, and Integration Suite on a single supplier collaboration reference flow so planning signals, contracted terms, and PO/ASN transactions move through one governed chain rather than four disconnected islands.
Launch a Data & AI activation workshop pairing HANA Cloud with Datasphere and SAC and scoping the first two SAP Build apps that consume that data, converting the combined ~$84K Build + HANA shortfall into franchisee analytics and store-ops use cases.
Run an enterprise BTP true-up and re-baseline that reallocates capacity from the declining Enterprise Agreement, Custom Domain, and Build entitlements toward the Integration Suite overrun, while binding the renewed plan to specific cross-domain extension workloads.
Commission a procurement governance restoration sprint that reconnects Ariba Sourcing and Contracts to Buying and onboards top-tier ingredient suppliers onto the Business Network, closing the upstream governance gap before the $119K network shortfall and $24K sourcing/contracts gap compound.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
75.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$41K
YTD Budget$54K
YTD ACV: $67K
Projected Consumed: $98K · Projected Budget: $133K · Projected ACV: $161K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation for Domino's is architecturally singular — HANA Cloud is the only active capability in BAIP, meaning the LoB has a database cloud running without the surrounding Business Data Cloud fabric (Datasphere, SAC, data products) that would convert raw storage and compute into business-ready insights and agentic AI.
Five months of 68-82% attainment with a $13.4K YTD shortfall and ~$35K projected annual gap confirm this is not a ramp curve but a structural scope deficit — HANA Cloud is being consumed as a transactional database rather than as the AI-ready data backbone it was contracted to be, leaving demand forecasting, store analytics, and supply chain intelligence use cases unserved.
Without Datasphere modeling and SAC consumption layers paired to HANA Cloud, Domino's data platform team has no architectural path to operationalize the contracted compute into franchisee-facing analytics or ML-driven operations, which explains why workloads cannot grow to fill the contracted capacity.
The agentic AI and business data fabric value proposition is dormant for this LoB — mission-critical pizza operations data (orders, store performance, supply chain) is likely still siloed outside HANA Cloud, blocking the real-time decisioning outcomes that justified the $132K annual investment.
Conduct a Data & AI architecture review with Domino's data platform team to map current HANA Cloud workloads against the Business Data Cloud reference architecture and identify which mission-critical domains (store ops, supply chain, demand) are not yet onboarded.
Position Datasphere and SAC as the missing consumption and modeling layers required to activate the $35K of unused HANA Cloud capacity, framing them as the route to ML-driven demand forecasting and real-time store analytics.
Scope a side-car workload expansion covering supply chain data tiering and franchisee analytics to absorb the $5.6K monthly ACV gap and lift attainment above 90%.
Engage Domino's AI and analytics leadership on an agentic AI use case roadmap anchored on HANA Cloud as the business data fabric, converting dormant contract value into pipeline for adjacent BAIP capabilities.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
81.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$88K
YTD Budget$109K
YTD ACV: $131K
Projected Consumed: $212K · Projected Budget: $261K · Projected ACV: $314K
Solution Insights and Actions
The technology foundation shows a classic split-brain pattern — SAP Integration Suite is running hot as a production workload (99% YTD, tracking to a $1,919 overrun) while SAP Build, Custom Domain, and BTP Enterprise Agreement are all contracting, meaning Domino's is successfully moving data between systems but is not building the extension apps, branded endpoints, or platform services that turn those integrations into differentiated business capabilities.
SAP Build's two-month decline to 52.7% attainment and projected $49K shortfall indicates the low-code development motion has stalled at Domino's — the platform that should be producing franchisee tools, store-ops apps, and pizza ordering extensions on top of the active Integration Suite backbone is instead seeing citizen developers disengage, leaving integration pipes without consuming applications.
The Custom Domain service contraction to 47.9% projected attainment while Integration Suite expands signals that new APIs and integrations are being exposed on default SAP-branded endpoints rather than Domino's-owned domains, weakening the trust posture for any customer-facing or franchisee-facing channels built on BTP.
The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement's three-month decline to 76.1% attainment confirms the foundational BTP services layer is being under-consumed even as Integration Suite overruns — Domino's is paying for platform headroom (runtime, identity, connectivity) that was scoped to support extension workloads that never materialized in SAP Build.
Reconnect the SAP Build and Integration Suite roadmaps by scoping two net-new low-code apps that consume existing iFlows, converting the integration overrun pressure into tangible LoB applications and closing the $19,532 Build YTD gap.
Run a domain inventory against the active Integration Suite API and iFlow catalog to identify customer- and franchisee-facing endpoints still on default domains, and migrate them onto SAP Custom Domain service to restore expansion on LPR663.
Initiate an Integration Suite capacity and governance review with Domino's integration team to right-size the FY2026 forecast against the $1,919 overrun trajectory and align on a true-up before contractual entitlement is breached.
Run a joint BTP workload discovery across the Enterprise Agreement and SAP Build to identify which provisioned-but-dormant platform services can be activated to support new extension use cases and reverse the consumption decline on LPR868.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
98.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$347K
YTD Budget$353K
YTD ACV: $434K
Projected Consumed: $834K · Projected Budget: $852K · Projected ACV: $1.0M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Source-to-Contract spine is structurally weakening — Ariba Sourcing has lost 34 attainment points in three months and Ariba Contracts has declined for four consecutive months to 66%, meaning the LoB is losing the upstream governance layer that feeds negotiated savings and compliant supply agreements into downstream buying.
Procurement execution is decoupling from procurement governance — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is pacing at 99.96% YTD while Contracts and Sourcing are projected to under-consume by ~$24K combined, indicating Domino's is transacting POs and invoices without the contract authoring and sourcing event throughput that should be authorizing that spend.
The Travel and Expense stack is structurally over-capacity across every layer — Concur Expense (114% YTD), ExpenseIt (114%+), Analytics (118%+), and Preferred Success (108%) are all over-consuming in lockstep, confirming T&E volume has genuinely outgrown entitlement rather than reflecting isolated spikes.
Analytics is actively shaping T&E behavior rather than sitting as passive reporting — Concur Analytics' sustained 100%+ attainment alongside Expense and ExpenseIt overconsumption shows the visibility layer is embedded in recurring finance workflows and driving the policy and submission activity downstream.
Run a joint Sourcing-Contracts diagnostic to determine whether categories have moved off-platform or sourcing events have stalled, and rebuild the event-to-contract pipeline so negotiated outcomes flow into Ariba Buying rather than bypassing governance.
Launch a contract migration and template adoption sprint in Ariba Contracts to bring offline agreements into the repository and reconnect authoring and approval workflows to the active Buying and Invoicing channel.
Initiate a consolidated true-up across Concur Expense, ExpenseIt, and Preferred Success to align contracted capacity with the demonstrated T&E volume before ACV ceilings are breached mid-year.
Document the high-value Concur Analytics dashboards driving sustained over-attainment and extend them to additional Domino's business units to protect and scale the one analytics capability proving embedded value.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
93.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$326K
YTD Budget$349K
YTD ACV: $326K
Projected Consumed: $783K · Projected Budget: $903K · Projected ACV: $783K
Solution Insights and Actions
Domino's planning backbone is architecturally complete on the demand side but broken on the supply collaboration side — IBP is running as production-grade S&OP at 100% attainment ($46,540 YTD), yet Business Network Supply Chain Collaboration shows a flat $55,968/month subscription signature with no transaction-driven variance, meaning planning signals generated in IBP are not flowing outbound to ingredient and packaging suppliers as the reference architecture intends.
The Business Network investment is contracted but dormant as a collaboration platform — the static consumption pattern and eroding budget attainment (97.3% → 87.9%, projecting a $119,982 annual shortfall against $791,592) indicate suppliers are not transacting POs, ASNs, or forecasts on the network, leaving Domino's exposed to the same manual upstream coordination risks the platform was bought to eliminate.
IBP is operating in a single-process steady state rather than as the multi-capability planning hub it is licensed to be — perfect metronomic consumption suggests one embedded S&OP cycle is running, but Demand Sensing, Response & Supply, and Inventory Optimization capabilities appear unactivated, capping the value Domino's can extract from its forecasting investment for a high-velocity QSR supply chain.
Activate forecast collaboration and PO/ASN collaboration scenarios on the Business Network with Domino's top-tier ingredient and packaging suppliers to convert the fixed $55,968 monthly baseline into transaction-driven consumption tied to real supplier activity.
Architect the IBP-to-Business-Network integration so that IBP demand and supply plans publish directly to suppliers as collaborative forecasts and replenishment signals, closing the loop between planning and execution.
Run an IBP expansion review with the Domino's planning team to scope activation of Demand Sensing and Response & Supply, deepening footprint beyond the current single-process run rate.
Recalibrate the LPR985 consumption plan against realistic supplier onboarding velocity to either commit to an enablement ramp that closes the $119,982 projected gap or right-size the subscription to actual network activity.
Eli Lilly & Company
Life sciences and healthcare
YTD Consumed: $7.3M
YTD Budget: $7.6M
YTD ACV: $8.4M
Projected Consumed: $17.5M
Projected Budget: $18.8M
Projected ACV: $20.1M
EA Insights and Actions
Across every L1 domain Lilly exhibits the same anti-pattern — premium success/advisory services (Preferred Success Ariba, SFSF, Commerce, BTM) burning at or above 100% while the underlying platforms they exist to activate (Ariba Sourcing, HXM Operations, Commerce Cloud, Process Intelligence, IBP, Batch Release Hub) run 40–98% under-consumed, meaning Lilly is systemically paying for transformation guidance that is being absorbed into run-the-business support rather than converted into platform adoption, which structurally caps ROI on a contract estate where roughly $1.5M+ of platform capacity is at risk of expiring unused.
BTP is split-brained at the foundation — Database Cloud and Joule Studio are over-consuming (135% and ~172%) while Integration Suite, BDC, and SAC BI are stalled, capped, or collapsing — meaning Lilly is ingesting data and spinning up AI agents faster than the integration fabric and analytics layer can govern or surface them, which breaks the AI-ready enterprise story across Finance (SAC Planning dormant), SCM (IBP-to-Batch Release disconnected), and HCM (HXM Operations under-scaled) simultaneously.
Lilly's pharma-critical Plan-to-Release-to-Compliance value chain is architecturally incomplete in three places at once — IBP runs as a static planning workload, Batch Release Hub is dormant in a GxP manufacturer, and Document & Reporting Compliance sits at 0.2% attainment — leaving the regulated-industry backbone the customer contracted for unrealized despite owning every component, exposing the business to lead-time, quality-release, and statutory-reporting risk the architecture was explicitly designed to eliminate.
The only capabilities showing organic demand-driven growth are identity (SSO pinned at 100%), AI tooling (Joule Studio at 172%), and compliance learning (SF Validated Learning at 96%) — meaning Lilly's workforce is pulling on access, intelligence, and regulated training faster than the LoB platforms behind them can scale, signaling that the next architectural bottleneck will be the integration and analytics fabric that connects identity-authenticated users and AI agents to under-consumed Ariba, SAC, IBP, and HXM workloads.
Convene a cross-domain Preferred Success governance review that converts all four success contracts (Ariba, SFSF, Commerce, BTM) from calendar-based drawdown to outcome-linked milestones tied explicitly to activating the dormant platform capacity in each respective domain.
Run an enterprise BTP rebalancing workshop that reallocates dormant Integration Suite and BDC budget toward the Joule Studio and Database Cloud overages, and mandates that every new AI agent or data workload is paired with a governed iflow/API and an SAC consumption use case before provisioning.
Commission a Pharma Value Chain architecture review with QA, Manufacturing, Supply Chain, and Tax leadership to wire IBP supply plans into Batch Release Hub disposition and Document & Reporting Compliance statutory output, closing the Plan-to-Release-to-Report loop on a single named legal entity as a reference architecture.
Initiate an enterprise contract rebaseline that lifts ceilings on the four demand-constrained capabilities (Database Cloud BTP +$20.9K, SAC BI +$6.5K, Joule Studio +$43K, SSO true-up) while right-sizing the under-consumed estate (Ariba Sourcing $447K, IBP $209K, Commerce Cloud $189K, Integration Suite $240K) into a single net-neutral commercial conversation rather than line-item negotiations.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
108.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$70K
YTD Budget$65K
YTD ACV: $14K
Projected Consumed: $168K · Projected Budget: $152K · Projected ACV: $34K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI footprint at Lilly is splitting into two opposite trajectories — Database Cloud BTP consumption is running 37% over budget while BDC Public BTP has collapsed 79% since January, signaling that data-tier workloads (HANA Cloud, persistence, data fabric services) are scaling with real production demand while the application extension and integration layer is being abandoned or migrated elsewhere.
SAC BI is architecturally healthy but commercially throttled — Q1 ran at 125% before flatlining at exactly 100% in Apr–May, meaning Lilly's analysts have hit a license ceiling and the unified analytics-and-planning vision (Joule, trusted AI, financial+operational planning) cannot expand even though demand structurally exceeds the envelope by $6,523 annually.
The BDC Public BTP collapse is the more strategic risk: without an active extension and integration platform, SAC BI becomes a stranded reporting tool disconnected from the data products and AI services BTP is meant to host, leaving Lilly with dashboards but no governed pipeline to feed forward-looking or AI-driven decisions.
Database Cloud BTP's sustained ~135% over-consumption with no corresponding lift in the analytics or extension layer suggests data is being ingested and stored faster than it is being modeled, visualized, or activated — a classic 'data lake without consumption' pattern that erodes ROI on the underlying platform spend.
Open a commercial rebaseline on Database Cloud BTP to lift the $56,461 ceiling ahead of the projected $20,928 overage, and pair it with a workload audit to confirm the growth is sanctioned production data workloads rather than unoptimized storage or runaway pipelines.
Expand the SAC BI tier to remove the Apr–May throttle and close the $6,523 projected overrun, anchoring the sizing on the Q1 125% pre-cap demand and the business units that drove it rather than the artificially flat May figure.
Run a discovery on the BDC Public BTP collapse to determine whether extension and integration workloads have migrated to a hyperscaler, been decommissioned, or simply stalled — the answer determines whether to reinvest in BTP adoption or formally rightsize the LPR868 BDC Public contract.
Establish an architectural link between the growing Database Cloud data estate and SAC BI consumption by sponsoring at least one end-to-end data product use case, ensuring the over-consumed data layer translates into business-facing analytics and planning rather than dormant storage.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
66.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$196K
YTD Budget$295K
YTD ACV: $151K
Projected Consumed: $472K · Projected Budget: $708K · Projected ACV: $362K
Solution Insights and Actions
The integration backbone is architecturally split-brained — Joule Studio on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is consuming at nearly 2x plan while the same agreement's Integration Suite allocation has decayed 54% and the standalone Integration Suite contract is flat at ~$25K/month, meaning AI agent tooling is scaling faster than the API and iflow layer that is supposed to feed it secure, governed data.
SAP Integration Suite is operating as a static iflow runtime rather than the AI-ready API fabric it is designed to be — tight $24.8K–$26K monthly consumption against a rising $341K budget plan shows the LoB is sustaining legacy integrations but not onboarding the new APIs, events, or B2B flows needed to make Joule agents and downstream apps consumable at enterprise scale.
The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is simultaneously over-burning on Joule Studio (projected ~$102K vs $59K ceiling) and under-burning on Integration Suite workloads (78% of $267K contract value at risk of expiring unused), indicating the BTP envelope is mis-allocated across capabilities rather than under- or over-sized in aggregate.
Rebalance the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement by reallocating the $239,836 dormant Integration Suite budget toward the Joule Studio overage and true-up only the residual gap, before pursuing a net-new ceiling expansion.
Launch a joint Joule–Integration Suite use-case activation plan that pairs each new AI agent workload on Joule Studio with a corresponding API, event, or B2B iflow on SAP Integration Suite, so agent growth pulls through the integration layer instead of bypassing it.
Conduct a Joule Studio workload review to classify the $42K YTD burn into production scaling vs experimental sprawl, and establish consumption governance before the 72% projected overshoot compounds.
Run an integration scenario expansion workshop on SAP Integration Suite to identify net-new AI-consumable APIs and retire any pre-provisioned tenants that are inflating the flat $25K/month baseline without delivering new business flows.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
48.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$47K
YTD Budget$96K
YTD ACV: $186K
Projected Consumed: $112K · Projected Budget: $245K · Projected ACV: $447K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Business Transformation Management stack is architecturally complete for process excellence — Process Management (modeling), Process Intelligence (mining), and Preferred Success (enablement) are all contracted — but only the enablement layer is fully operationalized, meaning Eli Lilly is consuming guidance without converting it into modeled or mined process outcomes.
