Process Intelligence is effectively dormant through Q1 with only a May activation spike, leaving the LoB with process models but no data-driven visibility into how those processes actually execute — the diagnostic half of the transformation loop is missing and $130K of contracted mining capacity is underwriting a single use case.
The April-to-May swing in Process Management combined with an $18.7K ACV expansion that didn't translate into consumption indicates newly provisioned modeling capacity is sitting idle, blocking the LoB from scaling process governance to the domains the expansion was meant to cover.
Preferred Success is pinned at its ACV ceiling for three consecutive months while the products it is meant to accelerate (Process Intelligence and the expanded Process Management scope) remain underutilized, signaling enablement demand is outpacing the LoB's ability to operationalize the tooling it underpins.
Redirect Preferred Success expert sessions toward activating Process Intelligence use cases beyond the May scope and onboarding the expanded Process Management capacity, tying enablement consumption directly to closing the dormant-capability gap.
Right-size the Preferred Success contract ceiling to reflect sustained capped consumption and prevent overage exposure while protecting the enablement workstreams driving Process Management and Process Intelligence adoption.
Establish named process owners and a recurring mining-and-modeling review cadence to convert Process Intelligence's May activation and Process Management's expanded capacity into a stable monthly consumption baseline rather than episodic spikes.
Launch a Process Intelligence use-case expansion plan across additional process domains to close the $130K budget gap and complete the model-to-mine feedback loop the Signavio architecture is designed to deliver.
Solution Area

65.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$121K
YTD Budget$185K
YTD ACV: $311K
Projected Consumed: $290K · Projected Budget: $462K · Projected ACV: $747K
Solution Insights and Actions
Lilly's Commerce architecture is split between a dormant growth engine and an overheating success service — Commerce Cloud is running at 45% attainment with ~$189K of contracted capacity projected to go unused, while Preferred Success Commerce is burning at 113%+ of plan, meaning Lilly is paying for accelerated guidance on a platform that isn't expanding its digital commerce footprint.
Commerce Cloud is being operated as static infrastructure rather than a unified commerce growth channel — the near-flat $10.6K→$12K monthly curve indicates no new storefronts, B2B portals, or patient-facing experiences are being launched, leaving capabilities like personalization, Context-Driven Services, and headless APIs architecturally available but functionally dormant.
The Preferred Success Commerce overrun against a flat Commerce Cloud baseline suggests the premium support entitlement is being absorbed by run-the-business tickets rather than transformation enablement — Lilly is consuming advisory capacity without translating it into expanded Commerce Cloud adoption, breaking the intended success-to-platform value loop.
Rebalance the Preferred Success Commerce engagement plan to redirect the $12.7K/month burn away from steady-state support activities and toward use-case activation workshops that unlock Commerce Cloud's idle $189K capacity.
Commission a Commerce Cloud capability audit with Lilly's digital team to identify a next-wave storefront or B2B/patient experience that converts the dormant ACV into an active growth channel.
Open an ACV rebalancing conversation with Lilly covering both products jointly — validate whether Preferred Success Commerce needs an uplift to cover the projected $17K overage or whether scope should shift to platform expansion outcomes.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
99.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$5.8M
YTD Budget$5.8M
YTD ACV: $6.3M
Projected Consumed: $13.8M · Projected Budget: $14.4M · Projected ACV: $15.1M
Solution Insights and Actions
Lilly's Procure-to-Pay spine is operationally live but strategically stalling — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is over-running its envelope by 9.3% ($677K projected overrun) while Ariba Sourcing has flatlined at its $366K ACV floor, meaning transactional PO/invoice volume is scaling without the upstream sourcing events and negotiated agreements needed to capture savings, leaving spend execution disconnected from spend strategy.
Preferred Success Ariba is burning entitlement on a calendar rather than on outcomes — the perfectly flat $84.9K/month drawdown alongside a sourcing plateau and a P2P ceiling breach shows advisory capacity exists but is not being deployed against the two highest-value architectural pressure points in the Procurement stack.
The Office of the CFO's analytics-and-compliance layer is effectively dormant — SAC Planning is locked at ~$3.4K/month (4.1% attainment, ~7% projected) on a single narrow model and Document & Reporting Compliance sits at 0.2% attainment, meaning Lilly has neither forward-looking integrated planning nor centralized statutory/e-invoicing compliance running on SAP, despite contracting for both.
Single Sign-on has pinned at 100% of ACV for three consecutive months while every other Finance & Spend product underperforms — the authentication backbone is the only capability showing organic, demand-driven growth, signaling that identity traffic is scaling faster than the LoB applications it secures, and a cap event here will throttle access to the very Ariba and SAC platforms Lilly is under-consuming.
Re-anchor the Procurement architecture by pairing an Ariba Sourcing event-pipeline review with a Buying and Invoicing transaction-volume audit, so the $447K sourcing underrun is closed with new RFx events that feed contracted savings into the over-running P2P channel rather than treating the two as independent line items.
Convert the projected $175K Preferred Success Ariba overage and remaining $566K entitlement into a milestone-based engagement plan explicitly tied to Sourcing event activation, supplier enablement, and P2P ceiling remediation, replacing fixed monthly drawdown with outcome-linked advisory.
Stand up an FP&A and Tax joint activation track that expands SAC Planning beyond its single static model into workforce/OpEx planning and onboards at least one high-volume legal entity onto Document & Reporting Compliance, addressing the combined ~$924K of unconsumed Office of the CFO budget.
Initiate a Single Sign-on mid-year true-up sized to the $4.2K projected annual run rate and identify the application populations driving the 5x January-to-February jump, ensuring the identity layer does not become the bottleneck for downstream Ariba and SAC adoption pushes.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
98.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$343K
YTD Budget$349K
YTD ACV: $371K
Projected Consumed: $824K · Projected Budget: $846K · Projected ACV: $889K
Solution Insights and Actions
Eli Lilly's HCM learning architecture is operating as a two-tier LMS — SF Learning for general workforce training and SF Validated Learning for GxP/regulated content — and both are tracking healthy month-over-month adoption curves (attainment rising to 96.3%), confirming the compliance-grade learning backbone is live and connecting completions to Employee Central skills profiles as designed.
Preferred Success SFSF is burning entitlement at ~119% of plan while the underlying SuccessFactors modules (Learning, Validated Learning, HXM Operations) all run 4–6% under budget — the success service is being consumed faster than the platform it is meant to accelerate, indicating advisory effort is going into break/fix or configuration rather than driving net-new module adoption.
HXM Operations — the cross-suite operational backbone for SuccessFactors (integrations, extensions, platform services) — is consistently under-consumed by ~$694 annualized, signaling that one or more HXM modules sitting on this foundation are dormant or under-scaled, which caps the value Eli Lilly can extract from its broader Talent and Learning investments.
The combined ~$1,000+ YTD shortfall across Learning, Validated Learning, and HXM Operations against a $22,837 Preferred Success overshoot reveals a value-realization imbalance — Lilly is paying for accelerated guidance faster than it is activating the SuccessFactors capabilities that guidance is meant to unlock.
Redirect Preferred Success SFSF service credits away from steady-state support and toward activating dormant HXM Operations workstreams and expanding Validated Learning cohorts, aligning the 119% advisory burn with the 94–96% platform consumption.
Run a joint HXM Operations module-utilization audit with Eli Lilly to identify which SuccessFactors components (integrations, extensions, reporting) are driving the persistent sub-scale consumption and target enablement at the weakest link.
Expand the SF Validated Learning content footprint into additional GxP-regulated cohorts at Eli Lilly to convert the rising attainment trend into full budget attainment and reinforce the compliance-training architecture.
Build a Preferred Success SFSF expansion case grounded in the $22,837 projected over-consumption, positioning an uplift tier that matches Lilly's demonstrated advisory velocity before entitlement exhausts.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
87.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$742K
YTD Budget$846K
YTD ACV: $1.1M
Projected Consumed: $1.8M · Projected Budget: $2.0M · Projected ACV: $2.6M
Solution Insights and Actions
Eli Lilly's Supply Chain Management architecture is functionally bifurcated — IBP is contracted as an end-to-end demand-to-supply planning platform but is being operated as a single recurring workload (flat $143K/month), meaning the LoB has bought a unified S&OP capability while running it as a static reporting tool disconnected from demand sensing, inventory optimization, and supply response.
Batch Release Hub is architecturally dormant in a pharma manufacturer where GxP batch disposition is mission-critical — 2.4% projected annual attainment with two consecutive zero-consumption months means Lilly is paying for digital batch release while QA release continues to run on the legacy (likely paper-based) process the platform was bought to replace.
The Plan-to-Release value chain is broken at both ends — planning signals from IBP are not flowing into expanded supply/inventory scenarios, and manufacturing output is not closing the loop through digital batch release, leaving Lilly with neither forward-looking supply orchestration nor downstream quality release automation despite owning both platforms.
The $14,103 unconsumed Batch Release Hub budget and $209K projected IBP shortfall together signal that Lilly's SCM investment thesis — integrated planning feeding compliant, automated release — exists on paper but not in operations, exposing the LoB to both planning agility gaps and compliance/lead-time risk the architecture was designed to eliminate.
Commission an executive adoption review of Batch Release Hub with Lilly's QA and Manufacturing leadership to confirm use-case ownership and drive the first live production batch disposition through the platform.
Decompose April's IBP consumption spike to identify the S&OP or demand review activity that drove 99.2% attainment, then institutionalize it as a recurring monthly planning cadence to raise the baseline workload.
Run an IBP module activation workshop with Lilly's supply chain planning team to bring inventory optimization, supply response, and control tower scenarios online alongside the existing demand workload.
Establish an architectural linkage review between IBP supply plans and Batch Release Hub release schedules so planning outputs trigger downstream quality release workflows and close the Plan-to-Release loop.
H B Fuller Company
Chemicals
YTD Consumed: $449K
YTD Budget: $752K
YTD ACV: $951K
Projected Consumed: $1.1M
Projected Budget: $1.8M
Projected ACV: $2.3M
EA Insights and Actions
H B Fuller has contracted BTP across three separate LoBs (Data & AI at ~$21K, Technology Foundation across Integration Suite/Joule/Other, and a fully dormant $0 Cloud Platform EA inside Finance & Spend), yet every tier is either dormant, declining, or activating in the wrong order — meaning the integration and extension fabric that should connect S/4HANA Public Cloud, Ariba, Concur, and Signavio simply does not exist as a coherent runtime, capping the entire SAP estate at out-of-the-box scope.
The Integration Suite erosion ($13.5K→$11.7K, tracking a $62K shortfall) is the single architectural fault line that simultaneously breaks the Ariba Contracts–to–Buying–to–Invoicing closed loop, the Concur Expense–to–Analytics data pipeline, the Signavio Process Intelligence source-system ingestion, and the Joule Studio agent reach — one shrinking layer is starving four LoBs of their cross-domain story.
H B Fuller is paying for premium advisory and intelligence layers — Preferred Success on both Private ($253K) and Public Cloud ERP, plus ~$893K of Signavio Process Intelligence and Management — but with Preferred Success flatlined at 38%, Process Intelligence reversing -$49K, and Process Management at 29%, the customer is underwriting transformation governance while operating ERP as a static system, guaranteeing that clean-core drift and undiscovered process debt will compound silently through FY2026.
The procure-to-pay and statutory compliance architectures are provisioned but structurally incomplete — Ariba Buying & Invoicing runs at 96% with $0 on Contracts, and Document & Reporting Compliance covers one workload with its companion service fully dormant — meaning H B Fuller's 90+ country manufacturing footprint is processing invoices without contract governance and filing statutory reports without scalable jurisdictional coverage, an audit and maverick-spend exposure that grows monthly.
Convene an enterprise BTP activation council spanning Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance & Spend Management to consolidate the fragmented BTP entitlements into a single governed workload backlog anchored on S/4HANA-to-Ariba-to-Concur integration and Signavio source ingestion.
Commission an Integration Suite reversal diagnostic that explicitly maps each declining iFlow to the downstream LoB capability it supports (P2P closed loop, T&E pipeline, process mining feeds, Joule agent reach) so remediation is prioritized by cross-domain business impact rather than platform utilization.
Run a transformation governance reset that wires Signavio Process Intelligence outputs directly into Process Management modeling and into Preferred Success advisory streams on both Private and Public Cloud ERP, converting three disconnected advisory investments into a single discover-design-adopt loop.
Launch a compliance and procurement architecture review to activate Ariba Contracts above the live Buying & Invoicing flows and expand Document & Reporting Compliance beyond its single workload, closing the governance gap before FY2026 statutory and audit cycles expose it.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
4.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$387
YTD Budget$8K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $928 · Projected Budget: $21K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
BAIP Data & AI is architecturally contracted on BTP across both Public Cloud and Database Cloud footprints, but with combined consumption of only $928 against ~$21K annual commitment, the foundational platform for extensions, integrations, and data services is effectively dormant — meaning every downstream Data & AI capability (analytics, ML, data pipelines) has no runtime to land on.
The May spike on Database Cloud ($387) alongside continued zero consumption on Public Cloud BTP signals H B Fuller is beginning to activate persistence/data services before the application and integration layer, an inverted adoption pattern that will leave data assets without governed extension or API surfaces to expose them to the LoB.
At 8.5% YTD attainment on Database Cloud and 0% on Public Cloud through 5 of 12 months, the run-rate trajectory points to roughly one-third of contracted BTP value being realized this fiscal year, which structurally caps how much Data & AI innovation the LoB can deliver regardless of business demand.
Convene a joint activation workshop covering both LPR868 entitlements to sequence a paired use case — one data/persistence workload on Database Cloud and one integration or extension workload on Public Cloud BTP — so the platform is exercised as an architectural whole rather than in isolated tiers.
Assign named technical owners at H B Fuller for each BTP tier and establish a monthly consumption baseline of ~$1,400 on Database Cloud and ~$913 on Public Cloud, tied to specific Data & AI use cases drawn from the LoB backlog.
Prioritize identifying the first production workload on Public Cloud BTP to break the five-month flatline, since continued zero consumption there will strand any data services activated on Database Cloud without an integration or extension path to the business.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
65.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$75K
YTD Budget$115K
YTD ACV: $107K
Projected Consumed: $179K · Projected Budget: $281K · Projected ACV: $257K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation is architecturally fragmented — BTP capacity is split across three consumption pools (Integration Suite, Joule Studio, and a wholly dormant 'Other' bucket) with overall attainment in the 34–45% range, meaning H B Fuller has contracted a broad platform footprint but is operating it as isolated point projects rather than a coherent extension and integration fabric.
Integration Suite — the architectural backbone for AI-ready APIs, process orchestration, and prebuilt content — is in active decline ($13.5K → $11.7K month-over-month, tracking to a $62K annual shortfall), signaling that integration workloads are being deprioritized or shifted off-platform and eroding the foundation that Joule Studio agents and downstream LoB AI use cases depend on.
Joule Studio's BTP runtime is ramping (103% growth Jan–May) while its sibling Integration Suite runtime is eroding — the LoB is investing in agent/extension tooling on top of an integration layer that is shrinking, which will cap the reach and reliability of any AI agents built on this foundation.
SAP Custom Domain service has zero ACV coverage for Jun–Dec despite being a persistent runtime entitlement bound to production SAP endpoints — this is a contractual gap that puts branded SAP tenant URLs at risk of lapse regardless of how well the rest of the BTP estate is performing.
Consolidate the three BTP consumption pools into a single governed workload backlog with H B Fuller's platform owner, prioritizing Integration Suite iFlows, API artifacts, and Event Mesh use cases that can absorb both the $62K Integration Suite gap and the $2.3K dormant 'Other' allocation.
Commission an Integration Suite reversal review to diagnose why monthly consumption is eroding — specifically whether iFlows are being retired, shifted to middleware alternatives, or simply not being built — and tie remediation to the AI agent roadmap forming in Joule Studio.
Engage H B Fuller procurement to true-up SAP Custom Domain service ACV for the unfunded Jun–Dec window and confirm the domain inventory behind the Jan–Mar overage to right-size the renewal.
Trigger a Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement activation workshop on the dormant Technology Foundation allocation to land at least one live workload and prevent forfeiture of the $5.8K annual entitlement.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
60.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$227K
YTD Budget$372K
YTD ACV: $512K
Projected Consumed: $544K · Projected Budget: $894K · Projected ACV: $1.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Business Transformation Management stack is architecturally split-brained — Process Intelligence (mining) and Process Management (modeling) are both contracted, but neither is operationally embedded, meaning H B Fuller has bought the full discover-to-design loop yet is running transformation initiatives without either fact-based process baselines or governed to-be models.
Process Intelligence is the architectural front door to transformation — its May reversal of -$49,543 against prior 100% attainment suggests the mining engine that should be surfacing supply chain and O2C friction has effectively gone dark, leaving downstream Process Management modeling with no data-driven priorities to act on.
Process Management entitlement scaled 5x to $97,009 ACV in May while consumption stayed at 29% attainment, indicating licenses were procured ahead of a modeler community and governance operating model — the LoB is paying for design capacity it cannot yet deploy against H B Fuller's transformation backlog.
With $559,107 unconsumed in Process Intelligence and $108,027 unconsumed in Process Management against a combined ~$893K budget, the transformation tooling layer is tracking to close FY2026 as shelfware unless mining outputs are wired directly into modeling workstreams.
Reconcile the May -$49,543 Process Intelligence entry with billing and delivery to confirm whether it is a true-up, credit, or data error before it distorts the FY2026 consumption baseline and ACV reconciliation.
Re-activate Process Intelligence by onboarding additional source systems (ERP, O2C, supply chain) and analyst users so the mining layer resumes producing the process baselines that justify the Signavio investment.
Pair the expanded Process Management entitlement with a named modeler cohort, a prioritized process inventory, and a governance cadence so the May ACV step-up converts into a sustained modeling run rate rather than a one-off spike.
Establish an explicit handoff between Process Intelligence findings and Process Management modeling backlog so mined inefficiencies in supply chain and order-to-cash are translated into governed to-be processes rather than parallel, disconnected efforts.
Solution Area
Cloud ERP Private
38.3%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$40K
YTD Budget$106K
YTD ACV: $126K
Projected Consumed: $97K · Projected Budget: $253K · Projected ACV: $304K
Solution Insights and Actions
H B Fuller's Cloud ERP Private LoB is operating its S/4HANA Private Cloud environment without the advisory scaffolding it has paid for — Preferred Success is architecturally the success-management layer (release guidance, CoE enablement, expert advisory, sustainability checks) that converts a private cloud ERP into a continuously improving platform, and its sudden flatline indicates the LoB has reverted to running ERP as a static system rather than a managed transformation.
The April step-down from $10,119 to a frozen $5,059 is not a gradual tail-off but a discrete disengagement, meaning a specific workstream — most likely a release-readiness, CoE, or empowerment track tied to an S/4HANA upgrade or roadmap milestone — has stalled, which directly puts the LoB's ability to absorb the next S/4 release and protect clean-core posture at risk.
With projected attainment at 38% and roughly $156K of entitled success services unused against a $253K budget, the LoB is underwriting a premium support tier while consuming it at standard-support levels, creating a value leakage where architectural risks (custom code drift, integration debt, upgrade backlog) accumulate silently inside the private cloud tenant.
Convene a Preferred Success service review with the H B Fuller S/4HANA Private Cloud owner to identify which advisory stream was paused in April and reinstate the named success resources against a documented engagement plan.
Re-baseline the Preferred Success engagement around concrete S/4HANA Private Cloud deliverables — release impact assessment, clean-core and extensibility review, integration health check, and key-user empowerment sessions — so unused entitlement is mapped to specific ERP adoption risks.
Tie remaining Preferred Success consumption to the LoB's S/4HANA roadmap milestones (upgrade waves, Fiori adoption, BTP extension governance) so the advisory layer is reactivated as a continuous capability rather than transactional hours.
Establish a monthly consumption and outcomes review between the SA and the H B Fuller ERP lead to prevent silent flatlines and ensure entitled success services translate into measurable private cloud adoption KPIs.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
70.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$107K
YTD Budget$152K
YTD ACV: $205K
Projected Consumed: $257K · Projected Budget: $371K · Projected ACV: $492K
Solution Insights and Actions
The procure-to-pay architecture is fundamentally broken — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is running hot at 96% YTD attainment while Ariba Contracts sits at $0 consumed and Ariba Buying is locked at ~$985/month (6.4% attainment), meaning H B Fuller is processing invoices and payments without contract governance or broad requisition discipline, exposing the LoB to maverick spend and unenforced negotiated terms.
Global tax and statutory compliance is architecturally provisioned but functionally narrow — Document & Reporting Compliance is locked on a single ~$310/month workload (38.5% projected attainment) while the companion Doc. & Rep. Compliance Service is fully dormant at $0, leaving H B Fuller covered for one jurisdiction's filings but unable to scale e-invoicing and statutory reporting across the 90+ country footprint the platform was contracted to serve.
The S/4HANA Public Cloud core is now consuming Preferred Success entitlements at a flat $6,653/month post-March activation, but the BTP Enterprise Agreement that should extend it sits at $0 — the LoB has a system of record without the extension, integration, or data-service fabric needed to connect S/4HANA to Ariba and Concur, capping the ERP's strategic value at out-of-the-box scope.
Travel and expense is the only fully-embedded capability in this LoB — Concur Analytics (115% YTD), Connectivity/Extract Services (113%), and ExpenseIt (123%) are all over-consuming while Concur Expense itself just collapsed to 37% attainment in May, signaling that the analytics and integration layers are running on inertia even as the underlying expense submission engine shows a sudden behavioral break that will erode the entire T&E data pipeline if unaddressed.
Convene a procurement architecture review with H B Fuller to activate Ariba Contracts as the governance layer above Ariba Buying and Invoicing, loading existing legal agreements and wiring clause compliance into the requisition and invoice flows already running through LPR746.
Run a compliance footprint workshop to map H B Fuller's legal entities and country obligations against Document & Reporting Compliance and the dormant Doc. & Rep. Compliance Service, expanding beyond the single fixed workload into multi-jurisdiction e-invoicing and SAF-T/VAT scenarios.
Launch a BTP activation engagement to identify the first two or three extension and integration use cases that connect S/4HANA Public Cloud with Ariba and Concur, breaking the $0 Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement baseline and unlocking the cross-LoB data flows the ERP investment depends on.
Diagnose the May Concur Expense submission collapse by pulling active submitter counts and report volumes, and confirm whether the consistent over-attainment on Analytics, Extract Services, and ExpenseIt reflects sustainable demand that warrants a T&E contract right-sizing rather than a year-end true-up.
Hill-Rom
Life sciences and healthcare
YTD Consumed: $288K
YTD Budget: $378K
YTD ACV: $453K
Projected Consumed: $691K
Projected Budget: $877K
Projected ACV: $1.1M
EA Insights and Actions
Hill-Rom's two operational L1 domains — Customer Experience and Finance & Spend — both show the same architectural anti-pattern: a single transactional pillar (Commerce Cloud storefront, Buying & Invoicing) running in isolation while the surrounding strategic capabilities (promotions/personalization, Sourcing/Contracts/Supplier Risk) sit dormant, indicating Hill-Rom has bought end-to-end SAP suites but is operating them as point solutions, forfeiting the closed-loop revenue-to-procurement story a MedTech manufacturer needs to defend margin against supplier volatility and channel complexity.
The combined ~$186K projected consumption shortfall across Commerce Cloud, Ariba Contracts, Sourcing, and Supplier Risk, set against a ~$36K overburn on Preferred Success Commerce, reveals an enterprise pattern where Hill-Rom is paying for expert services to sustain steady-state rather than investing in platform activation — a posture that erodes the ROI case for renewal across both domains simultaneously.
Neither domain shows evidence of an active integration or extensibility backbone (no signal of BTP, Integration Suite, or Master Data Governance underpinning the Commerce↔Ariba↔ERP flows), meaning Hill-Rom's medical-device commerce orders are not architecturally tied to governed supplier contracts or risk signals — a critical gap for an FDA-regulated manufacturer whose traceability and supplier-qualification obligations depend on that integration spine.
The dormancy of governance-layer capabilities (Ariba Contracts at 4%, Commerce Cloud personalization/AI features unused, Supplier Risk inert) indicates Hill-Rom's enterprise architecture is stuck at a 'transactional digitization' maturity stage, blocking the shift to data-driven, AI-augmented operations that the broader SAP investment was sized to enable.
Convene a cross-domain enterprise architecture review with Hill-Rom that maps the Commerce Cloud → ERP → Ariba Contracts → Buying flow end-to-end, identifying the integration and master-data gaps that are causing both domains to operate as disconnected transactional islands.
Reframe both Preferred Success engagements (Commerce overburn, and a parallel Ariba enablement track) onto a single capability-activation roadmap tied to the ~$186K underconsumption envelope, so expert services produce platform adoption outcomes rather than sustain status-quo operations.
Commission a BTP and Integration Suite footprint assessment for Hill-Rom to determine whether the extensibility and integration layer required to connect Commerce, Ariba, and the ERP backbone is contracted and activated, given that its absence is the root cause of the closed-loop break across both L1 domains.
Right-size the FY27 renewal conversation around a consolidated value narrative that bundles Commerce Cloud activation, Ariba governance reactivation, and integration-layer adoption, rather than negotiating each L1 entitlement independently against its current run-rate.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
Customer Experience
86.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$215K
YTD Budget$249K
YTD ACV: $284K
Projected Consumed: $516K · Projected Budget: $566K · Projected ACV: $683K
Solution Insights and Actions
Hill-Rom's Customer Experience architecture is operating in a paradox — SAP Commerce Cloud, the core storefront and catalog engine, is plateaued at 80-82% attainment ($39K cumulative shortfall) while Preferred Success Commerce is overburning at ~120% attainment, meaning the customer is consuming more expert guidance than they are activating commerce capabilities, which signals advisory dependency rather than platform-led growth.
The flatlined Commerce Cloud consumption band ($35.6K-$37K for five months) indicates the B2B/B2C unified commerce capability is anchored to a single fixed workload, leaving promotions, personalization, customer segmentation, and AI innovation features dormant — the LoB is running Commerce Cloud as a transactional storefront rather than the omnichannel growth engine it is architected to be.
The inverse trajectory between Preferred Success consumption (projected $15.9K overrun) and Commerce Cloud underutilization ($66K projected underconsumption) suggests Hill-Rom is using Preferred Success to operate the platform rather than to expand it, blocking the 'accelerate time to value' outcome that the Preferred Success entitlement was intended to unlock.
Convene a Commerce Cloud capability activation workshop focused on B2B channel expansion, promotions, and personalization to convert the $66K underconsumption envelope into active storefront features rather than shelfware.
Redirect the Preferred Success Commerce engagement plan away from steady-state operational support and toward enablement milestones tied to dormant Commerce Cloud capabilities, ensuring the overage is producing adoption outcomes and not just sustaining the status quo.
Right-size the Preferred Success Commerce entitlement to reflect the demonstrated $78.9K annual run-rate, and bundle the uplift with a Commerce Cloud adoption roadmap so the expanded tier is justified by capability activation, not reactive consumption.
Audit Hill-Rom's Commerce Cloud feature footprint (AI, segmentation, unified commerce, insights) against the $503K annual ceiling to produce a prioritized activation backlog that closes the recurring ~$7.9K monthly budget gap.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
56.7%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$73K
YTD Budget$128K
YTD ACV: $169K
Projected Consumed: $175K · Projected Budget: $311K · Projected ACV: $405K
Solution Insights and Actions
Hill-Rom's source-to-pay architecture is fundamentally broken at the governance layer — Ariba Sourcing is in active decline (78% drop Jan-May) and Ariba Contracts is dormant at 4% attainment, meaning the few supply agreements being negotiated are never landing in a managed repository, so Buying and Invoicing is executing transactions without upstream contract or sourcing discipline.
Supplier risk visibility is architecturally provisioned but operationally inert — the flat $201/month pattern collapsing to $100 in May indicates Supplier Risk is running as a license placeholder rather than an active monitoring feed into procurement decisions, leaving Hill-Rom's $65K of Ariba-routed spend exposed to financial, ESG, and geopolitical supplier events the platform was bought to surface.
Buying and Invoicing is the only operational pillar of the Finance and Spend stack at 80.5% attainment, but its $39.5K projected shortfall reveals that significant invoice and requisition volume is still flowing outside Ariba — without the contract and sourcing layers feeding it, the AP automation is processing transactions but not enforcing negotiated terms or three-way match against governed agreements.
The cumulative ~$120K projected consumption gap across Contracts, Sourcing, and Supplier Risk signals that Hill-Rom procurement has effectively reverted to a transactional-only operating model, forfeiting the strategic sourcing-to-contract-to-risk closed loop that justifies the Ariba suite investment.
Convene a procurement architecture review with Hill-Rom to re-establish the Sourcing → Contracts → Buying → Risk closed loop, prioritizing migration of active agreements into Ariba Contracts so downstream Buying and Invoicing transactions are governed by managed terms.
Diagnose the Ariba Sourcing five-month decline with procurement leadership and rebuild an event pipeline against the $51,638 unconsumed contract value, targeting categories that can flow directly into Contracts and then into Buying.
Activate Supplier Risk against Hill-Rom's tier-1 supplier list and wire risk signals into requisition and sourcing workflows so the $28,728 projected gap converts into embedded risk-aware procurement decisions.
Run a Buying and Invoicing channel audit to identify spend categories and supplier invoices still bypassing Ariba, expanding catalog-enabled suppliers and PO-flip invoicing to close the $39,506 consumption gap.
Ingredion Incorporated
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $441K
YTD Budget: $696K
YTD ACV: $602K
Projected Consumed: $1.1M
Projected Budget: $1.6M
Projected ACV: $1.4M
EA Insights and Actions
BTP is the common dormant denominator across every L1 domain at Ingredion — Datasphere/SAC have no extensibility layer, Integration Suite and Joule Studio are at $0, and SAC Planning/PaPM have no underpinning platform — meaning Ingredion has bought an integrated, AI-ready SAP enterprise on paper but is operating it as a set of disconnected SaaS islands, blocking every cross-domain ambition from Joule-enabled finance to AI-driven spend intelligence.
The architectural pattern across Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance shows the same governance failure repeated three times — Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreements procured without activation plans (combined dormant commitment ~$5,768 against $0 YTD consumption) — signaling that platform purchases at Ingredion are being treated as entitlements rather than capabilities, which will compound into multi-year forfeiture and an unrecoverable AI/integration gap as a CPG-ingredients business that depends on connected supply, spend, and demand signals.
Ingredion has two simultaneous and opposite architectural failures — analytics and T&E spines (Datasphere 108%, SAC BI 115%, Concur stack at/above plan) are over-consuming while the integration and extension fabric beneath them is at zero — meaning the layers business users touch are straining against contractual ceilings while the layers that would let those investments scale and connect remain inert, capping enterprise value at the BI glass rather than business process automation.
The Ariba Spend Analysis (240% of plan) versus Ariba Buying & Invoicing (-45% since March) divergence, viewed alongside dormant Integration Suite, reveals that Ingredion's procure-to-pay process is decoupling — visibility is growing while transactional governance is eroding — and without an activated integration backbone there is no architectural path to re-converge spend insight with executed buying, undermining margin discipline in a commodity-exposed ingredients business.
Convene a single enterprise BTP activation review across Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance that binds all dormant CPEAs to one anchor scenario (e.g., Datasphere-fed Joule agent invoking Integration Suite to surface Ariba spend anomalies in SAC Planning) to unlock the platform foundation across three domains in one motion.
Establish enterprise-level CPEA governance with named platform owners, monthly consumption telemetry, and use-case-backed activation roadmaps for every Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement on Ingredion's contract, replacing the current pattern of passive entitlement with accountable capability delivery.
Run a consolidated commercial true-up across the over-consuming analytics and T&E spine (Datasphere, SAC BI, Ariba Spend Analysis, Concur connectors) sized to the combined ~$14,500+ projected overrun, and pair it with a reallocation conversation that converts dormant BTP entitlement into activated integration and AI capacity.
Commission a cross-domain integration architecture review focused on the Ariba Spend Analysis to Buying/Invoicing decoupling, using it as the proof case to activate Integration Suite as the procure-to-pay backbone and demonstrate the business cost of dormant platform layers.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
105.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$50K
YTD Budget$47K
YTD ACV: $46K
Projected Consumed: $119K · Projected Budget: $107K · Projected ACV: $111K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI stack is architecturally coherent but only two-thirds activated — Datasphere is delivering the unified semantic layer and SAC BI is consuming it for decision-making, yet the BTP Enterprise Agreement that should host extension services, AI workloads, and integration runtimes sits dormant, leaving Ingredion with analytics on top of data but no platform to operationalize AI or custom data apps.
Datasphere and SAC BI are running structurally above plan (108% and projected 115% respectively), which confirms the semantic-layer-to-visualization pipeline is the load-bearing spine of Ingredion's analytics estate — the contract is undersized for real demand, not under-adopted, and the risk is hitting the ACV ceiling mid-cycle and throttling business reporting.
Two separate Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreements (BDC Public and Database Cloud) are both at $0 consumption against a combined $3,009 annual commitment, indicating the platform foundation intended to extend Datasphere with custom services, Joule-based AI, and database-tier workloads has never been activated — the AI-ready data capability Datasphere advertises cannot be realized without it.
The adoption pattern shows Ingredion treating Data & AI as a reporting investment rather than an intelligent data platform — heavy BI consumption with zero platform extensibility means trusted AI, Joule analytics, and federated AI workloads described in the catalog are contracted but architecturally unreachable.
Run a joint activation workshop across both Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreements to bind the dormant BTP capacity to concrete Datasphere-adjacent workloads such as AI model serving, data product APIs, or Joule enablement for SAC.
Initiate a capacity true-up for SAC BI and Datasphere together, sized against the combined projected overrun of ~$14,500, and frame it as protecting the analytics spine rather than an isolated SKU adjustment.
Conduct a workload attribution review on Datasphere's Q1 112.5% spike and SAC BI's Jan–Mar overage to confirm whether the elevated run rate reflects sustained business expansion (user, model, story growth) versus inefficient pipelines that should be optimized before true-up.
Build an AI-readiness roadmap that sequences BTP activation behind Datasphere's existing semantic models so the dormant platform spend converts directly into Joule, AI, and data product capabilities the LoB is already paying for.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation

YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$0
YTD Budget$1K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $0 · Projected Budget: $3K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The entire BTP Technology Foundation at Ingredion is architecturally licensed but operationally inert — Joule Studio (AI extensibility), Integration Suite (process integration backbone), and broader platform services all sit at $0 consumption, meaning the LoB has no active extension, integration, or AI fabric beneath its SAP estate despite paying for one.
The dormancy of Integration Suite is the most consequential architectural gap — without an activated integration layer, every downstream ambition (S/4 extensions, Joule agents, data services, third-party connectivity) is blocked at the foundation, leaving Ingredion's SAP landscape as isolated systems rather than a connected platform.
Joule Studio sitting at $0 means Ingredion has forfeited its AI co-pilot and agent-building capability for the year so far, blocking any LoB ambition to embed generative AI into business processes even as the contractual entitlement continues to accrue.
With $2,759 in combined annual entitlement and only 7 months remaining against a $0 baseline, the LPR868 CPEA is on a trajectory toward full-year forfeiture — this is a governance failure, not a consumption ramp issue, and signals the platform was procured without an attached activation plan.
Convene a single BTP activation workshop covering Integration Suite, Joule Studio, and Technology Foundation services jointly, anchored on one concrete integration scenario that exercises all three capabilities to unlock the foundation in one motion.
Prioritize Integration Suite as the first workload to activate, since it is the architectural prerequisite for Joule agents and any meaningful platform extensions at Ingredion.
Escalate the 0% CPEA attainment to Ingredion's executive sponsor with a consumption roadmap tied to named business use cases, since the $2,759 entitlement cannot be recovered through passive adoption.
Stand up a monthly LPR868 consumption governance cadence with platform owners to convert the dormant CPEA into measurable BTP service usage across all three capability areas.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
60.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$391K
YTD Budget$647K
YTD ACV: $556K
Projected Consumed: $939K · Projected Budget: $1.5M · Projected ACV: $1.3M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Travel & Expense stack is the most architecturally complete domain in Ingredion's Finance footprint — Concur Expense, Request, Travel, Triplink, ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, and Reporting are all live and pacing at or above budget, meaning the request-to-reimburse process is operating as a connected workflow rather than isolated modules.
The Procure-to-Pay process is architecturally contracted but operationally fracturing — Ariba Spend Analysis is consuming at 2.4x its planned ceiling while Ariba Buying and Invoicing has declined 45% since March, meaning the LoB has rich spend visibility but is increasingly executing transactions outside the governed buying and invoicing channel, eroding the value of the sourcing-to-pay investment.
The FP&A layer is half-built — SAP Analytics Cloud Planning has crossed into active use and Profitability and Performance Management is finally generating runs, but the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement that should underpin extensibility, integration, and data services for these analytics workloads is entirely dormant, leaving the planning and profitability models without their intended platform foundation.
Concur Expense's May collapse to 58% attainment breaks the otherwise healthy T&E telemetry and signals that the policy-and-audit fabric (Intelligent Audit, Request, Reporting) is now operating against a shrinking pool of submitted expenses, threatening the integrity of the end-to-end T&E control environment.
Stand up the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement as the integration and extension layer for SAC Planning and Profitability and Performance Management, anchoring activation to a concrete FP&A workload (data pipelines, planning extensions, or PaPM integration) rather than treating it as a standalone consumption target.
Run a P2P channel diagnostic on Ariba Buying and Invoicing to identify which business units or spend categories defected after March and re-route them through catalog and invoice automation, restoring alignment between the spend visibility Ariba Spend Analysis is providing and the transactional execution layer.
Open a commercial true-up conversation on Ariba Spend Analysis and Concur (Expense connectors, Direct Travel, Triplink, Preferred Success) where projected consumption is exceeding contracted ceilings, converting demonstrated over-attainment into right-sized commitments before capacity becomes a constraint.
Investigate the May drop in Concur Expense submission volume and pair the remediation with expanded Intelligent Audit rule coverage so the audit, reporting, and request modules continue to operate against a complete expense dataset rather than a degrading one.
Kraft Heinz Foods Company
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $2.7M
YTD Budget: $3.5M
YTD ACV: $4.5M
Projected Consumed: $6.6M
Projected Budget: $8.6M
Projected ACV: $10.8M
EA Insights and Actions
Across all five L1 domains, the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is the common substrate, yet its components are simultaneously overrun (BDC Public at 390%, Integration Suite on EA projected +$11.7K, Joule Studio hot) and dormant (Database Cloud at $0, Finance/Procurement EA activation at $0) — meaning Kraft Heinz is paying for a unified BTP backbone but operating it as disconnected silos, which blocks the clean-core, AI-ready architecture that every other domain depends on.
The integration spine is architecturally fracturing — the standalone SAP Integration Suite SKU is shedding $2,238 of ACV while Integration Suite-on-EA overruns, and this matters because Ariba Spend Analysis is flatlined, Business Network forecast collaboration is dormant, SAC BI is unconsumed, and Signavio Process Intelligence findings aren't flowing into Process Management, all of which are integration-dependent stories that cannot close without a single governed API layer.
Kraft Heinz has contracted a near-complete CPG enterprise stack (S/4HANA Finance, Ariba suite, IBP+Business Network, Signavio, SAC, BDC, BTP) worth multi-million ACV but is materially under-absorbing it — Process Intelligence at $526K remaining, Business Network at $1.1M shortfall, SAC BI at $0, Ariba Sourcing at 35%, Supplier Risk dormant — signaling that the customer bought a transformation platform but is operating it as point tools, putting both the FY26 consumption story and renewal narrative at structural risk.
The foundational identity and analytics layers are disengaged (Single Sign-On declining, SAC BI dormant, Database Cloud not activated) while transactional volume is overrunning across Ariba Buying, BDC Public, and Joule Studio — meaning Kraft Heinz is scaling execution and AI agent authoring on top of an ungoverned identity and unconsolidated data foundation, which directly undermines the trusted-AI and compliance posture required for a regulated CPG operator.
Convene a cross-LoB BTP architecture review to reconcile the simultaneous overrun and dormancy across Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement components (BDC Public, Database Cloud, Integration Suite, Joule Studio, Finance/Procurement EA) and rebalance ceilings onto a single governed consumption shape.
Run an enterprise integration coherence workshop that maps Signavio Process Intelligence outputs, IBP supply commits, Ariba Spend Analysis, S/4HANA Finance close, and Joule Studio agents onto a consolidated Integration Suite contract, eliminating the bifurcation between the standalone SKU and EA-hosted flows.
Establish an enterprise data and identity foundation track that activates Database Cloud with a first governed dataset, lights up SAC BI on a finance/operations workload, and re-engages Single Sign-On as the identity layer for Finance, Procurement, and Supply Chain solutions.
Initiate an executive value-realization review with Kraft Heinz covering the combined ~$1.6M+ of under-absorbed ACV across Process Intelligence, Business Network, SAC BI, Ariba Sourcing, and Supplier Risk, tying each to a named CPG outcome (supplier resiliency, tail-spend reduction, O2C cash, integrated planning) before renewal positioning.

Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI architecture for Kraft Heinz is inverted — the compute substrate (Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement on BDC Public) is burning at 390% attainment while the consumption-facing analytics layer (SAP Analytics Cloud BI) sits at zero, meaning platform spend is being driven by workloads other than the BI use cases the LoB contracted for.
SAP Analytics Cloud BI is architecturally provisioned but functionally dormant with $0 consumed against $4,122 YTD budget and $8,651 annual exposure, leaving Kraft Heinz without the unified analytics and planning layer needed for trusted, AI-assisted decision-making across finance and operations.
The Database Cloud footprint of Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is a non-starter at $0 against $100 annual, indicating the persistence tier intended to underpin Data & AI workloads has not been activated — any analytics or AI use case relying on governed BDC data has no foundation to land on.
The BDC Public overrun coexisting with a dormant SAC BI and dormant Database Cloud signals unmanaged, non-analytics consumption on the platform — the LoB is paying for capacity without converting it into the business intelligence outcomes the agreement was sized for.
Run a workload attribution review on the BDC Public Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement to identify which services are driving the $145 projected overage and determine whether they support sanctioned Data & AI use cases or unmanaged sprawl.
Trigger a go-live readiness review for SAP Analytics Cloud BI anchored on one high-value finance or operations reporting workload to convert the dormant $8,651 entitlement into measurable adoption.
Activate the Database Cloud tenant under Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement with a defined first dataset and consumption checkpoint so the persistence layer can support downstream SAC BI and AI scenarios.
Initiate a true-up and architectural realignment conversation that rebalances ceiling from the overrunning BDC Public line toward the underused SAC BI and Database Cloud entitlements to match Kraft Heinz's actual Data & AI consumption shape.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation

YTD Consumed$118K
YTD Budget$112K
YTD ACV: $27K
Projected Consumed: $283K · Projected Budget: $273K · Projected ACV: $64K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation is operating in a state of structural under-budgeting — three of four Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement workstreams (Joule Studio, Integration Suite, Technology Foundation Other) are running hot with projected annual consumption of ~$1,350, $205K, and $18.5K respectively, indicating BTP has moved from experimental to production-critical for Kraft Heinz but the commercial envelope was sized for a pilot-stage footprint.
Integration capability is bifurcating architecturally — the dedicated SAP Integration Suite SKU is decelerating ($5,030 → $4,328) and trending $2,238 under ACV while Integration Suite consumption on the Enterprise Agreement is projected to breach by $11,696, signaling integration workloads are migrating onto the EA envelope and leaving the standalone contract stranded rather than reflecting a real decline in integration demand.
Joule Studio consumption sustaining ~$140/month against a collapsed $1 budget shows the AI agent authoring capability has quietly entered active use at Kraft Heinz, but with no planned budget the LoB is consuming agent tooling without a governed adoption roadmap to tie it back to the Integration Suite APIs that are meant to feed it.
The architectural intent of Integration Suite — secure APIs optimized for AI agent consumption — is only half-realized: Joule Studio is live and integration workloads on the EA are over-running, but the dedicated Integration Suite contract is shedding flows, meaning AI agents risk being built against a shrinking, ungoverned API surface.
Consolidate the integration footprint by reconciling why Integration Suite workloads are growing on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement while the standalone SAP Integration Suite SKU is declining, and rationalize onto a single contracted vehicle to close the $2,238 ACV gap and the $11,696 projected EA overshoot.
Initiate a commercial true-up conversation on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement covering Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and Technology Foundation Other workloads, reforecasting to the observed ~$140/month, ~$17K/month, and ~$2,100/month run rates before the contract ceiling is breached.
Run a joint workload review with Kraft Heinz to map active Joule Studio agents to the Integration Suite APIs they depend on, ensuring the AI consumption layer and the integration governance layer are architecturally aligned rather than scaling on independent trajectories.
Identify net-new integration scenarios (S/4 interfaces, B2B partner onboarding, event-driven flows) to redirect onto the standalone SAP Integration Suite contract and restore the $5,030/month baseline before further ACV erosion compounds.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
55.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$172K
YTD Budget$308K
YTD ACV: $425K
Projected Consumed: $413K · Projected Budget: $750K · Projected ACV: $1.0M
Solution Insights and Actions
Kraft Heinz has architecturally assembled the full Signavio discover-to-design loop — Process Intelligence for mining and Process Management for modeling — but the two halves are operating as disconnected tools rather than a closed transformation cycle, meaning mined inefficiencies are not flowing into governed process redesigns and modeled processes are not being validated against execution reality.
Process Intelligence is the load-bearing capability in this LoB with $681K ACV versus Process Management's $46K, yet the May -$71K reversal and pre-reversal April surge to 120% attainment suggest mining workloads are being run in exploratory bursts rather than as a continuous transformation telemetry layer, leaving Kraft Heinz with episodic insight instead of an always-on process performance signal.
The May ACV expansion on Process Management from $19K to $46K without a corresponding modeling ramp indicates the customer has committed to scaling process governance but has not yet stood up the design authority, process owner network, or modeling cadence required to convert licensed seats into an embedded transformation practice.
With Process Intelligence sitting at only $156K consumed against $526K remaining ACV and Process Management showing a $43K gap, the Business Transformation Management LoB is materially under-absorbing its contracted capacity, which puts the renewal narrative at risk and signals that transformation initiatives (O2C, P2P, S2P) are not yet anchored to the Signavio platform as the system of record.
Reconcile the May -$71,055 Process Intelligence reversal with finance to establish a clean consumption baseline, then re-plan the $526K remaining ACV against named process investigations such as O2C cash collection, P2P maverick buying, and S2P cycle time.
Architect a closed-loop integration between Process Intelligence findings and Process Management models by requiring every mined inefficiency to produce a governed to-be process artifact, converting the two products from parallel tools into a single transformation pipeline.
Activate the May Process Management ACV expansion by onboarding the newly licensed cohort onto specific modeling use cases tied to the workflow that drove April's 116% attainment spike, turning a one-time event into a repeatable process ownership pattern.
Establish a Business Transformation Management steering forum with Kraft Heinz to assign executive process owners across O2C, P2P, and S2P, since the adoption pattern shows tooling is contracted but the operating model for continuous process improvement is not yet in place.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
87.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$2.0M
YTD Budget$2.3M
YTD ACV: $2.7M
Projected Consumed: $4.8M · Projected Budget: $5.4M · Projected ACV: $6.5M
Solution Insights and Actions
Kraft Heinz's procurement architecture is operating with a critical governance gap — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is overconsuming at 113% of plan ($346K projected overage) while Ariba Sourcing sits stalled at 35% and Ariba Supplier Risk is completely dormant, meaning the LoB is executing high-volume transactional purchasing without the upstream strategic sourcing, risk vetting, or supplier qualification that the Ariba suite was designed to feed into it.
The Finance close and profitability stack is architecturally assembled but operationally fragmented — S/4HANA Finance Cloud shows sawtooth period-end-only usage with a February outage, while Profitability and Performance Management sat dormant for Q1 before a single $213K May spike, indicating that unified financial reporting and granular P&L analytics are being run as episodic batch exercises rather than the continuous steering capability the architecture is meant to deliver.
Spend visibility has effectively collapsed across the procurement landscape — Ariba Spend Analysis flatlined to $0 in April and May after a declining Q1, which means the LoB is processing massive invoice volume through Ariba Buying with no analytical feedback loop to identify savings, consolidate suppliers, or inform the stalled Sourcing pipeline.
Foundational security and platform layers are disengaged — Single Sign-on consumption is declining inversely to its budget and Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement has zero activation, leaving the broader SAP estate without consolidated identity governance or an extension platform to host integrations between the active Finance and Procurement workloads.
Trigger a true-up and capacity realignment for Ariba Buying and Invoicing and Preferred Success Ariba to absorb the combined ~$493K projected overrun while simultaneously launching activation workshops for Ariba Supplier Risk and a re-baseline of Ariba Sourcing to restore upstream procurement governance.
Re-activate Ariba Spend Analysis through a root-cause review of the April–May zero-consumption event and anchor it to a tail-spend or supplier consolidation use case that directly informs the Sourcing pipeline and validates Buying overconsumption drivers.
Operationalize the Finance stack by investigating the February S/4HANA Finance Cloud outage, converting the May Profitability and Performance Management spike into recurring monthly cost allocation and P&L models, and scaling Document & Reporting Compliance into additional jurisdictions to lock in its proven run-rate.
Convene an identity and platform review with Kraft Heinz to diagnose Single Sign-on disengagement and activate the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement against a concrete first workload, restoring the authentication and extension foundation the Finance and Procurement solutions depend on.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
55.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$457K
YTD Budget$830K
YTD ACV: $1.3M
Projected Consumed: $1.1M · Projected Budget: $2.2M · Projected ACV: $3.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
Supply Chain Collaboration is the sole Business Network capability contracted for Kraft Heinz's supply chain, meaning the entire architectural promise of supplier-side resiliency — forecast sharing, PO collaboration, and inventory visibility — rests on this one product, and its erratic consumption pattern indicates the LoB is treating it as a transactional bolt-on rather than the trading partner backbone it was designed to be.
The volatile March-to-May consumption curve signals episodic supplier onboarding rather than steady-state document collaboration, which architecturally means Kraft Heinz has connectivity without process embedding — POs and ASNs may flow for onboarded suppliers, but forecast collaboration and procurement collaboration capabilities that drive true supply chain resiliency remain dormant.
With 20.8% budget attainment at 58% of the year elapsed and a projected $1.1M shortfall against the $2.19M ACV, the contracted collaboration footprint is materially under-activated, leaving demand-signal synchronization between S/4HANA, IBP, and the supplier base architecturally possible but operationally unrealized.
Without forecast collaboration live on the Business Network, IBP's supply planning outputs cannot close the loop with suppliers, breaking the intended architectural pattern where IBP commitments become supplier-visible signals — Kraft Heinz is planning internally but collaborating externally only on transactional documents.
Activate forecast collaboration and scheduling agreement releases on the Business Network to move Kraft Heinz from transactional PO/ASN exchange into the predictive supplier collaboration tier the LoB contracted for.
Define a supplier segmentation and onboarding wave plan that targets the top spend and top disruption-risk suppliers, converting the unrealized $1.1M of ACV into measurable trading partner coverage rather than continued episodic onboarding spikes.
Establish integration between IBP supply commits and Business Network forecast collaboration so demand signals flow end-to-end, closing the architectural gap between internal planning and external supplier execution.
Introduce recurring document automation use cases — self-billing, quality notifications, consignment, and subcontracting collaboration — to stabilize monthly consumption against the $174K–$211K budget pace and embed the network into steady-state supply operations.
Lancaster Colony Corporation
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $52K
YTD Budget: $85K
YTD ACV: $130K
Projected Consumed: $124K
Projected Budget: $208K
Projected ACV: $311K
EA Insights and Actions
Every BTP-dependent stream across Lancaster Colony's estate — Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance extensibility — sits at $0 consumption against ~$4,074 combined annual entitlement, meaning the entire platform layer that was meant to unify data, integration, and AI across the enterprise is contractually owned but architecturally absent, leaving every L1 domain to operate as an isolated SaaS island with no sanctioned path to interoperate.
The Signavio stack is the only operationally live capability in the portfolio, but with Process Intelligence at 30% attainment and Process Management at 20.9% the discover-to-redesign loop is broken inside Signavio itself, and because Integration Suite is dormant there is also no downstream path to push redesigned processes into S/4 or LoB execution — meaning Lancaster Colony can see process pain but cannot architecturally act on it.
With CPEA dormant simultaneously across BDC Public, Database Cloud, Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and Finance BTP entitlements, the consistent pattern is absence of a named platform owner rather than workload-specific blockers, which signals an enterprise governance gap that will cause the ~$3,900+ of FY2026 BTP entitlement to forfeit and force any future clean-core ambition to restart from procurement.
The combination of a live process-mining capability with a dormant integration and data platform means Lancaster Colony — a CPG manufacturer whose margin depends on O2C velocity, trade-spend control, and supply responsiveness — has bought the tools to identify process waste but none of the platform plumbing to automate remediation, undermining the core industry thesis behind the SAP investment.
Convene a single enterprise-level BTP activation council spanning Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance to nominate one anchor cross-domain use case (e.g., Signavio-identified O2C inefficiency remediated via Integration Suite flow into S/4 with SAC planning extension) that simultaneously exercises CPEA entitlements across all three L1 areas.
Appoint a single accountable BTP platform owner at Lancaster Colony with authority over Integration Suite, Database Cloud, and Joule Studio consumption, since five months of uniform zero usage across four independent contracts confirms the gap is governance, not technology.
Establish a closed-loop architectural contract between Signavio and BTP by mandating that every Process Intelligence finding lands as a Process Management to-be model and is executed via an Integration Suite flow, converting the only live capability into the forcing function for platform activation.
Run an enterprise architecture review of the full $165k+ FY2026 SAP commitment to rebalance entitlement against realistic consumption trajectories and prevent recurring forfeiture of the BTP layer that underpins every other domain's roadmap.

BAIP - Data & AI

Projected Consumed: $0 · Projected Budget: $2K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The BAIP Data & AI foundation for Lancaster Colony is architecturally contracted but operationally inert — Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is dormant across both BDC Public and Database Cloud tenants, meaning the LoB has no active runtime for data pipelines, AI services, or analytics extensions despite holding $1,880 in combined annual entitlement.
With zero consumption against $712 of already-elapsed YTD budget across both CPEA contracts, the Data & AI capability has not progressed past procurement — there is no first workload, no integration backbone, and no database cloud footprint, which blocks any downstream BAIP use cases (AI Core, HANA Cloud, Datasphere) that depend on an active BTP subscription.
The parallel stall on both the application platform (BDC Public) and the data platform (Database Cloud) signals a foundational onboarding gap rather than a workload prioritization issue — without the database tier activated, even a successful BDC Public launch would lack a persistence layer for Data & AI workloads.
Run a joint CPEA activation workshop covering both BDC Public and Database Cloud entitlements to select a single anchor Data & AI use case (e.g., Datasphere ingestion or AI Core inference) that exercises both contracts simultaneously.
Pair Lancaster Colony with SAP BTP enablement to provision a baseline HANA Cloud or Datasphere instance against LPR868 Database Cloud, establishing recurring burn and unblocking the dependent BAIP data services.
Build a phased consumption ramp tied to named service activations (Integration Suite, Build, AI Core) against the remaining $1,168 of combined FY2026 budget to recover entitlement before year-end forfeiture.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
0.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$0
YTD Budget$1K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $0 · Projected Budget: $4K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The entire BTP Technology Foundation is architecturally contracted but operationally dormant — Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and broader platform services all sit at $0 consumption against $744, $505, and $195 cumulative budgets respectively, meaning Lancaster Colony has no extensibility, no integration backbone, and no AI development surface despite owning the platform to deliver them.
The dormant Integration Suite is the most consequential gap — without an activated iPaaS layer, any S/4 or line-of-business extension built on BTP has no sanctioned path to exchange data with surrounding systems, so even if Joule Studio or other BTP services were switched on tomorrow they would land in an integration vacuum.
Joule Studio inactivity signals the LoB has no foundation for AI-assisted extensibility or Joule agent development, which means business-user productivity scenarios and generative extensions that SAP's roadmap increasingly assumes are simply unavailable to Lancaster Colony today.
With 42% of FY2026 elapsed and zero consumption across all three Technology Foundation streams, the $3,817 combined annual entitlement is on a trajectory to be forfeited entirely, indicating this is a procurement decision that never converted into an architectural program.
Convene a single Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement activation workshop covering Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and Technology Foundation together, since the three streams are architecturally interdependent and cannot be operationalized in isolation.
Anchor initial Integration Suite consumption to a named S/4 or SaaS-to-SaaS interface so the iPaaS layer is established as the sanctioned integration backbone before extension workloads are introduced on top of it.
Identify one concrete Joule Studio use case — such as an agent or AI-assisted extension tied to an existing LoB process — to convert the dormant AI development entitlement into a measurable adoption baseline.
Assign a technical owner at Lancaster Colony accountable for BTP consumption across all three streams, since five months of uniform zero usage points to absent ownership rather than a workload-specific blocker.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
62.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$52K
YTD Budget$83K
YTD ACV: $130K
Projected Consumed: $124K · Projected Budget: $202K · Projected ACV: $311K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Business Transformation Management stack is architecturally bifurcated — Process Intelligence is generating mining signals while Process Management sits at 20.9% attainment, meaning Lancaster Colony can see process inefficiencies but has no governed target-state models to remediate them, breaking the discover-to-improve loop Signavio was bought to deliver.
Process Intelligence's May -$3,883 reversal against a 30% YTD attainment on a $161,077 annual budget signals the analytics layer is being consumed tactically on a single use case rather than scaled across LoB processes, leaving the transformation program without the breadth of fact-based insight needed to prioritize enterprise process change.
Process Management consumption of $3,446 YTD with only one month above $1,000 indicates the Collaboration Hub is effectively unpopulated — without published, owned process content, mined insights from Process Intelligence cannot be translated into standardized SOPs, governance, or change actions across the business.
Bind Process Intelligence findings to Process Management modeling by selecting one priority end-to-end process (O2C or P2P), assigning a process owner, and requiring every mined inefficiency to land as a modeled to-be process published in the Collaboration Hub.
Reconcile the May -$3,883 Process Intelligence adjustment with billing and restore the April run-rate by expanding active investigations beyond the initial use case and onboarding additional process analysts to close the $45,239 projected annual shortfall.
Stand up a modeler enablement and publishing cadence on Process Management with explicit monthly content targets to lift consumption above the $3,500 run-rate and convert the $37,736 remaining ACV into governed process assets.
