Solution Insights and Actions
The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is the foundational extensibility and integration layer for Finance and Spend Management, yet zero consumption against the $71 YTD budget means Lancaster Colony has no active BTP-based finance extensions, custom planning apps, or integration flows — the LoB is operating on out-of-the-box capabilities only, with no architectural runway for differentiated finance automation.
With $0 consumed of the $186 annual commitment, the FP&A function is paying for an enablement platform it isn't using, meaning any roadmap items dependent on BTP — such as SAC-to-source-system integrations, planning extensions, or finance data pipelines — are architecturally blocked despite the contract being in place.
The collapsed adoption runway (requiring ~$26.5/month versus the planned $13–$18) signals this is not a ramp problem but a missing activation owner — no finance workload has been nominated to the platform, which typically indicates the LoB and IT have not jointly identified which Spend or FP&A use case the BTP entitlement is meant to enable.
Run a BTP activation workshop with Finance and Spend Management stakeholders to nominate a first production workload — likely a SAC planning extension, an Ariba/S4 integration flow, or a finance data product — and convert the dormant $71 YTD entitlement into a live architectural capability.
Build a parallel onboarding plan across BTP extension, integration, and data services to absorb the $115 remaining budget, rather than sequencing services, so the FP&A and Spend domains gain multiple capability footholds simultaneously.
Assign a named BTP platform owner within Finance to govern consumption, prioritize use cases, and prevent the entitlement from lapsing into a repeated zero-adoption cycle next fiscal year.
Land O'Lakes Inc.
Agribusiness
YTD Consumed: $563K
YTD Budget: $674K
YTD ACV: $836K
Projected Consumed: $1.4M
Projected Budget: $1.6M
Projected ACV: $2.0M
EA Insights and Actions
Integration Suite at zero consumption is the single architectural fault line running through every L1 domain — it simultaneously prevents Datasphere from ingesting broader source systems in Data & AI, blocks Fieldglass-to-Ariba-to-S/4 reconnection in Spend, and forces all cross-application flows onto legacy middleware, meaning Land O'Lakes cannot deliver any closed-loop enterprise process despite owning the components to do so.
The CPEA envelope (BDC Public, Database Cloud, and BTP services totaling ~$20K+ annual) is flatlined at near-zero burn while Datasphere, Joule Studio, and Ariba Spend Analysis each independently stall — the common root cause is an unactivated platform substrate, so Land O'Lakes is operating each SAP solution as an island rather than the integrated, AI-ready enterprise architecture it has contracted for.
The Source-to-Pay chain is architecturally broken in the middle — Fieldglass dark and Ariba Sourcing flatlined while Ariba Buying overruns 108% and Concur over-consumes across every SKU — meaning Land O'Lakes is processing invoice and expense volume without upstream sourcing, services, or analytics governance, exposing the enterprise to uncontrolled spend leakage precisely where a CPG/agribusiness depends on margin discipline.
Across all three L1 areas the pattern is identical: consumption layers (SAC BI, Concur, Ariba Buying) are active or over-running while semantic, integration, and AI extensibility layers (Datasphere, Integration Suite, Joule, BTP, Fieldglass, Spend Analysis) are dormant — Land O'Lakes has an enterprise architecture inverted toward transactional execution with no intelligence or governance layer, which will block any move toward predictive supply chain, agentic finance, or AI-driven sourcing outcomes.
Convene a cross-domain enterprise architecture review with Land O'Lakes CCoE to reposition Integration Suite as the shared backbone for Data & AI (Datasphere connectivity), Spend (Fieldglass–Ariba–S/4 reconnection), and Joule extensibility, anchoring CPEA activation to these three named workloads rather than per-LoB pilots.
Establish a single enterprise consumption governance forum spanning Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance & Spend so Datasphere, BTP/CPEA, Ariba Spend Analysis, and Fieldglass are managed against integrated architectural outcomes instead of isolated SKU attainment.
Run a Source-to-Pay architecture remediation workshop to reactivate Fieldglass and expand Ariba Sourcing as upstream controls on the Ariba Buying overrun, and feed the resulting spend signals into Datasphere/SAC BI to give procurement leadership a closed-loop view.
Reframe the FY26 renewal narrative for LPR868 and the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreements with the account executive and Land O'Lakes sponsor — positioning BTP, Datasphere, and Joule as the missing intelligence layer for an already-active transactional estate rather than independent platform entitlements at risk of forfeiture.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
28.7%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$42K
YTD Budget$146K
YTD ACV: $236K
Projected Consumed: $100K · Projected Budget: $352K · Projected ACV: $567K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation for Land O'Lakes is architecturally assembled but functionally stalled — Datasphere is live as the semantic data layer feeding SAC BI for visualization, yet Datasphere sits at 6% realization while SAC BI consumption is declining, meaning the unified data fabric is delivering one narrow pipeline to BI rather than powering the broader analytics and AI estate it was contracted to enable.
Both Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreements (BDC Public and Database Cloud) remain at $0 consumption across five months, leaving the LoB without the BTP runtime and database services needed to extend Datasphere with custom data products, AI services, or integration flows — the platform layer that should multiply the value of SAC BI and Datasphere is entirely dormant.
SAC BI's two-month consumption decline against a healthy 31% YTD attainment indicates the LoB has stood up an initial set of dashboards but has not expanded beyond the original user base or business domain, so the 'unified analytics and planning' promise is degenerating into static reporting disconnected from Datasphere's broader semantic model.
The combined picture shows a Data & AI stack where the semantic layer (Datasphere) is underused, the consumption layer (SAC BI) is contracting, and the extensibility layer (BTP) is unactivated — Land O'Lakes is paying for an integrated AI-ready data platform but operating it as a siloed BI tool.
Convene a joint Datasphere–SAC BI architecture review with Land O'Lakes data and analytics owners to identify net-new business domains (finance, supply chain, operations) where Datasphere semantic models can feed SAC BI dashboards, breaking both the Datasphere flatline and the SAC BI decline with a single coordinated expansion.
Activate both Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreements by anchoring them to concrete Data & AI extension use cases — such as custom data product development on BTP, Joule enablement in SAC, or AI-ready data pipelines into Datasphere — rather than treating BTP activation as an independent workstream.
Run a Datasphere technical health check to determine whether the $3K/month ceiling reflects a sizing constraint, limited source system connectivity, or user enablement gap, and remediate the specific blocker preventing the platform from scaling to its $257K annual capacity.
Establish a unified Data & AI consumption governance cadence covering Datasphere, SAC BI, and both BTP agreements together, so adoption decisions are made against the integrated architecture rather than per-product attainment percentages.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
0.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$74
YTD Budget$8K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $178 · Projected Budget: $20K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation layer is architecturally contracted but operationally inert — CPEA is the single entitlement vehicle funding Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and broader BTP services, yet all three are flatlined at 1.8% or 0% consumption, meaning Land O'Lakes has no live AI extensibility, no iPaaS-based integration fabric, and no platform substrate to extend S/4 or surrounding LoB systems.
The Integration Suite zero-burn is the most consequential gap because it leaves the LoB without a governed integration backbone — any cross-application data flow is therefore happening through point-to-point or legacy middleware, accumulating technical debt and blocking the API-led architecture that downstream SAP investments assume.
Joule Studio's $74 footprint indicates sandbox-level experimentation only, meaning the generative AI and copilot roadmap for Land O'Lakes has no production anchor and cannot deliver the agentic extensions that BAIP business cases typically depend on.
With 42% of the fiscal year elapsed and effectively zero consumption against a combined ~$19.9K annual CPEA envelope, the contract is trending toward full forfeiture and a high-risk renewal posture rather than a platform foundation.
Convene a CPEA activation workshop with Land O'Lakes enterprise architects to commit Integration Suite as the first production workload, targeting a named iPaaS use case that retires a legacy integration and proves the platform pattern.
Define a Joule Studio production use case anchored to an existing S/4 or LoB process so the AI investment moves from exploratory spend into a measurable business capability.
Establish a BTP consumption governance cadence with the Land O'Lakes CCoE to track monthly CPEA burn across Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and Foundation services, with a workload backlog sized to absorb the remaining contract value.
Escalate the CPEA underutilization to the account executive and customer sponsor to reframe LPR868 as a platform enablement program rather than a passive entitlement, mitigating renewal risk on the Technology Foundation.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
100.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$521K
YTD Budget$520K
YTD ACV: $600K
Projected Consumed: $1.2M · Projected Budget: $1.2M · Projected ACV: $1.4M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Procure-to-Pay spine is operating with a broken front end — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is running 108% over plan with a projected $82K overrun while Ariba Sourcing is flatlined at ~57% attainment and Fieldglass Services Procurement has gone dark, meaning Land O'Lakes is transacting heavy invoice volume without the upstream strategic sourcing and services-spend governance that justifies the suite investment.
The Travel & Expense stack is the most architecturally coherent domain in the LoB — Concur Expense, ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, Reporting Services, and Connect/Extract services are all consistently over-attaining, indicating an end-to-end T&E workflow (capture → audit → reporting → integration) that is genuinely operationalized, but the entire stack is on track to breach contract ceilings simultaneously.
External services spend is architecturally unmanaged — Fieldglass at $0 consumption from March onward means SOW, contingent labor, and vendor time tracking are happening outside SAP, leaving Ariba Buying to process services invoices without the upstream requisition, rate card, and reconciliation controls Fieldglass is designed to enforce.
Procurement intelligence is stuck in a descriptive baseline — Ariba Spend Analysis is flat at ~85% attainment with a projected 21% shortfall while Preferred Success Ariba is over-consuming at 119%, signaling the customer is paying for guided adoption but not channeling it into expanding analytics scope or feeding insights back into Sourcing event creation.
Escalate to Land O'Lakes procurement leadership to reconnect the Source-to-Pay chain by reactivating Fieldglass Services Procurement against a defined services category and expanding Ariba Sourcing events to upstream the $82K Ariba Buying overrun into governed, contracted spend.
Initiate a consolidated true-up across the Concur portfolio (Expense, ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, Reporting Services, Connect/Extract) to right-size the T&E contract ceiling to the demonstrated end-to-end adoption rather than negotiating each SKU in isolation.
Redirect Preferred Success Ariba engagement hours toward expanding Ariba Spend Analysis category coverage and translating findings into new Ariba Sourcing events, converting over-consumed enablement into measurable sourcing pipeline.
Run a root-cause review on the May Concur Expense drop and the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement zero-burn to determine whether these reflect integration, policy, or activation gaps that threaten the broader Finance and Spend architecture.
Marathon Petroleum Company LP
Oil, gas, and energy
YTD Consumed: $767K
YTD Budget: $805K
YTD ACV: $1.0M
Projected Consumed: $1.8M
Projected Budget: $2.0M
Projected ACV: $2.4M
EA Insights and Actions
Marathon has contracted BTP across Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance (CPEA at $0 against budget, Public tenant dormant, Tech Foundation Other idle) yet every L1 domain — S/4HANA Private Cloud, Ariba, Signavio, SAC Planning — depends on BTP as the integration, extension, and AI activation fabric, meaning the enterprise has bought the connective tissue for a clean-core, intelligent-enterprise architecture but is operating each domain as an island, guaranteeing custom code in the core and broken cross-domain data flows at the next upgrade.
The only proven BTP capability in production is Integration Suite at 95–108% consumption, but Ariba Catalog decline, Process Intelligence reversal, and dormant CPEA on FP&A all point to integration flows that were never built between Source-to-Pay, Process Mining, and Planning — Marathon has the integration runtime running but no enterprise integration blueprint pulling demand through it, which blocks the closed-loop 'mine-to-model-to-execute-to-measure' story across Signavio, S/4HANA, and Ariba.
Marathon is overspending on consumption-led AI and transactional capacity (Joule Studio 9x, HANA Cloud 13x, Ariba Buying 126%) while underspending on the upstream governance layers that should shape that demand (Preferred Success at 38%, Ariba Sourcing at 42%, Process Intelligence collapsing, Public BTP at 0%) — the architectural posture is 'run hot, govern cold,' which for a downstream refiner and midstream operator means scaling automation and AI without the sourcing discipline, process evidence, or release assurance needed to protect margin and operational reliability.
Aggregate contracted ACV is being eroded simultaneously by uncapped overage on HANA Cloud and Joule Studio (combined ~$308K projected against ~$37K budget) and stranded entitlement on Preferred Success, Ariba Sourcing, CPEA Public, and Tech Foundation Other (~$300K+ idle) — the net enterprise position is that Marathon is paying twice: once in overage for unmanaged consumption and once in shelfware for unactivated capability, with no contract vehicle aligning the two.
Convene a cross-LoB enterprise architecture review anchored on BTP as the shared platform across Data & AI, Finance, Procurement, and ERP, with the explicit deliverable of a single activation roadmap that lands Integration Suite, AI Core, and SAC against named use cases drawn from Ariba, Signavio, and S/4HANA.
Initiate a consolidated CPEA and HANA Cloud commercial true-up that simultaneously caps overage exposure on Joule Studio and Database Cloud and redirects dormant Public BTP, Tech Foundation, and FP&A CPEA entitlement toward sanctioned cross-domain workloads.
Establish a Source-to-Pay-to-Plan architectural workstream that re-links Ariba Sourcing → Catalog → Buying with Process Intelligence mining and SAC Planning forecasts, closing the governance loop that is currently allowing invoice volume and AI spend to scale without upstream control.
Reframe Preferred Success consumption as the architectural assurance layer for the entire SAP estate — not just S/4HANA — by scheduling Expert Sessions and CQCs against the highest-risk cross-domain integration points (BTP extensibility, Ariba-S/4 integration, Signavio-S/4 process alignment) before remaining advisory capacity is forfeited.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
1024.7%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$49K
YTD Budget$5K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $118K · Projected Budget: $12K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
Marathon's Data & AI footprint on BTP is architecturally bifurcated — the Database Cloud tenant is absorbing the entire workload (HANA Cloud, data services) at a 13x budget overrun while the Public tenant sits dormant, meaning extension, integration, and AI services that normally complement the data layer have no runtime home and the LoB is operating a data platform without the surrounding application and integration fabric.
The $118K projected Database Cloud burn against a $9K budget with zero ACV signals that Marathon has adopted BTP as a strategic data persistence layer for Data & AI workloads but is consuming it on a pay-as-you-go basis, exposing the LoB to uncapped overage risk on what is now mission-critical infrastructure.
The $0 Public tenant consumption against a $3,300 annual budget indicates the broader Data & AI value chain — AI Core, Build Apps, Integration Suite, SAC — is unprovisioned, so Marathon's growing data estate on HANA Cloud cannot yet be activated into analytics, AI inferencing, or business-facing applications.
Initiate a commercial true-up on LPR868 Database Cloud to convert the $118K run-rate into contracted ACV and eliminate uncapped overage exposure on the workloads already in production.
Run a BTP Public tenant activation workshop with Marathon's Data & AI architects to identify the first AI, integration, or analytics use case that consumes the dormant Public entitlement and connects to the active HANA Cloud data layer.
Conduct a workload inventory on the Database Cloud tenant to distinguish sanctioned production data services from unmanaged sprawl before sizing the renewal.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
167.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$179K
YTD Budget$107K
YTD ACV: $110K
Projected Consumed: $429K · Projected Budget: $264K · Projected ACV: $264K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation at Marathon is architecturally incoherent — CPEA is simultaneously massively over-consumed on Joule Studio ($79K against $8.7K YTD budget) and completely dormant on core BTP services, indicating the platform is being used as an unmanaged AI sandbox rather than a governed foundation for integration, extension, and data workloads.
Integration Suite is the only genuinely embedded capability in this foundation — steady 95–108% consumption proves iFlows and APIs are running production workloads, but the April ACV uplift to $25,775 has opened a $7,682 capacity gap that signals capacity was procured ahead of a pipeline that hasn't materialized.
The Joule Studio burn pattern (projected $190K against $28K annual ceiling) combined with declining month-over-month consumption suggests early agent experimentation hit the platform without sanctioned use cases or cost governance — Marathon is paying for AI consumption capacity faster than it is architecting AI outcomes.
CPEA capacity for general BTP and Integration Suite expansion is sitting idle ($3,016 headroom on Integration Suite CPEA, $1,832 fully dormant on Tech Foundation Other) while Joule Studio overruns 9x — the same contract vehicle is funding runaway AI spend and stranded platform entitlement, meaning the LoB has no unified BTP adoption plan.
Trigger a CPEA contract true-up and workload governance review on LPR868 to right-size the Joule Studio ceiling against the $190K projected run rate and redirect dormant Tech Foundation and Integration Suite CPEA headroom toward sanctioned BTP workloads.
Reconcile the April Integration Suite ACV uplift to a named pipeline of iFlows, API products, event mesh, or B2B trading partner scenarios to convert the $7,682 monthly capacity gap into measurable integration outcomes.
Establish a Joule Studio use-case board with Marathon's platform owner to classify current agent consumption as production, pilot, or sprawl, and gate further AI agent activation behind approved business outcomes tied to Integration Suite APIs.
Launch a BTP activation workshop targeting the dormant Technology Foundation CPEA envelope to land a first workload (extension, data service, or integration) and establish a baseline run rate before remaining contract value is forfeited.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
61.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$97K
YTD Budget$158K
YTD ACV: $181K
Projected Consumed: $234K · Projected Budget: $384K · Projected ACV: $434K
Solution Insights and Actions
Marathon's process transformation stack is architecturally bifurcated — Process Management (modeling/design) is accelerating into active use while Process Intelligence (mining/execution analytics) is decelerating and posted a May reversal, meaning the LoB is documenting target processes without the empirical execution data needed to validate or prioritize transformation.
The Signavio investment is shifting from data-driven discovery to design-led modeling — Process Management consumption grew 2.3x Jan–May with attainment hitting 164%, but Process Intelligence's $150K projected shortfall against a $374K budget signals the original mining use case has exhausted without a successor, leaving Marathon's transformation program design-heavy and evidence-light.
A capacity-versus-consumption mismatch is forming on Process Management — the May ACV jump to $9,222 against $1,222 actual usage indicates a center-of-excellence expansion was provisioned ahead of named-user activation, creating near-term shelfware risk on the very capability that is otherwise the healthiest signal in the portfolio.
Reconcile the May -$29,700 Process Intelligence entry with finance to confirm whether it is a credit memo, contract adjustment, or genuine usage collapse, and restate the YTD adoption position accordingly.
Launch a second-wave Process Intelligence use case in a high-volume Marathon domain such as procure-to-pay or order-to-cash mining to rebuild the analytical foundation that feeds the modeling work happening in Process Management.
Drive a named-user activation and enablement plan with the Process Management center-of-excellence owner to convert the $7,999 single-month ACV gap into published, governed process content before the expanded entitlement becomes shelfware.
Establish an architectural linkage between Process Intelligence findings and Process Management models so that mined inefficiencies directly seed prioritized to-be designs, closing the discovery-to-design loop the LoB currently lacks.
Solution Area
Cloud ERP Private
38.3%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$9K
YTD Budget$23K
YTD ACV: $27K
Projected Consumed: $21K · Projected Budget: $55K · Projected ACV: $65K
Solution Insights and Actions
Marathon's Preferred Success entitlement for S/4HANA Private Cloud is architecturally positioned as the advisory layer that de-risks the core ERP transformation — its declining consumption signals that the CoE is operating the private cloud platform without SAP's prescriptive guidance on release adoption, technical debt, and architectural decisions, exposing the broader S/4HANA investment to avoidable run-time risk.
The April consumption halving indicates Continuous Quality Checks, Expert Sessions, and Empowerment workshops are sitting dormant while S/4HANA is in active production — meaning Marathon is absorbing architectural and release-management decisions internally rather than leveraging the success services contracted to validate them.
At a $1,725 monthly run rate against a $4,532 budget pace, Marathon is tracking to consume only ~38% of the $54,709 annual entitlement, leaving $33,990 of advisory capacity unused and the S/4HANA roadmap under-supported on the very services designed to accelerate it.
Map unused Preferred Success entitlements directly to Marathon's active S/4HANA workstreams — release upgrades, custom code remediation, and integration architecture — so advisory consumption is pulled by real CoE demand rather than pushed as generic engagements.
Establish a quarterly Preferred Success governance cadence with Marathon's S/4HANA CoE lead to schedule Continuous Quality Checks and Expert Sessions against the private cloud release calendar, converting dormant entitlement into measurable architectural assurance.
Reframe the remaining $33,990 ACV as a structured recovery plan anchored on Marathon's highest-risk S/4HANA areas — performance, security, and custom development — to restore monthly consumption above the budget pace.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
84.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$433K
YTD Budget$513K
YTD ACV: $688K
Projected Consumed: $1.0M · Projected Budget: $1.2M · Projected ACV: $1.7M
Solution Insights and Actions
Marathon's Procure-to-Pay architecture is operationally inverted — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is over-consuming at 126% ($756K projected vs $601K budget) while Ariba Sourcing sits at 42% attainment and Ariba Catalog is in active decline, meaning transactional invoice processing is scaling without the upstream strategic sourcing and catalog governance that should be feeding it, exposing the LoB to maverick spend and uncontracted prices.
The Ariba Catalog disengagement signal ($5.3K declining to $3.4K monthly, tracking to 34% of annual budget) directly explains the Ariba Buying overrun — without curated catalog content guiding buyers to preferred suppliers and negotiated prices, transactions are flowing through invoice management as free-text or off-catalog purchases, inflating downstream AP automation volume while bypassing the compliance and savings layer the architecture was designed to enforce.
The Financial Planning & Analysis stack is half-built — SAP Analytics Cloud Planning is at a stable adoption ceiling (~103% attainment, $42.9K projected) while the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement sits at $0 consumption against $664 annual budget, meaning Marathon has operational planning models but no extension, integration, or data-services foundation to unify financial and operational planning across the enterprise.
Ariba Sourcing's flatline at ~$15.9K/month against a $37K/month plan indicates strategic sourcing is being executed for only a narrow slice of spend, which architecturally starves both Ariba Catalog (no negotiated content to publish) and Ariba Buying (no contracted prices to enforce) — the $262K projected sourcing shortfall is the upstream root cause of the downstream procurement imbalance.
Reconnect the Source-to-Pay chain by mandating that high-volume spend categories currently transacting through Ariba Buying be routed through Ariba Sourcing events and published into Ariba Catalog, converting the invoice-management overrun into governed, catalog-driven spend.
Initiate a true-up on Ariba Buying and Invoicing to right-size the contract ceiling against the $155K projected overrun, while pairing it with a catalog content audit to determine how much of that overrun is legitimate volume versus off-catalog leakage.
Convene a Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement activation workshop anchored on extending SAP Analytics Cloud Planning into adjacent domains (workforce, capex, driver-based forecasting), turning the dormant $664 entitlement into the integration fabric that unifies financial and operational planning.
Run a sourcing enablement sprint with Marathon's category managers to break the $15K/month Ariba Sourcing ceiling, prioritizing categories that will produce contracted prices feedable into Ariba Catalog and enforceable through Ariba Buying.
Medtronic, Inc.
Life sciences and healthcare
YTD Consumed: $2.1M
YTD Budget: $2.6M
YTD ACV: $3.2M
Projected Consumed: $4.9M
Projected Budget: $6.4M
Projected ACV: $7.7M
EA Insights and Actions
BTP is contracted as the connective fabric across Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance & Spend Management but sits at ~1% activation in every domain, meaning Medtronic is operating Ariba, Concur, and Commerce Cloud as isolated transactional silos with no shared AI, integration, or extensibility plane — blocking the closed-loop, agent-driven enterprise the contract was designed to enable.
Joule Studio is consuming at 150% while Integration Suite runtime contracts for three straight months and Watchlist Screening, Supplier Risk, and BTP data services remain at $0, creating an architecturally inverted stack where AI agents are being built faster than the governed APIs, data products, and compliance signals they need to act on — leaving agentic investments without trustworthy enterprise context.
Across every L1 domain, governance and enablement spend (Preferred Success Commerce +11%, Preferred Success Concur +19%, Joule Studio +50%) is outpacing the productive platforms they accelerate (Commerce Cloud at 3.3%, Ariba Sourcing at ~56%, Integration Suite declining), indicating Medtronic is funding adoption velocity on footprints that are not expanding — a portfolio imbalance that converts contracted ACV into stranded entitlement rather than business outcomes.
The sourcing-to-buying break in Ariba, the storefront-to-channel stall in Commerce Cloud, and the expense-to-card reconciliation collapse in Concur are three instances of the same architectural pattern — transactional endpoints running without their upstream master-data, catalog, and integration flows — which a healthy Integration Suite + BTP layer is specifically designed to close for a regulated medtech operating across global entities and channels.
Convene a single cross-domain BTP activation review with Medtronic's CIO, CFO, CPO, and digital commerce leadership to select 3–5 named workloads (supplier risk + screening integration, Commerce Cloud B2B catalog extension, Concur-to-AP reconciliation, Joule agent on sourced-agreement data) that seed BTP, Integration Suite, and Build simultaneously against the largest stranded ACV pools.
Commission an enterprise integration architecture review covering Integration Suite, Joule Studio, and SAP Build to reverse the three-month Integration Suite decline and align the API, event, and extension surface with the AI and commerce workloads currently starved of governed data.
Open a consolidated portfolio rebalancing conversation that pairs every over-consuming Preferred Success and AI tooling line against its under-consuming productive platform (Commerce Cloud, Ariba Sourcing, Concur Expense, BTP) so success spend is re-anchored to platform expansion milestones rather than flat entitlement drawdown.
Run a compliance-by-design workshop sequencing Watchlist Screening, Ariba Supplier Risk, and BTP integration into the active Ariba Buying and Concur flows, positioning the dormant control-plane products as the first BTP-integrated workloads serving Medtronic's medical-device regulatory and third-party-risk obligations.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
0.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$11
YTD Budget$3K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $26 · Projected Budget: $5K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation for Medtronic is architecturally contracted but operationally inert — SAP BTP is the designated platform for agentic AI, integration, and data services across both BDC Public and Database Cloud environments, yet near-zero consumption across both instances means Medtronic has no active runtime for AI-enabled extensions, business process integration, or analytics workloads it has already paid for.
The dual-environment BTP footprint (BDC Public + Database Cloud) signals an intent to run both application-extension and data-tier workloads, but with both tenants effectively dark, Medtronic is operating SAP applications without the contextual AI, custom extensibility, or cross-system integration fabric that BTP is meant to provide — leaving business logic locked inside core systems and unavailable to downstream Data & AI use cases.
With ~$3,985 in combined annual BTP budget tracking toward ~1% utilization, the risk is not under-consumption but a missing architectural anchor — no first workload has been identified to seed the platform, which means every dependent capability (AI agents, data products, integrations) remains theoretical rather than deployed.
Convene a joint BTP activation workshop with Medtronic's Data & AI and platform owners to select 2–3 named production workloads — prioritizing an AI extension, a data service on Database Cloud, and a cross-application integration — that establish the platform's architectural purpose before further budget erodes.
Stand up a BTP consumption and onboarding governance cadence covering both BDC Public and Database Cloud tenants to remove activation friction, assign workload owners, and convert the $3,909 unconsumed budget into a measurable ramp tied to specific Data & AI business outcomes.
Map Medtronic's existing SAP application landscape to candidate BTP extension and integration scenarios so the platform is positioned as the connective tissue for Data & AI rather than an isolated procurement line item.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
66.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$82K
YTD Budget$123K
YTD ACV: $159K
Projected Consumed: $197K · Projected Budget: $321K · Projected ACV: $380K
Solution Insights and Actions
Medtronic's Technology Foundation is architecturally fragmented across three BTP Enterprise Agreement allocations — Joule Studio is running 50% hot ($40,812 projected vs $27,170 budget) while Integration Suite and Technology Foundation Other allocations sit at $0, meaning the AI/development capability is racing ahead of the integration backbone it depends on to deliver context-aware business outcomes.
The Integration Suite contract carrying actual workloads (LPR499) is contracting for three straight months toward a $131,974 shortfall, signaling that existing iFlows and APIs are being retired faster than new ones are onboarded — this starves the agentic AI investments on BTP of the secure, governed data pipelines they require to operate on real business processes.
SAP Build adoption is project-burst rather than platform-sustained ($30→$127→$85) with 46% of contracted value projected unused, indicating the low-code extension layer is being treated as a one-off tool rather than the standing fabric meant to extend the core SAP processes that Integration Suite and BTP are designed to expose.
The combined pattern — accelerating Joule Studio consumption, dormant Integration Suite EA entitlement, declining Integration Suite runtime, and episodic Build usage — shows Medtronic is buying the Technology Foundation as discrete SKUs rather than operating it as a connected platform, which blocks the intended outcome of AI agents acting on integrated business data through extensible apps.
Rebaseline the Joule Studio BTP Enterprise Agreement budget to the $3K–$6K monthly run rate and conduct a service-level consumption review to confirm which AI/automation workloads are driving the 5x growth and whether they have the integration dependencies to scale.
Consolidate the three BTP Enterprise Agreement allocations under a single platform governance review with Medtronic to activate the $3,041 in dormant Integration Suite and Technology Foundation Other entitlements against the same workloads fueling Joule Studio growth.
Run an Integration Suite scenario discovery workshop targeting API Management, Trading Partner Management, and Event Mesh to reverse the three-month consumption decline and stand up the API surface that AI agents and Build apps need to consume.
Convert the April–May SAP Build consumption spike into a repeatable extension pattern by identifying the originating use case and scaling it to additional business units, anchoring Build as the standing extension layer on top of Integration Suite and BTP rather than a project tool.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
27.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$132K
YTD Budget$475K
YTD ACV: $890K
Projected Consumed: $318K · Projected Budget: $1.2M · Projected ACV: $2.1M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Commerce Cloud platform is architecturally deployed but functionally stranded at a single workload — $32,466 consumed against $985,098 ACV (3.3%) with flat April–May burn indicates Medtronic has activated the storefront engine but never extended it across the B2B/B2C/B2B2C channels, catalogs, or unified commerce experiences the platform is designed to orchestrate, leaving 92% of contracted digital commerce capacity dormant.
Preferred Success is consuming faster than the underlying platform it is meant to accelerate — a fixed $19,999 monthly burn projecting to $239,985 (11.3% over budget) while Commerce Cloud itself sits at 3.3% attainment signals success services are being drawn as a flat entitlement rather than tied to launch readiness, release guidance, or mission-critical events that would scale Commerce adoption.
The Commerce architecture is inverted: governance and enablement spend is outpacing the productive platform it supports, meaning Medtronic is paying for adoption acceleration on a footprint that is not expanding, which blocks the intended outcome of growing sales across every channel and realizing AI-driven commerce innovation.
Re-anchor Preferred Success Commerce consumption to specific Commerce Cloud expansion milestones — new storefronts, B2B catalog rollouts, or personalization releases — so the $19,999 monthly burn directly funds platform scale rather than flat entitlement drawdown.
Run a Commerce Cloud capability activation workshop with Medtronic's digital commerce leadership to map dormant capabilities (unified commerce, B2B catalogs, AI personalization, insights) to in-flight business priorities and recover material ACV utilization.
Reconcile the projected $24,327 Preferred Success over-consumption against Commerce Cloud's $952,632 underutilization in a single executive conversation, framing the imbalance as a portfolio rebalancing decision rather than two isolated commercial issues.
Diagnose the post-April Commerce Cloud consumption freeze to determine whether it reflects a technical blocker, organizational ownership gap, or roadmap pause, and build a quarterly ramp plan tied to measurable channel or storefront expansion.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
91.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$1.8M
YTD Budget$2.0M
YTD ACV: $2.1M
Projected Consumed: $4.4M · Projected Budget: $4.9M · Projected ACV: $5.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
Medtronic's Finance & Spend Management estate is architecturally broad but governance-shallow — transactional layers (Ariba Buying, Buying & Invoicing, Concur Expense/Travel/Drive/Reporting) are actively consumed while the control-plane products (Watchlist Screening at $0, Ariba Supplier Risk at $0, BTP at $0) are entirely dormant, meaning the LoB is executing spend at scale without the compliance, supplier risk, and extensibility guardrails the contract was designed to provide.
The sourcing-to-buying chain is broken in the middle — Ariba Buying and Buying & Invoicing are running near plan while Ariba Sourcing consumption is declining month-over-month toward ~56% of annual budget, indicating Medtronic is transacting against supply agreements without refreshing them through competitive sourcing events, which erodes negotiated savings over time.
Concur is the most mature pillar but is structurally over-contracted on engagement and under-contracted on capacity — Preferred Success Concur (+19%), Reporting, Triplink, Drive, ExpenseIt, and User Support Desk are all trending into overage while Concur Expense collapsed to 32% in May and Company Bill Statement sits at 12% YTD, signaling that T&E is operationally embedded for travelers and admins but the core expense submission and corporate card reconciliation workflows are either disrupted or never fully rolled out across entities.
BTP's zero consumption is the highest-leverage architectural gap — without an activated platform, the integration, AI, and extensibility fabric meant to connect Ariba, Concur, and downstream finance systems is absent, which explains why dormant products like Watchlist Screening and Supplier Risk have no clear path to embed into existing procurement and AP flows.
Convene a joint activation program for BTP, Watchlist Screening, and Ariba Supplier Risk with Medtronic's procurement, compliance, and platform owners, sequencing supplier onboarding into risk monitoring and sanctions screening as the first BTP-integrated workloads to recover the combined ~$6,500+ of stranded compliance entitlement.
Diagnose the May Concur Expense collapse and the chronic Company Bill Statement under-utilization as a single AP/T&E workflow issue, validating whether corporate card feeds, policy changes, or entity rollout gaps are suppressing submission volume across Medtronic's cardholder base.
Re-engage Medtronic procurement on Ariba Sourcing to reverse the three-month consumption decline by routing tail-spend RFx, auctions, and supplier discovery through the platform so that the buying volume already flowing through Ariba Buying is governed by current sourced agreements.
Open a consolidated true-up conversation covering Preferred Success Concur, Concur Reporting, Triplink, Drive, ExpenseIt, User Support Desk, and Connectors/Extract Services to right-size ceilings against demonstrated over-consumption before multiple ACV breaches force reactive contract actions.
Molson Coors Beverage Company
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $1.9M
YTD Budget: $2.2M
YTD ACV: $2.3M
Projected Consumed: $4.5M
Projected Budget: $5.3M
Projected ACV: $5.5M
EA Insights and Actions
BTP is the single architectural dependency underneath every L1 domain — Data & AI, Technology Foundation, Ariba Spend Management extensibility, and the eventual SuccessFactors skills-to-pay automation — and its 100% dormancy across roughly $5,465 of combined entitlement means Molson Coors has contracted for a clean-core, AI-ready enterprise but is operating a point-to-point, out-of-the-box estate that cannot evolve toward agentic AI or closed-loop process automation.
The absence of an active Integration Suite backbone is the structural reason Source-to-Pay runs as disconnected islands (Sourcing 71%, Contracts 55–66%, Buying 97%) and HCM talent modules plateau at 86–94% while Career/Comp languish at 67–68% — without a governed iPaaS fabric, Ariba contracts cannot enforce Buying compliance and SuccessFactors Performance cannot drive Compensation, forfeiting the cross-domain savings and pay-for-performance outcomes a CPG brewer depends on.
Across Spend Management and HCM, Preferred Success is the only line consistently at or above 120% consumption while every product module under-consumes, revealing an enterprise that is leaning on SAP-delivered services to operate the platform rather than building internal ownership — a structural fragility that, combined with $0 BTP activation, signals Molson Coors has no internal platform team driving demand for the contracted architecture.
The architectural pattern across all four L1 domains is identical — licensed breadth, shallow depth, no platform glue — meaning Molson Coors is paying for an integrated SAP enterprise but realizing the value of disconnected SaaS tools, which directly undermines the supply-chain visibility, procurement savings, and workforce productivity outcomes that justify the investment in a beverage industry running on thin margins.
Convene a single cross-domain Enterprise Architecture review with Molson Coors that treats BTP (Integration Suite, Joule Studio, Database Cloud) as the shared substrate for Ariba, SuccessFactors, and S/4, and sequences one anchor integration scenario per domain to activate the platform end-to-end rather than per-LoB.
Establish a named BTP platform owner inside Molson Coors with accountability for workload onboarding, consumption visibility, and an integration-and-AI use-case backlog spanning Source-to-Pay, Hire-to-Retire, and Data & AI, since the uniform $0 BTP pattern proves no internal demand pipeline exists today.
Launch a closed-loop process activation play that wires Ariba Contracts into Ariba Buying compliance and SuccessFactors Performance into Compensation via Integration Suite, directly attacking the projected six-figure shortfalls in Contracts, Spend Analysis, and Compensation with one integration investment.
Reframe the Preferred Success overconsumption across Ariba and SuccessFactors as a value-realization conversation that redirects expert capacity toward activating the dormant talent, contracts, and spend-analytics modules and builds Molson Coors' self-sufficiency on the platform.

Solution Insights and Actions
The entire BAIP Data & AI foundation for Molson Coors is architecturally contracted but operationally inert — both the Public Cloud and Database Cloud instances of the BTP Enterprise Agreement sit at $0 consumption against a combined $4,973 annual budget, meaning the LoB has no active substrate for agentic AI development, integration, or data services despite having paid for the platform.
The dual-environment BTP footprint (Public Cloud + Database Cloud) signals an intended architecture where application extensions and persistent data services would work together, but with zero activation on either side, Molson Coors has neither the runtime layer nor the data layer engaged — blocking any downstream AI, analytics, or integration use case the Data & AI LoB was meant to deliver.
With 7 months remaining to absorb 100% of a $4,973 commitment that was planned as a steady Sep–Dec ramp, the gap is no longer a consumption variance but an architectural non-starter — the original adoption plan assumed workloads that were never identified, scoped, or owned, leaving the LoB with a shelf-ware platform rather than a Data & AI capability.
Run a joint BTP activation workshop with Molson Coors Data & AI leadership to identify and commit to a first production workload spanning both Public Cloud (integration/extension) and Database Cloud (HANA/data service) to light up the contracted architecture end-to-end.
Rebuild the consumption ramp for the combined $4,973 BTP commitment with named use cases, technical owners, and a workload sequencing plan that pairs Database Cloud data services with Public Cloud application or AI scenarios so the two environments draw down in concert.
Escalate the dual zero-consumption pattern to the account executive as a single architectural issue rather than two line items, framing it as a stalled Data & AI foundation that puts the entire BAIP investment thesis at risk.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
—
YTD Budget Attainment
