YTD Budget$0
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $0 · Projected Budget: $10K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation layer is architecturally provisioned but functionally dormant — Molson Coors holds an active BTP Enterprise Agreement spanning Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and broader platform services, yet zero consumption across all three means the LoB has no agentic AI, no cross-system integration fabric, and no extension runtime in production, leaving every downstream SAP application to operate as an island.
Integration Suite dormancy is the most consequential gap in this stack — without it, business processes that span SAP and non-SAP systems (S/4, Ariba, SuccessFactors, third-party brewing/distribution platforms) have no governed iPaaS backbone, forcing Molson Coors into point-to-point integrations that erode the very ERP and LoB investments the BTP agreement was meant to connect.
Joule Studio at $0 consumption signals the AI roadmap is contractually purchased but architecturally unstarted — Molson Coors cannot build the grounded, context-aware AI agents that BTP is designed to deliver, meaning planned productivity and decision-support outcomes across Finance, Supply Chain, and HR remain theoretical rather than operational.
With the full annual entitlement unconsumed and the majority of the fiscal year remaining, the recovery math forces a steep back-half ramp that, without committed workloads, will convert platform investment into shelfware rather than capability.
Stand up an Integration Suite landing zone with a named first integration scenario (e.g., S/4 to non-SAP logistics or Ariba-to-ERP master data sync) to establish the integration backbone the rest of the SAP estate depends on.
Launch a Joule Studio pilot anchored to one high-frequency LoB process (Finance close assist, procurement requisition, or HR service request) to convert AI entitlement into a working agent and prove the BTP AI pattern.
Establish a BTP use-case backlog jointly owned by Molson Coors' platform team and SAP, sequencing Integration Suite, Joule Studio, and extension workloads against the unconsumed entitlement to convert contract into architecture.
Assign a BTP platform owner inside Molson Coors accountable for activation, consumption visibility, and workload onboarding, since the $0 pattern indicates no current internal demand pipeline for the foundation layer.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
82.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$1.1M
YTD Budget$1.3M
YTD ACV: $1.3M
Projected Consumed: $2.6M · Projected Budget: $3.2M · Projected ACV: $3.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Source-to-Pay architecture is fully licensed but operating as disconnected islands — Ariba Sourcing (71% attainment), Contracts (55-66%), Buying (97%), and Buying & Invoicing (70%) all show flat, capped usage patterns, meaning Molson Coors has the end-to-end procurement spine contracted but is running each stage as a standalone tool rather than a connected sourcing-to-settlement flow that would convert negotiated savings into compliant spend.
Contract governance is the weakest link in the procurement chain — Ariba Contracts' $29K YTD shortfall and 55-66% attainment against healthy Buying consumption indicates purchases are executing without contract backing, structurally undermining the savings and compliance outcomes that Sourcing and Spend Analysis are designed to deliver.
BTP's complete non-activation severs the extensibility and integration layer the Ariba estate depends on — with $0 consumed against the full $492 annual budget, Molson Coors has no platform foundation for AI-assisted procurement, custom workflows, or cross-system data flows, leaving the Finance & Spend Management portfolio unable to evolve beyond out-of-the-box transactional use.
Spend intelligence is decaying just as it should be compounding — Ariba Spend Analysis attainment fell from 97.8% in March to 75.2% in May with a projected $130,842 annual shortfall, meaning the analytical layer that feeds category strategy and sourcing pipeline is losing breadth precisely when Sourcing and Contracts need richer signal to expand beyond their flat baselines.
Convene a Source-to-Pay architecture review with Molson Coors procurement leadership to map sourcing events into contracts and contracts into catalog-enabled buying, converting the parallel under-utilization across Sourcing, Contracts, and Buying & Invoicing into a single connected flow.
Trigger a BTP Enterprise Agreement kickoff focused on Ariba-adjacent use cases — supplier data integration, invoice exception automation, and spend analytics extension — to activate the platform foundation that the rest of the Spend Management estate requires to mature.
Launch a contract-compliance enablement push tying Ariba Contracts authoring and clause libraries directly to Ariba Buying requisition policies, closing the $81K projected Contracts shortfall while raising the governance quality of the $45K/month Buying baseline.
Rebalance the Preferred Success Ariba overconsumption into targeted expert engagements on Spend Analysis category enrichment and Sourcing event expansion, redirecting the $76K overrun into the two products showing the steepest projected shortfalls.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
90.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$778K
YTD Budget$864K
YTD ACV: $959K
Projected Consumed: $1.9M · Projected Budget: $2.1M · Projected ACV: $2.3M
Solution Insights and Actions
Molson Coors' HCM suite is architecturally complete but operationally plateaued — Employee Central, Learning, Performance, Recruiting, and HXM Ops all sit in flat 86–94% attainment bands, indicating the core HR system of record is live but the talent modules built on top of it are not pulling new employee or manager activity through the connected data model.
The talent development value chain is the weakest link — SF Career and Talent Development (68.5% attainment) and SF Compensation (declining to 67.6%) are both under-consumed, meaning the skills-to-pay architecture that should connect performance data to growth and rewards is contracted but not embedded in manager routines, blocking the intended pay-for-performance and career-pathing outcomes.
Preferred Success is the only signal of genuine demand — consumption pinned at the 120% ceiling while the underlying SuccessFactors modules under-consume suggests Molson Coors is leaning on SAP support to operate the platform rather than self-sufficiently expanding adoption, a structural dependency that will worsen if module activation does not catch up.
Compensation is actively eroding while Employee Central holds flat — the inverse trend (consumption falling as budget rises) indicates the comp planning workflows are losing users mid-year, which breaks the architectural loop from Performance and Goals into merit and bonus execution and forfeits ~31% of contracted value.
Prioritize a Career Development and Compensation co-activation play that wires Performance and Goals outputs into talent profiles, development plans, and merit/bonus worksheets so the skills-to-pay chain operates as one connected workflow rather than three under-utilized modules.
Commission an Employee Central module utilization audit (position management, MDF objects, workflows, manager self-service) to expose which foundational capabilities are dormant, since every downstream talent module's plateau traces back to how deeply the core HRIS is being used.
Open a Preferred Success expansion and value-realization conversation that documents the services driving the 120% consumption ceiling and ties the uplift to a joint adoption plan for the under-consumed talent modules.
Run a manager enablement program across Performance and Goals, Recruiting, and Learning to convert the static licensed footprint into active goal, requisition, and learning-assignment volume, directly attacking the recurring monthly ACV gaps across the Learning and Talent cluster.
Mondelez Global LLC
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $1.9M
YTD Budget: $2.2M
YTD ACV: $2.3M
Projected Consumed: $4.5M
Projected Budget: $5.0M
Projected ACV: $5.5M
EA Insights and Actions
Mondelez's entire SAP estate is converging on a single broken dependency — BTP (Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement collapsed to ~$0, Integration Suite running 43% over plan at $181K YTD without complementary platform services) — meaning Integration Suite is moving data between systems but there is no governed runtime for the extensions, Joule agents, and data products that Data & AI, Finance, and Supply Chain all depend on, leaving Mondelez with point-to-point integration instead of a composable AI-ready enterprise.
The customer has paid for closed-loop architectures in three domains and operationalized none of them — Data & AI has SAC BI without BDC ($14,288 dormant), Business Transformation has Process Intelligence without Process Management (9.3% attainment, $517K of $662K unused), and Supply Chain has IBP without Integrated Product Development ($4,601 fully dormant) — so Mondelez can see, plan, and report but cannot model, design, or innovate against those signals, blocking the CPG-critical NPI-to-shelf and continuous-planning outcomes the contracts were sized for.
Across a roughly $5M+ contracted footprint, the production-grade workloads (IBP at 100% monthly attainment, Integration Suite at 124–156%, SAC BI at 93.5%) are all hitting capacity ceilings simultaneously while the architectural complements (BDC, IPD, Process Management, CPEA, Joule Studio) sit at 0–10% — Mondelez is over-running the legacy half of every domain and under-running the next-generation half, which means the renewal conversation is about rebalancing, not expansion.
Finance's flat $97K–$102K SAC Planning consumption and Supply Chain's flat $164K IBP burn are running as parallel silos with no SAC-IBP integrated business planning bridge active, meaning Mondelez has both halves of unified financial-and-operational planning contracted but is operating them as disconnected tools — undermining the integrated margin and supply decisioning that defines CPG performance.
Convene a single cross-domain BTP architecture review covering CPEA reactivation, Integration Suite capacity uplift, and Joule Studio re-scoping, treating the platform foundation as the prerequisite to unlocking dormant capacity in Data & AI, Transformation, and Supply Chain rather than as a standalone domain conversation.
Launch a closed-loop activation program pairing each dormant complement with its active counterpart — BDC to SAC BI, Process Management to Process Intelligence, and Integrated Product Development to IBP — with named business owners and a shared transformation roadmap to convert ~$540K+ of dormant ACV into measurable consumption.
Run an integrated planning workshop bridging SAC Planning and IBP through a shared driver model (volume, cost, margin) to break the Finance–Supply Chain silo and exercise the unified planning architecture both contracts were sized for.
Commission an enterprise ACV rebalancing review ahead of renewal to align contracted capacity with actual architectural posture — uplifting Integration Suite and IBP where ceilings are imminent, and converting unused CPEA, BDC, IPD, and Process Management entitlement into activation-linked commitments rather than passive shelfware.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
96.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$276K
YTD Budget$285K
YTD ACV: $322K
Projected Consumed: $663K · Projected Budget: $637K · Projected ACV: $774K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation at Mondelez is architecturally inverted — SAC BI (the visualization layer) is overconsuming toward a $41,880 annual overage while SAP Business Data Cloud (the underlying business data fabric meant to feed it) sits at 0% attainment with the full $14,288 unactivated, meaning the LoB is scaling dashboards on legacy data plumbing rather than the unified data product architecture they contracted for.
The agentic AI and mission-critical data product capabilities Mondelez paid for are entirely dormant — with BDC unprovisioned, there is no business data fabric anchoring Datasphere or trusted AI in SAC, so 'Joule for analytics and planning' and AI-grounded decisioning remain architecturally unreachable despite the BI investment.
The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement collapse from 165% attainment in Q1 to $0 in Apr–May, combined with BDC non-activation, signals a broken extension and data-integration backbone — without active platform services, the LoB has no runtime for the custom data pipelines or integrations that would connect source systems into BDC and onward into SAC.
SAC BI consumption is decelerating (93.5% avg in Apr–May) precisely when BDC should be amplifying it, indicating Mondelez is hitting the ceiling of standalone BI without the unified planning and data product layer — the LoB has reporting visibility but no path to the financial-and-operational planning convergence SAC is designed to deliver.
Convene a Data & AI architecture review with Mondelez to sequence BDC activation ahead of further SAC BI expansion, positioning the $14,288 BDC contract as the data fabric that grounds existing analytics rather than a parallel workstream.
Launch a BDC onboarding plan covering tenant provisioning, Datasphere source connectivity, and a first data product tied to an existing high-traffic SAC story to convert dormant capacity into measurable consumption and prove the unified analytics pattern.
Diagnose the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement Apr–May collapse with the technical owner and rebuild a workload pipeline aligned to BDC integration and SAC extension needs, restoring the platform runtime that the broader Data & AI stack depends on.
Right-size projected SAC BI overconsumption by auditing high-volume content creators and scheduled publications, redirecting demand toward planning and Joule-enabled scenarios that justify the ACV trajectory with forward-looking business value.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
108.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$201K
YTD Budget$185K
YTD ACV: $224K
Projected Consumed: $481K · Projected Budget: $403K · Projected ACV: $536K
Solution Insights and Actions
The integration backbone is carrying real production load while the broader platform foundation is hollow — Integration Suite is running 43% over plan ($181K vs $126K YTD) indicating active iFlows moving business data, but the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement that should host extensions, Joule Studio agents, and complementary BTP services has collapsed to near-zero consumption, meaning Mondelez is integrating systems without building the AI agents, extensions, or governed processes the foundation was contracted to enable.
Joule Studio adoption is effectively stalled — the Jan-Feb burn of $13,958 followed by zero-to-negative consumption signals a pilot or POC was decommissioned rather than scaled, leaving the LoB without the agent-building capability that Integration Suite's AI-ready APIs were architected to feed, breaking the intended 'integrate + orchestrate + agent' value chain.
Integration Suite is on a $434K annual trajectory against a $316K budget, putting active integration flows at risk of hitting the ACV ceiling and disrupting the very workloads that justify the Technology Foundation investment in the first place.
The Technology Foundation is operating as an integration-only layer rather than a composable AI platform — with only ~$47K of $87K CPEA likely to be consumed, roughly half the contracted platform capacity is dormant, signaling the LoB has not yet operationalized BTP for the extensibility and Joule agent scenarios it was sized for.
Engage Mondelez on an Integration Suite capacity uplift to cover the projected $117,846 overrun and protect production integration scenarios from ceiling exhaustion.
Run a workload discovery on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement to identify why Jan-Feb Joule Studio consumption stopped and rebuild a deployment path that connects agent scenarios to the active Integration Suite APIs.
Investigate the negative $467 May CPEA consumption to confirm whether any active workloads remain before scoping expansion or reallocation of the unused $67K balance.
Conduct an Integration Suite consumption review to map the iFlows and APIs driving 124–156% monthly attainment to business scenarios, and use that inventory to seed Joule Studio agent use cases on the dormant BTP capacity.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
20.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$60K
YTD Budget$291K
YTD ACV: $250K
Projected Consumed: $144K · Projected Budget: $662K · Projected ACV: $601K
Solution Insights and Actions
Mondelez's Business Transformation Management stack is architecturally broken in half — Process Management (the modeling/design layer where target processes are defined) is effectively abandoned at 9.3% attainment while Process Intelligence (the mining/analysis layer) showed real Q1 traction, meaning the LoB can see how processes actually run but has no governed to-be model to transform them against.
The May -$113,176 reversal on Process Intelligence wipes out the only active transformation capability Mondelez had, leaving the LoB with no live analytical foundation to drive process improvement despite a $585K annual commitment and a proven Q1 user base.
With Process Management dormant since February and Process Intelligence collapsing in Q2, the Signavio investment has degraded from a closed-loop transformation platform (model → measure → improve) into disconnected tooling, blocking Mondelez's ability to operationalize any process change initiative end-to-end.
Combined YTD consumption trajectory leaves roughly $517K of contracted transformation capacity unused against a ~$662K total budget, signaling that Business Transformation Management at Mondelez is contractually funded but operationally stalled.
Validate the May -$113,176 Process Intelligence reversal with billing and the account team to confirm whether it is a credit memo, license reallocation, or genuine usage dispute before shaping any renewal or adoption narrative.
Re-anchor Process Management adoption on one high-value end-to-end process such as Order-to-Cash or Procure-to-Pay, with named process owners publishing to the Collaboration Hub to restore the design layer.
Reconnect the Q1 Process Intelligence user community to live mining dashboards and explicitly link their findings to Process Management models, restoring the model-measure-improve loop the platform was purchased to deliver.
Run a joint enablement and license-activation audit across both Signavio products to confirm modelers and analysts are provisioned, trained, and operating against a shared transformation roadmap rather than in isolated pilots.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
86.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$499K
YTD Budget$578K
YTD ACV: $661K
Projected Consumed: $1.2M · Projected Budget: $1.3M · Projected ACV: $1.6M
Solution Insights and Actions
Mondelez has deployed SAC Planning as a standalone financial planning tool but is not leveraging its core architectural value — the unification of financial and operational planning — as evidenced by five months of flat $97K–$102K consumption, indicating the LoB has anchored on a single FP&A use case rather than extending into workforce, capex, or driver-based scenarios.
The capped consumption pattern signals that SAC Planning's AI and Joule-driven decision capabilities are architecturally available but operationally untapped, leaving Finance with a traditional planning tool footprint rather than the trusted-AI, context-rich planning environment the contract was sized for.
At 37.9% consumption through 42% of the year and a projected $120K ACV shortfall on a $1.32M contract, the run rate reflects a structural ceiling on active planner personas, not a ramp delay — the LoB lacks the modeler community needed to absorb the contracted capacity.
Commission a planning use-case expansion workshop with Finance to extend SAC Planning beyond core FP&A into workforce, capex, and driver-based models that exercise the unified planning architecture.
Run a persona and license utilization audit on SAC Planning to identify dormant planners and reactivate them as model contributors, addressing the recurring monthly ACV gap.
Enable Joule and AI-assisted planning features within SAC to shift the LoB from static budget refreshes toward continuous, AI-supported decision cycles that justify the contracted capacity.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
100.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$821K
YTD Budget$821K
YTD ACV: $821K
Projected Consumed: $2.0M · Projected Budget: $2.0M · Projected ACV: $2.0M
Solution Insights and Actions
Mondelez's Supply Chain architecture is anchored almost entirely on the Plan domain — IBP is running at production steady-state ($821,340 consumed YTD at exactly 100% monthly attainment) while the Make & Deliver side via Integrated Product Development sits at $0 consumed, meaning S&OP decisions are being made without an upstream feedback loop from new product introduction, cost, and design feasibility.
IBP's projected $1,971,217 burn against a $2,022,338 budget leaves only 2.5% headroom heading into the Nov/Dec planning peaks ($185K and $194K budget steps), signaling that the LoB's core demand, supply, and S&OP capability is one cycle away from hitting a hard capacity ceiling that will throttle planner throughput during the highest-stakes months of the year.
The Integrated Product Development contract is in a pre-launch posture with its entire $4,601 budget concentrated in December — architecturally this means the bridge between R&D, S/4HANA manufacturing, and procurement is contracted but not wired in, leaving NPI decisions disconnected from the supply chain feasibility signals IBP is generating.
Mondelez has the predictive planning half of the supply chain stack live but not the product innovation half — IBP is forecasting demand and optimizing supply response while Integrated Product Development sits dormant, so new product cost targets and design choices are not being validated against the same plan that drives supply commitments.
Open a capacity expansion conversation for IBP to lift the 2.5% annual headroom before the Nov/Dec planning peaks force throttling of demand sensing and supply response scenarios.
Validate with Mondelez planning leads which IBP modules (demand, supply, S&OP, inventory) are driving the flat $164,268 monthly burn to confirm whether constrained user groups or scenario limits are masking latent demand for additional entitlement.
Launch an enablement kickoff for Integrated Product Development with Mondelez R&D and supply chain stakeholders to activate the dormant NPI-to-supply bridge and connect product design decisions to the IBP planning process.
Rework the Integrated Product Development budget distribution away from the December-only allocation into a ratable monthly plan with defined adoption milestones tied to specific new product introduction use cases.
Primary Products Ingredients Americas
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $54K
YTD Budget: $69K
YTD ACV: $85K
Projected Consumed: $131K
Projected Budget: $168K
Projected ACV: $204K
EA Insights and Actions
The customer's entire enterprise architecture footprint for Ingredients Americas is concentrated in a single L1 domain — Finance and Spend Management, dominated by T&E — with no visible Supply Chain, Manufacturing, HR, or CX adoption, meaning a primary ingredients business is running SAP as a back-office travel utility rather than as a process backbone for its core ingredient sourcing, production, and distribution flows.
Concur Expense — the transactional spine of the only active L1 domain — has collapsed into negative consumption while every downstream T&E product accelerates, exposing a single-point-of-failure architecture where the customer's sole functioning SAP value stream is structurally inconsistent and at risk of an integration break that would unwind the entire YTD adoption story.
Document & Reporting Compliance is contracted as global statutory infrastructure but running at 5% attainment and ~$40/month, indicating the customer has paid for an enterprise-grade regulatory platform and is using it as a single-country tool, leaving the Americas ingredients business exposed to manual statutory and e-invoicing risk in every other jurisdiction it operates in.
Company Bill Statement's decline from 99.5% to 72.3% while Direct Travel and ExpenseIt accelerate reveals a broken card-to-expense-to-reimbursement integration chain inside the only mature domain, signaling that even within a narrow architectural footprint the customer is not maintaining end-to-end process coherence — a leading indicator for how broader cross-domain integration would fare if expanded.
Convene an enterprise architecture review to map Ingredients Americas' core ingredient sourcing, manufacturing, and distribution processes against the current SAP footprint and identify the L1 domains (Supply Chain, Manufacturing, Sustainability) required to move SAP from a T&E utility to an industry-aligned process backbone.
Trigger an urgent root-cause investigation on Concur Expense's negative ACV and consumption to determine whether a true-down, refund, or data-load failure occurred, and stabilize the submission engine before downstream audit, reporting, and ExpenseIt run rates lose their feeder source.
Run a Document & Reporting Compliance scope-expansion workshop with tax and finance leadership to activate additional country mandates and e-invoicing flows on the existing tenant, converting dormant contracted capacity into realized regulatory coverage and protecting committed ACV.
Commission an integration coherence assessment of the T&E card-to-expense chain (Company Bill Statement ↔ Direct Travel ↔ ExpenseIt ↔ Concur Expense) to restore closed-loop spend visibility and establish a reference pattern for cross-domain integration discipline as the footprint expands.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
78.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$54K
YTD Budget$69K
YTD ACV: $85K
Projected Consumed: $131K · Projected Budget: $168K · Projected ACV: $204K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Travel & Expense stack is the operational core of Finance and Spend Management adoption for Ingredients Americas — Direct Travel, ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, Reporting Services, User Support Desk, and Preferred Success are all trending at or above plan, indicating the LoB has successfully embedded the end-to-end T&E lifecycle from booking through audit and reporting.
Concur Expense — the transactional backbone the rest of the T&E architecture depends on — has collapsed to negative consumption while its downstream products (ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, Reporting Services) continue to grow, exposing a structural inconsistency where audit and receipt-capture volume cannot be sustained without the underlying expense submission engine running at full capacity.
Document & Reporting Compliance is architecturally positioned as the global statutory and e-invoicing backbone but is running at 5% attainment with flat ~$40/month consumption, meaning the LoB has the compliance platform contracted as enterprise infrastructure yet is operating it as a single-country, single-scenario utility while exposing the business to manual statutory risk elsewhere.
Company Bill Statement is the only T&E component on a declining trajectory (99.5% → 72.3%) while Direct Travel and ExpenseIt accelerate, indicating corporate card reconciliation is decoupling from the booking and capture flows it should be feeding — a break in the card-to-expense-to-reimbursement chain that erodes spend visibility.
Investigate the negative May ACV and consumption on Concur Expense to determine whether a license true-down, refund, or data-load failure occurred, and restore the submission baseline before downstream audit, reporting, and ExpenseIt volumes lose their feeder source.
Run a Document & Reporting Compliance scope discovery with the tax and finance teams to identify additional country mandates, e-invoicing flows, and statutory reports that can be onboarded into the existing tenant, converting dormant compliance capacity into active regulatory coverage.
Reconnect Company Bill Statement to the accelerating Direct Travel and ExpenseIt flows by enabling automated card-feed imports and expanding cardholder enrollment so corporate card spend reconciles into the same expense pipeline that downstream audit and reporting depend on.
Formalize the May run rates for Intelligent Audit, Reporting Services, User Support Desk, and Preferred Success Concur as the new operating baseline by replicating the use cases driving over-attainment across remaining finance and T&E stakeholders to lock in the upward adoption curve.
S.C. Johnson & Son, Inc.
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $600K
YTD Budget: $735K
YTD ACV: $1.0M
Projected Consumed: $1.4M
Projected Budget: $1.8M
Projected ACV: $2.4M
EA Insights and Actions
BTP is the common dormant denominator across every L1 domain — Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement sits at $0 in both Data & AI and Finance, BTP Enterprise Agreement pools are 0–4% consumed in Technology Foundation, meaning S.C. Johnson has bought a clean-core extensibility fabric but is operating without one, forcing Concur-to-S/4, Blackline-to-GL, BDC-to-app, and Business Network-to-IPD integrations into point-to-point patterns that will calcify as technical debt against the next S/4HANA upgrade.
The enterprise is over-investing in systems of insight and under-investing in systems of action — BDC is at 158% projected attainment ($112K vs $71K) while the platform layer that operationalizes it is at $0, and Integration Suite is decaying post-March with Joule Studio stalled, meaning S.C. Johnson is generating analytics and moving data but has no AI agent or extension surface to turn either into closed-loop business outcomes.
The CPG value chain is architecturally fragmenting at both ends — Integrated Product Development is dormant (5.7% YTD) upstream and Business Network collapsed 28% in May with a $129K shortfall downstream, leaving the S/4HANA core executing transactions without R&D innovation inputs or supplier demand signals, which directly undermines the formulation-velocity and supply-resiliency outcomes a household-products business depends on.
Finance is the only domain operating as a coherent end-to-end architecture (T&E suite + Blackline actively consumed in tight bands), but the May Concur Expense collapse to 21% combined with BTP dormancy means the one mature pillar has no platform safety net, so a system-of-record incident cascades unmitigated into Analytics, Reporting, Triplink, and downstream S/4 postings.
Convene a single enterprise BTP activation review spanning Data & AI, Finance, Technology Foundation, and Supply Chain to assign each dormant CPEA and BTP EA pool to a named cross-domain workload (BDC extension, Concur-to-S/4 posting, Blackline reconciliation automation, Business Network event mesh) so platform spend is drawn down through real architectural dependencies.
Initiate a BDC contract true-up paired with consumption governance to cover the projected $41,258 overage before mid-year stoppage halts the AI and analytics workloads that the rest of the enterprise increasingly depends on.
Run a CPG value-chain integration workshop that re-anchors Integrated Product Development as the mandatory NPI path and reconnects its outputs to Business Network supplier collaboration cycles, closing the upstream-to-downstream loop around the S/4HANA core.
Treat the May Concur Expense drop as a system-of-record incident with AP/Finance and use the Preferred Success entitlement to stabilize the T&E backbone before submission decay erodes Analytics, Reporting, and policy-compliance visibility across the full travel suite.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
189.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$47K
YTD Budget$25K
YTD ACV: $154K
Projected Consumed: $112K · Projected Budget: $73K · Projected ACV: $369K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation is split between a runaway success and a stalled platform — SAP Business Data Cloud is delivering the business data fabric, analytics, and AI workloads the LoB clearly needs (May at 276% of plan, YTD already consuming 66% of annual budget in 5 months), while the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement that should host the extension, integration, and database services around it sits at $0 consumed, meaning BDC insights cannot be operationalized into custom apps or governed data pipelines.
BDC adoption is architecturally outpacing its contractual envelope — projected annual consumption of $112,282 against a $71,024 budget (158% of plan, $41,258 overage) signals that mission-critical data products, Datasphere, and SAC are becoming the LoB's analytical backbone, but without a true-up the accelerating burn rate risks a mid-year stoppage that would halt the very AI and analytics workloads driving the value.
The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is a five-month cold start across both BDC Public and Database Cloud metrics ($0 against $1,345 combined YTD budget), indicating a structural provisioning or activation blocker rather than slow adoption, which leaves the LoB without the platform layer needed to extend BDC outputs into operational business processes.
Initiate a contract true-up on SAP Business Data Cloud to expand the capacity ceiling ahead of the projected $41,258 overage, paired with a workload review to confirm the 276% May attainment is tied to high-value AI and analytics use cases and to install consumption governance.
Escalate the dual $0 consumption pattern on Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (BDC Public and Database Cloud) to the account executive and assign a technical adoption resource to diagnose whether the blocker is provisioning, entitlement, or use-case definition.
Run a CPEA activation workshop anchored to BDC adjacencies — identifying 2–3 extension, integration, or database workloads that consume BDC data products — so the platform is drawn down through real architectural dependencies rather than a year-end spike.
Establish a unified Data & AI consumption review covering both BDC and CPEA so the LoB sees the analytics layer and the platform layer as one architecture, preventing the current pattern of over-investment in insights and under-investment in the platform that operationalizes them.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
70.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$19K
YTD Budget$26K
YTD ACV: $21K
Projected Consumed: $45K · Projected Budget: $84K · Projected ACV: $51K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation is contracted as an integration-and-extension platform but is operating as integration-only — SAP Integration Suite carries a flat ~$3,500/month baseline while both BTP Enterprise Agreement pools (Joule Studio at 4.3% and Technology Foundation Other at 0%) are dormant, meaning S.C. Johnson is moving data between systems but has no active extension, AI, or custom app layer to act on it.
Integration Suite itself is being consumed as a one-time project rather than a governed API backbone — the March spike to 181% followed by April–May decay and a $1,800 ACV shortfall indicates a single iFlow build-out completed, with no recurring B2B, event mesh, or AI-agent API patterns embedded into business processes as the architecture intends.
The Joule Studio footprint on BTP shows an aborted January workload ($77) followed by five months of near-zero consumption, signaling that the AI agent-building capability that would extend Integration Suite's AI-ready APIs into business outcomes has stalled before reaching a production use case.
With ~$35,500 of BTP Enterprise Agreement capacity and the full $505 Technology Foundation Other pool tracking to forfeiture, the LoB is paying for a composable extension platform but consuming only its integration pipes — leaving extension, side-by-side development, and AI scenarios architecturally unavailable to the business.
Investigate the January Joule Studio workload to recover the original AI agent use case and rebuild a production adoption path that pairs Joule Studio with the existing Integration Suite APIs.
Convert Integration Suite from project-based usage to steady-state adoption by onboarding recurring B2B, API management, and event mesh scenarios that reverse the April–May decay and absorb the $1,800 ACV gap.
Run a BTP Enterprise Agreement activation workshop with S.C. Johnson's technical owners to identify at least two extension or side-by-side workloads that consume the dormant $35,500 Integration Suite-linked pool and the $505 Technology Foundation Other pool.
Establish a Technology Foundation adoption baseline that explicitly couples each new Integration Suite iFlow with a downstream extension, Joule agent, or analytics consumer so integration spend translates into a business-facing capability.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
90.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$300K
YTD Budget$333K
YTD ACV: $427K
Projected Consumed: $721K · Projected Budget: $810K · Projected ACV: $1.0M
Solution Insights and Actions
The T&E architecture is the most mature pillar of S.C. Johnson's Finance & Spend Management estate — Concur Expense, ExpenseIt, Triplink, Direct Travel, Analytics, and Reporting Services are all actively consumed in tight bands, meaning the end-to-end traveler journey (book → capture → submit → analyze → report) is operationally embedded rather than partially deployed.
Concur Expense's May collapse to 21% attainment breaks the T&E backbone — because Expense is the system of record that feeds ExpenseIt receipts, Triplink bookings, Analytics, and Reporting Services downstream, a sustained submission drop will hollow out spend visibility and policy compliance across the entire Travel and Expense architecture, not just one product.
The Record-to-Report capability is contracted but under-scoped — Blackline is automating reconciliations for an existing account population but the $47K projected annual gap shows the S/4HANA-integrated substantiation footprint hasn't expanded to new entities or auto-certification rules, leaving close acceleration value on the table.
BTP is architecturally dormant — Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement at $0 consumption means S.C. Johnson has no active extension, integration, or automation fabric underneath Concur and Blackline, forcing any cross-product orchestration (e.g., Concur-to-S/4 expense posting, Blackline-to-GL reconciliation enrichment) to rely on point integrations rather than a governed platform layer.
Treat the May Concur Expense drop as a system-of-record incident — diagnose with AP/Finance whether SSO, approval routing, or mobile submission broke, because the downstream Analytics, Reporting, and Triplink value depends on Expense remaining the canonical capture point.
Run a Blackline scope-expansion workshop with controllership to onboard additional entities and auto-certification rules, converting unused automation capacity into measurable close-cycle compression rather than letting the $47K under-burn surface at renewal.
Activate the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement against a concrete Concur or Blackline integration use case (e.g., Integration Suite for expense-to-GL posting or Build Process Automation for reconciliation exceptions) so BTP becomes the connective tissue the Finance LoB architecture currently lacks.
Convene a Preferred Success Concur value-realization review to redirect unused expert sessions and adoption assessments toward the Expense re-engagement effort and Analytics depth gap, turning the success entitlement into the lever that lifts the broader T&E suite toward full attainment.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
66.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$234K
YTD Budget$351K
YTD ACV: $417K
Projected Consumed: $562K · Projected Budget: $846K · Projected ACV: $1.0M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Supply Chain Collaboration backbone is architecturally live but losing momentum — the Business Network is the synchronization layer between S.C. Johnson's S/4HANA orders and supplier commitments, and a 28% May drop with a projected $129K annual shortfall signals that trading partner document flows are contracting, eroding the forecast and PO collaboration visibility the LoB depends on for supply resiliency.
Product innovation is architecturally connected but operationally abandoned — SAP Integrated Product Development is the bridge that aligns R&D designs with manufacturing feasibility and cost targets, and zero consumption in April–May at 5.7% YTD attainment means new product introductions are happening outside the integrated PLM-to-S/4HANA flow, breaking the design-to-supply-chain handshake for a CPG business whose growth depends on formulation velocity.
The two supply chain pillars are decoupling in parallel — collaboration with suppliers downstream and product development upstream are both dormant, leaving the LoB with an S/4HANA execution core that lacks both forward demand signals from partners and innovation pipeline inputs from R&D, which together undermine end-to-end supply chain orchestration.
Reactivate the Business Network trading partner flows by auditing which supplier transaction types (forecast, PO, inventory) collapsed in May and re-onboarding dormant partners to restore demand-supply synchronization with S/4HANA and IBP.
Convene R&D, formulation, and supply chain leadership to re-anchor SAP Integrated Product Development as the mandatory NPI path, targeting Enterprise Product Development and Product Lifecycle Costing use cases so design decisions flow into manufacturing and procurement.
Establish a joint architectural review between the Make & Deliver and Business Network domains to confirm that NPI outputs from Integrated Product Development are triggering supplier collaboration cycles on the Business Network, closing the upstream-to-downstream loop.
Stepan Company
Chemicals
YTD Consumed: $506K
YTD Budget: $672K
YTD ACV: $650K
Projected Consumed: $1.2M
Projected Budget: $1.7M
Projected ACV: $1.6M
EA Insights and Actions
Stepan has contracted a full SAP suite spanning HCM, Procurement, T&E, Data & AI, and BTP — roughly $6,138 in Data & AI, ~$44K in Integration Suite, $725K in Ariba Buying, plus the full SuccessFactors stack — but the connective tissue (BTP Integration Suite declining -17%, Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreements at $0 across both Data & AI and Finance) is collapsing, meaning every domain is running as an island and the enterprise-wide closed-loop process automation the investment was scoped to deliver is structurally impossible.
The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is the single dormant dependency that simultaneously blocks Data & AI activation (BDC cannot host data products without it), Finance compliance extension (Document & Reporting Compliance has no extension substrate), and BTP-led innovation across Ariba/Concur/SuccessFactors — one unactivated SKU is gating three L1 domains' forward roadmap and the customer's entire clean-core extensibility posture.
Stepan's transactional systems (Ariba Buying, SuccessFactors EC, EC Payroll) are processing volume while their upstream governance layers (Ariba Catalog at $97 of $20K, SF Onboarding at 41% attainment, Performance-to-Compensation chain all declining together) are disengaging in parallel — indicating an enterprise-wide pattern where systems of record are being used as data capture tools rather than as integrated process backbones, eroding the master data quality that any future AI or analytics layer would depend on.
Joule Studio sits at ~$580/month against a $13,478 ceiling while BDC, Datasphere, and SAC show zero consumption — Stepan has paid for both the AI runtime and the data fabric AI requires, but neither is live, meaning the customer's agentic AI ambition has no foundation and the FY26 AI investment narrative is at full renewal risk.
Convene a single cross-domain enterprise architecture review that treats the dormant Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreements (Data & AI and Finance) as one activation program, sequencing Database Cloud and BDC Public stand-up as the prerequisite for BDC, Datasphere, SAC, Document & Reporting Compliance extensions, and Joule Studio pilots.
Appoint a single enterprise integration owner accountable for Integration Suite roadmap across Ariba, Concur, SuccessFactors, and S/4, and decompose the declining ~$44K Integration Suite spend into a use-case-led plan that rebuilds the broken handoffs (Onboarding→EC, Sourcing→Catalog→Buying, Performance→Compensation).
Run a master data and process-integrity diagnostic spanning Ariba Catalog, SF Onboarding, and EC Payroll to identify why governance layers are being bypassed while transactional layers continue to run, since this pattern will compound technical debt and starve any downstream Data & AI use case.
Reframe the FY26 renewal conversation around an enterprise activation scorecard tying the $6,138 Data & AI envelope, the $7.9K BTP shortfall, the $20K Ariba Catalog gap, and the SF Onboarding 59% unused value into one cross-domain value realization plan rather than four separate domain renewals.

BAIP - Data & AI

YTD Consumed$0
YTD Budget$3K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $0 · Projected Budget: $9K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
Stepan's entire BAIP Data & AI footprint is architecturally contracted but operationally dark — both SAP Business Data Cloud and the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (BDC Public and Database Cloud) show flat-zero consumption, meaning the LoB has no active data fabric, no compute substrate, and no analytics surface despite having paid for the full stack.
The dormancy is structurally self-reinforcing — Business Data Cloud depends on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement's Database Cloud and BDC Public capacity to host data products and run compute, so the zero activation on LPR868 is the upstream blocker preventing any Datasphere or SAC use case from ever materializing on LPR1156.
Without BDC live, Stepan has no business data fabric to anchor agentic AI or mission-critical data products, leaving the LoB unable to deliver the actionable insights and reliable AI outcomes the Data & AI investment was scoped to enable — this is a pre-adoption failure, not a consumption shortfall, with the full ~$6,138 annual envelope at risk across 7 remaining months.
Sequence activation by first standing up the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (Database Cloud and BDC Public) with a named technical owner at Stepan, since BDC cannot onboard data products without the underlying compute and database substrate live.
Run a joint Data & AI activation workshop that scopes a single end-to-end use case spanning Datasphere ingestion, Database Cloud persistence, and SAC consumption to convert all three dormant SKUs into a working reference pattern.
Assign a single enablement owner accountable for the full BAIP Data & AI stack at Stepan to prevent further siloed procurement-to-activation gaps and to govern the catch-up ramp against the unconsumed FY26 budget.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
81.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$23K
YTD Budget$28K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $56K · Projected Budget: $69K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
Stepan's Technology Foundation is anchored almost entirely on Integration Suite (~$44K projected) with Joule Studio and other BTP services contributing less than 20% combined — the platform is functioning as an integration backbone rather than an innovation foundation, leaving AI, extension, and app development capabilities architecturally available but operationally dormant.
All three Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement workstreams show flat or declining consumption (Integration Suite -17%, Joule Studio flatlined at ~$580/mo, Other -17%), indicating the BTP estate has reached a steady-state ceiling with no new workloads being activated — the LoB is consuming what was built, not building what's next.
Joule Studio consumption at ~$580/month against a $13,478 ceiling signals the AI/copilot extensibility layer is funded but functionally idle, meaning Stepan is paying for generative AI development capacity while business processes continue to run without AI-assisted automation or Joule-driven user experiences.
The combined $7.9K projected annual shortfall across the three BTP streams reflects an architectural under-utilization rather than a budgeting error — roughly half the platform capacity Stepan contracted for is unconsumed, putting the renewal narrative at risk unless new capabilities are activated.
Stand up a BTP capability activation plan that moves Joule Studio from idle to a defined pilot (e.g., Joule-enabled S/4 user assistant or agent for a high-volume LoB process) to justify the $6,515 of unconsumed Joule Studio budget.
Conduct an Integration Suite service-level decomposition to identify which iPaaS capabilities (Cloud Integration, API Management, Event Mesh, Open Connectors) are decaying and rebuild the integration roadmap around net-new B2B, event-driven, or API-monetization use cases.
Audit the Technology Foundation Other subaccounts to confirm which BTP services (Build Apps, AI Core, Data Intelligence, Workzone) are provisioned but unused, and launch a targeted enablement sprint to activate at least one extension or AI workload before renewal.
Reframe the BTP commercial conversation with Stepan around use-case-led consumption planning rather than entitlement renewal, tying H2 activation milestones directly to the $5,333 Integration Suite and $1,382 Other projected gaps.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
69.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$292K
YTD Budget$421K
YTD ACV: $396K
Projected Consumed: $700K · Projected Budget: $1.1M · Projected ACV: $950K
Solution Insights and Actions
Stepan's procurement stack is architecturally complete but operationally collapsing — Ariba Buying & Invoicing, Sourcing, and Catalog are all contracted yet all three are declining or flatlined, meaning source-to-pay governance is being bypassed and the $725K Buying contract is processing spend without upstream sourcing discipline or catalog-enforced compliance.
The Ariba Catalog dormancy ($97 of $20K YTD) is the architectural root cause of Buying & Invoicing's decline — without hosted/punch-out content guiding buyers to negotiated prices, requisitions are flowing off-catalog and eroding the three-way-match value that Buying & Invoicing was designed to enforce.
Concur is the one healthy domain architecturally, with Analytics, ExpenseIt, Reporting, Support Desk, and Integration Services all pacing above budget — T&E is operating as a fully embedded closed-loop (capture → audit → report → support), though Concur Expense's May collapse to 27.9% and Intelligent Audit's 54% volatility signal the submission funnel feeding that loop is breaking down.
The compliance and planning layers are effectively missing — Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement sits at $0 consumption and Document & Reporting Compliance only went live in April, leaving Stepan without the statutory reporting backbone and extension platform that should underpin both the Ariba and Concur investments globally.
Treat Ariba Catalog activation as the unlock for the entire procurement domain by onboarding top indirect-spend suppliers with hosted and punch-out content to restore guided buying flows into Ariba Buying & Invoicing and reverse the three-month consumption slide.
Run a source-to-pay process diagnostic with Stepan's procurement leadership to determine why Sourcing events and Buying volume are simultaneously declining, and re-anchor the architectural linkage between sourcing awards, contract catalogs, and requisition execution.
Trigger a Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement activation workshop anchored to a concrete first workload — most logically extending Document & Reporting Compliance country coverage or building Concur/Ariba integration extensions — to convert shelfware into a functioning extension and compliance backbone.
Investigate the Concur Expense submission collapse and Intelligent Audit volatility together with Stepan's AP/T&E owner, since both signals point to a single upstream issue in expense report submission volume that, if unresolved, will starve the otherwise healthy Concur analytics and audit layers.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
87.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$191K
YTD Budget$220K
YTD ACV: $255K
Projected Consumed: $460K · Projected Budget: $533K · Projected ACV: $611K
Solution Insights and Actions
Stepan's SuccessFactors suite is architecturally complete but operationally regressing — Employee Central, Learning, Performance & Goals, Compensation, and Career Development all show month-over-month consumption declines, meaning the integrated talent fabric that depends on EC as the system of record is quietly disengaging in lockstep rather than failing in one module.
The hire-to-productive-employee journey is broken at the entry point — SF Recruiting is flat-lined at a fixed requisition base and SF Onboarding is tracking to leave 59% of contracted value unused, so new hires are bypassing the integrated onboarding flow that should hand off cleanly to Employee Central and Learning.
Payroll is architecturally consolidated but functionally under-scoped — Employee Central Payroll has held in a tight 68–70% attainment band for five months against a healthier (though declining) EC core, indicating parallel legacy payroll systems are still running for entire employee populations and blocking the single-HCM-cloud value proposition.
The skills-to-rewards loop is collapsing — Performance & Goals (76% attainment), Career & Talent Development (49.5%), and Compensation (74.3%) are all declining together, severing the architectural chain where performance data should feed skills profiles, development plans, and merit decisions, leaving Stepan with disconnected point usage instead of integrated talent management.
Run a payroll population and country-scope audit on Employee Central Payroll to identify the employee groups still on legacy payroll and build a migration plan to close the structural ~$2,100/month gap.
Diagnose why SF Onboarding is being bypassed by new hires and re-anchor it as the mandatory system of record feeding Employee Central, since recruiting throughput is static and onboarding capture is shrinking.
Launch an integrated talent cycle re-engagement program that links Performance & Goals reviews to Career Development worksheets and Compensation planning, treating the synchronized decline as one architectural disengagement rather than four separate module issues.
Convert the 18.2% over-consumption on Preferred Success SFSF into a value realization review that targets the declining modules (EC, Learning, Performance, Career Development) with guided playbooks and release readiness sessions before renewal.
Target Corporation
Retail
YTD Consumed: $2.6M
YTD Budget: $2.4M
YTD ACV: $3.1M
Projected Consumed: $6.2M
Projected Budget: $5.8M
Projected ACV: $7.5M
EA Insights and Actions
Target's entire SAP estate is consuming the transactional and integration layers heavily (Ariba Buying at 111%, Concur stack at 101–116%, Integration Suite at 128.5%) while the governance, planning, and intelligence layers above them — BDC, SAC Planning, Ariba Sourcing/Contracts, Joule Studio, SAP Build, and Multi Bank Connect — are dormant or decaying, meaning Target is scaling SAP as a system of record without the system of insight and control that converts volume into margin, compliance, and forward-looking decisions.
The dormancy of BTP's extensibility and AI surface (SAP Build at 17%, Joule Studio declining, CPEA for Integration Suite at $0) directly explains why downstream domains can't progress — BDC has no agentic consumption pattern to justify activation, SAC BI is decaying without new data-product-driven content, and Ariba/Concur overages have no low-code governance layer to channel them into managed processes, so a single platform gap is simultaneously capping Data & AI, Finance, and Procurement outcomes.
Target is systematically over-consuming Preferred Success entitlements across Ariba (118%), Concur (118%), and SCM (113%) while the underlying solution adoption in those same domains is either uneven (procurement upstream gaps), under-contracted (T&E), or invisible (no active SCM execution products surfaced), indicating the customer is buying advisory capacity to compensate for architectural incompleteness rather than to accelerate a coherent roadmap — a pattern that inflates run cost without producing transformation velocity.
Across a portfolio carrying ~$36K Data & AI YTD shortfall, a $575K projected Ariba Buying overage, ~$74K Integration Suite ceiling pressure, and ~$13K SCM Preferred Success overrun against dormant adjacent capacity, Target's contract shape is structurally misaligned to actual consumption — overspending on transactional vehicles while leaving strategic platform budget (BDC $27,840, CPEA Integration Suite $32,058, CPEA Joule, Multi Bank Connect) unconsumed, which means a contract rebalance is now a prerequisite to any architectural recovery.
Convene a cross-LoB Enterprise Architecture review that treats BDC, CPEA Integration Suite, SAP Build, and Joule Studio as a single activation bundle and ties each to a named downstream use case in Data & AI, Finance, or Supply Chain, so platform activation is justified by domain outcomes rather than pursued in isolation.
Run a portfolio contract rebalancing exercise that redirects unconsumed BDC, CPEA, Multi Bank Connect, and dormant Ariba Sourcing/Contracts budget toward the demonstrated overconsumption in Integration Suite, Ariba Buying, and Concur, aligning ACV shape to actual architectural center of gravity.
Establish a Source-to-Pay-to-Plan closed-loop architecture workshop spanning Ariba Sourcing/Contracts, SAC Planning, Multi Bank Connect, and BDC to reconnect upstream governance, planning, and treasury layers to the transactional spend already flowing through Buying, Invoicing, and Concur.
Reframe Preferred Success consumption across Ariba, Concur, and SCM into outcome-linked scorecards tied to specific adoption milestones (BDC data products live, Sourcing events executed, SCM execution modules in flight) so advisory burn is anchored to architectural progress rather than fixed monthly drawdown.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
87.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$60K
YTD Budget$69K
YTD ACV: $97K
Projected Consumed: $145K · Projected Budget: $173K · Projected ACV: $232K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation for Target is architecturally inverted — SAP Business Data Cloud, which is supposed to anchor the business data fabric and feed mission-critical data products into SAC, sits at $0 consumption while SAC BI runs on top of it, meaning analytics are being delivered without the unified semantic layer BDC was contracted to provide.
The stalled BDC activation breaks the intended path to agentic AI and trusted, context-rich analytics — without Datasphere and BDC data products live, SAC BI is operating as a standalone visualization tool rather than the planning-and-AI surface the LoB invested in, capping decision velocity at whatever siloed sources it can reach today.
SAC BI adoption is decaying from the top of the funnel — the 14-point attainment slide from 94.4% to 80.3% and widening monthly gap (now $2,767) indicate the initial dashboard footprint has saturated and no new business-context use cases are landing, which is exactly the demand signal BDC was meant to unlock through governed data products.
Target is carrying a combined $36,460 YTD shortfall across the Data & AI stack against a contracted trajectory that assumed BDC would be the growth engine for SAC consumption, so every month of BDC dormancy structurally caps SAC's recovery ceiling regardless of enablement effort.
Treat BDC activation as the gating dependency for the entire Data & AI LoB outcome and sequence a first Datasphere-to-SAC data product use case that materially expands the analytics surface rather than relighting BDC in isolation.
Re-baseline the BDC ramp by redistributing the $27,840 unconsumed budget against a named high-volume source system onboarding, tied to a SAC story that consumes the new data product end-to-end.
Pair the SAC BI power-user enablement push with net-new content built on BDC-published data products, so reversing the attainment decline simultaneously proves out the business data fabric pattern.
Identify the business units behind the April–May SAC dip and qualify whether their stalled use cases are blocked by missing governed data — if so, escalate them as the pilot demand for BDC activation.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
76.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$65K
YTD Budget$86K
YTD ACV: $380K
Projected Consumed: $157K · Projected Budget: $253K · Projected ACV: $911K
Solution Insights and Actions
Target's BTP foundation is architecturally inverted — Integration Suite is overconsuming at 128.5% YTD ($64,431 vs $50,160 budget) while the parallel Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement for Integration Suite sits at $0 against $32,058 YTD budget, meaning integration workloads are running on a single license vehicle while the enterprise agreement meant to absorb scale remains unactivated.
The low-code and AI extensibility layer is dormant relative to integration — SAP Build is on track for 17% attainment and CPEA for Joule Studio is declining toward 41% attainment, so Target has built the API and integration backbone (Integration Suite) without the application and agent development surface needed to turn those integrations into business-facing outcomes.
Joule Studio adoption is actively regressing — CPEA consumption fell from $116 to $102 even as budget expanded to $247/month, signaling that the AI agent and Joule extension capability has no anchor use case and the foundation for agentic SAP experiences is stalling before it scales.
SAP Build's 3.3x consumption climb is real but immaterial against a $6,465 annual plan, indicating early developer experimentation rather than an operationalized app delivery practice that can extend SAP processes at enterprise scope.
Reconcile the Integration Suite license architecture by activating the CPEA for Integration Suite as the consumption vehicle and migrating the overage from the standalone Integration Suite contract before it breaches the $74,803 ACV ceiling.
Convene a BTP foundation activation workshop covering CPEA Integration Suite, Joule Studio, and SAP Build to define 2–3 anchor workloads that connect Target's active integrations to Joule agents and Build apps.
Stand up an SAP Build Center of Excellence with citizen and pro-code intake governance to convert the May uplift into a committed app backlog that extends the Integration Suite APIs already in production.
Launch a Joule Studio use-case discovery tied to Target's original CPEA business case to reverse the five-month consumption decline and establish AI agent patterns on top of the Integration Suite API estate.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
109.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$2.4M
YTD Budget$2.2M
YTD ACV: $2.6M
Projected Consumed: $5.8M · Projected Budget: $5.2M · Projected ACV: $6.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
Target's Procure-to-Pay architecture is operationally lopsided — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is running 11.4% over plan ($575K projected overage) while Ariba Contracts sits at 67% attainment and Ariba Sourcing is flat-lined at a single $466 recurring event, meaning Target is transacting heavily on purchases and invoices without the upstream sourcing and contract governance that would convert that spend into negotiated savings and compliance.
The Travel & Expense stack is the most architecturally coherent domain in the LoB — Concur Expense, ExpenseIt, Drive, Direct Travel, Analytics, Company Bill Statement, Connectors, Preferred Success, and WalkMe are all consuming 101–116% of budget in lockstep, indicating an end-to-end T&E process (capture → submit → reconcile → analyze → guide) that is fully embedded but structurally under-contracted across every component.
The Financial Planning and Treasury layers are the weakest links in the Finance architecture — SAC Planning is stuck on a fixed set of recurring models ($28K projected shortfall) and Multi Bank Connect is effectively dormant with a May reversal and 36% projected attainment, leaving Target without forward-looking planning depth or the unified bank connectivity hub that would close the loop between S/4HANA treasury and downstream payment execution.
Preferred Success Ariba and Preferred Success Concur are both running 118%+ against plan, signaling that Target is consuming advisory and enablement capacity faster than contracted — a leading indicator that the procurement and T&E platforms are expanding in scope and the success envelope is undersized to sustain that trajectory.
Re-sequence the procurement architecture by launching a Sourcing-to-Contract activation play that routes Ariba Buying volume through Ariba Sourcing events and Ariba Contracts authoring, converting the $575K Buying overage into governed, contract-backed spend rather than uncontrolled transaction growth.
Initiate a coordinated T&E true-up across Concur Expense, Analytics, Direct Travel, Drive, ExpenseIt, Company Bill Statement, Connectors, Preferred Success Concur, and WalkMe to right-size ACV to the demonstrated 105–125% consumption pattern before ceiling breaches disrupt traveler and expense workflows.
Stand up a Financial Planning expansion workstream that activates SAC Planning use cases beyond the current recurring models — workforce, OpEx, and driver-based forecasting — to close the $28K projected gap and connect planning to the operational spend visibility already present in Concur Analytics.
Run a Multi Bank Connect activation review with Target's treasury team to diagnose why contracted bank connections are not live, and either accelerate onboarding to realize the $12.5K consumption gap or formally reset scope so S/4HANA treasury has a defined path to multi-bank payment and statement flows.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
114.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$48K
YTD Budget$42K
YTD ACV: $48K
Projected Consumed: $115K · Projected Budget: $102K · Projected ACV: $115K
Solution Insights and Actions
Preferred Success SCM is the only active SCM investment signal for Target's supply chain LoB, which means the architecture is currently buying advisory entitlement without evidence of underlying SCM execution products (IBP, EWM, TM, Ariba SCM) consuming that guidance — the success layer exists above an unseen or dormant operational stack.
The flat $9,576/month burn against a rising $8,333–$8,443 budget shows entitlement is being consumed as a fixed subscription rather than pulled by adoption events (go-lives, release uptake, expert sessions), indicating Preferred Success is functioning as a standing cost rather than an accelerator tied to SCM capability milestones.
Projected $114,917 annual consumption against a $101,886 budget (12.8% overrun) with declining attainment from 114.9% to 113.4% signals that the LoB is structurally over-entitled for its current SCM adoption depth — Target is paying for a guided-success motion that has no measurable downstream SCM workstream to guide.
Map the $9,576 monthly Preferred Success burn to specific SCM products and workstreams (planning, logistics, warehousing, sourcing) to confirm whether the entitlement is supporting active solution adoption or sitting orphaned from any live SCM initiative.
Rebalance the remaining $60,247 of planned Preferred Success budget toward targeted Expert Sessions, Release Guidance, and Success Checks aligned to a named SCM adoption priority rather than continuing fixed monthly drawdown.
Engage the SCM LoB to surface which operational SAP SCM capabilities (IBP, EWM, TM, Ariba) are in scope or in flight, so Preferred Success consumption can be re-anchored to concrete business outcomes rather than passive entitlement burn.
Establish a Preferred Success SCM value scorecard tracking deliverables consumed, SCM adoption KPIs influenced, and business outcomes realized to convert the overrun signal into a justified investment narrative or a right-sizing decision.
Tate and Lyle Americas LLC
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $109K
YTD Budget: $135K
YTD ACV: $173K
Projected Consumed: $261K
Projected Budget: $330K
Projected ACV: $416K
EA Insights and Actions
Tate and Lyle Americas' SAP footprint is narrowly anchored in Concur-led Travel and Expense with no visible activation of Document & Reporting Compliance, leaving the enterprise architecture without a statutory close-the-loop layer and exposing the Americas record-to-report chain to e-invoicing and VAT compliance risk as jurisdictions tighten mandates.
The Concur Connectors/Extract layer running in March-style batch spikes rather than continuous feeds is the single integration substrate underpinning both downstream Reporting Services and a future Document & Reporting Compliance activation — its batch posture means every analytics and compliance ambition inherits period-end latency rather than real-time finance visibility.
The contract shape itself is architecturally inverted — Company Bill Statement is breaching ACV in 5 of 5 months (projected $7,078 vs $5,689 plan) while Direct Travel underruns by $5,040, meaning Tate and Lyle is paying for policy-first travel architecture but operating a card-first capture model that structurally bypasses pre-trip compliance controls.
With Travel and Expense as the only materially consumed L1 area and no evidence of BTP, Integration Suite, or broader S/4 domain activation, the customer's SAP estate functions as a point solution rather than an enterprise platform, capping the achievable business outcome at expense efficiency rather than integrated finance transformation.
Convene a cross-domain Finance architecture review to scope Document & Reporting Compliance activation against Americas statutory obligations and wire it into the existing Concur Invoice and Expense data flows.
Rebaseline the Concur Company Bill Statement entitlement to the $7,078 run-rate and reallocate the $5,040 Direct Travel underrun by running a booking-channel leakage audit to restore policy-first travel capture.
Convert the Concur Connectors and Extract Services from period-end batch to scheduled continuous feeds so the integration substrate can support both Reporting Services and a future compliance reporting layer.
Run an enterprise architecture roadmap session to position BTP and Integration Suite as the connective tissue beyond Concur, opening the path from isolated T&E consumption to an integrated finance and compliance platform.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
80.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$109K
YTD Budget$135K
YTD ACV: $173K
Projected Consumed: $261K · Projected Budget: $330K · Projected ACV: $416K
Solution Insights and Actions
Travel and Expense is the operational backbone of Finance and Spend Management at Tate and Lyle Americas — Concur Company Bill Statement, ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, and Reporting Services are all live and routine — but the architecture is missing its compliance bookend: Document & Reporting Compliance is effectively shelfware (0.4% YTD attainment), meaning expenses and invoices flow through Concur without the statutory e-invoicing and VAT reporting layer that closes the record-to-report loop.
The expense capture and audit stack is functionally complete but consumption-imbalanced — Intelligent Audit and Company Bill Statement are at or above plan while Direct Travel (84%), ExpenseIt (85%), and Reporting Services (84%) trail, indicating spend is being audited and reconciled after the fact but upstream traveler enablement, mobile receipt capture, and analytics consumption are lagging the contracted capacity.
Concur Company Bill Statement hitting the ACV ceiling in 5 of 5 months while Direct Travel underruns by a projected $5,040 reveals an architectural inversion — corporate card-driven AP volume is outpacing managed travel bookings, meaning expense is being captured downstream of policy enforcement rather than steered through the booking channel where compliance is designed to start.
The Connectors/Web Services/Extract layer (LPR963) shows March-spike volatility against an otherwise steady cadence, signaling that Concur-to-S/4 integration is operating in period-end batch mode rather than as the continuous data backbone needed to feed both Document & Reporting Compliance and downstream finance reporting.
Activate Document & Reporting Compliance by mapping Tate and Lyle's Americas statutory obligations (e-invoicing, SAF-T, periodic VAT) to a first reporting scenario and connecting it to the existing Concur Invoice/Expense data flows so the compliance layer stops being shelfware.
Rebaseline and expand the Concur Company Bill Statement contract to reflect the projected $7,078 annual consumption against the $5,689 plan, eliminating the recurring ACV ceiling breach in routine AP operations.
Run a Concur Direct Travel booking-channel compliance audit to identify offline bookings bypassing the tool and route them back through Direct Travel, closing the $5,040 projected annual underrun and restoring the policy-first travel architecture.
Smooth the Concur Connectors and Extract Services cadence by converting March-style batch runs into scheduled continuous feeds, lifting attainment above 90% and creating a reliable integration substrate for both Reporting Services and Document & Reporting Compliance.
Tenneco Automotive
Automotive
YTD Consumed: $596K
YTD Budget: $780K
YTD ACV: $966K
Projected Consumed: $1.4M
Projected Budget: $1.8M
Projected ACV: $2.3M
EA Insights and Actions
Tenneco has contracted a full platform spine — CPEA across Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance plus standalone Integration Suite and Joule Studio — yet every platform entitlement shows zero consumption, meaning the architectural control plane that should govern integration, AI, analytics, and finance extensibility across all LoBs is universally dormant and every domain-level gap (IBP plateau, C4C isolation, SuccessFactors decay) is being amplified by the absence of the connective tissue meant to unify them.
The dormant Integration Suite is the single point of failure breaking three concurrent cross-domain stories — C4C-to-ERP quote-to-order, IBP-to-S/4 supply response, and Recruiting-to-Onboarding-to-EC hire-to-productive — meaning Tenneco's automotive aftermarket, planning agility, and talent pipeline outcomes are all blocked by the same unactivated middleware rather than by domain-specific failures.
Across the portfolio, every functional domain (HCM declining, IBP plateaued at $1.8K/month with a $12K projected unconsumed gap, C4C unmeasured, Finance and Data & AI dark on CPEA) shows the same pattern of contracted capability outpacing operationalization, indicating Tenneco's enterprise architecture posture is procurement-led rather than outcome-led and the FY26 renewal cycle will surface significant shelfware risk unless activation is centrally governed.
HCM is the only domain with rich consumption telemetry and it shows a system-of-record (Employee Central) decaying while Preferred Success burns at 105% — a leading indicator that even where Tenneco has live SAP adoption, success investment is not being directed at the integration and skills-data flows that would connect HCM to the broader enterprise talent and workforce planning narrative.
Convene an enterprise architecture review with Tenneco's CIO and LoB heads to consolidate the duplicated Integration Suite entitlements (CPEA + LPR499) and the dormant CPEAs under Data & AI and Finance into a single platform activation roadmap tied to named cross-domain use cases (supplier EDI, plant-to-ERP telemetry, C4C-to-S/4, IBP-to-S/4).
Launch an integration-first activation program that pairs each Joule Studio agent and each domain expansion (IBP module, C4C extension, SF Onboarding handoff) with a governed Integration Suite API package, preventing further point-to-point shadow integration as adoption scales.
Instrument consumption telemetry and ownership across every dark entitlement (BDC Public, Database Cloud, Integration Suite, Joule Studio, FP&A CPEA, C4C) to establish a unified enterprise consumption baseline before any FY26 renewal or expansion conversation.
Redirect Preferred Success and CSM capacity away from HCM-only cadence toward a cross-domain activation play targeting the Integration Suite, IBP planning-area expansion, and C4C-to-ERP integration that collectively unblock Tenneco's automotive aftermarket, supply resilience, and talent outcomes.

BAIP - Data & AI
