YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $0 · Projected Budget: $71 · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation for Tenneco Automotive rests on a single Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement spanning both BDC Public and Database Cloud, indicating the LoB has procured the runtime and persistence layer but no specialised analytics, integration, or AI services on top — meaning the architectural scaffolding exists without the value-generating capabilities that justify it.
With zero contract insights or consumption telemetry on either LPR868 entitlement, the Data & AI estate is effectively dark — there is no evidence the LoB is operationalising data products, AI models, or governed datasets, which blocks any downstream manufacturing analytics, supplier intelligence, or predictive quality use cases the platform was presumably bought to enable.
The dual presence of BDC Public and Database Cloud under one enterprise agreement suggests an intended pattern of business data fabric over a managed database tier, but without adoption signals on either, the architectural dependency chain (data ingestion → persistence → consumption) cannot be validated as functioning end-to-end.
Instrument both LPR868 entitlements with consumption and workload telemetry to establish whether the BDC Public and Database Cloud tenants are provisioned-but-idle or actively hosting data products before any expansion conversation.
Engage Tenneco Data & AI stakeholders to map the intended use cases (manufacturing analytics, supplier data, AI/ML scenarios) against the contracted platform and identify which SAP Data & AI services need to be layered on to convert the platform investment into LoB outcomes.
Validate that the Database Cloud entitlement is architecturally aligned with the BDC Public workloads — if they are serving disconnected purposes, the LoB is paying for two foundations rather than one integrated data platform.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
37.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$4K
YTD Budget$10K
YTD ACV: $10K
Projected Consumed: $9K · Projected Budget: $24K · Projected ACV: $23K
Solution Insights and Actions
Tenneco Automotive has contracted the full Technology Foundation stack — Integration Suite, Joule Studio, and the broader CPEA envelope — positioning the LoB for AI-ready, API-governed integration, but the absence of any consumption signal across all four entitlements indicates the foundation is architecturally provisioned yet operationally dormant.
Joule Studio sits on top of Integration Suite by design — agents need governed APIs to act on SAP data — so the parallel non-adoption of both products means Tenneco has no live path from AI agent intent to ERP execution, blocking the very automation use cases the CPEA was sized for.
The duplicated Integration Suite entitlement (under both CPEA and standalone LPR499) suggests overlapping commercial commitments without a corresponding integration backlog, indicating the LoB has not yet defined the process boundaries (S/4 to non-SAP, B2B, event-driven) that Integration Suite is meant to govern.
With CPEA credits committed against Technology Foundation Other but no directed consumption, the LoB risks treating the platform as a generic cloud budget rather than an architectural control plane, eroding the governance and AI-acceleration value the suite is designed to deliver.
Stand up a foundational integration use case on SAP Integration Suite — prioritizing a high-volume Tenneco interface such as supplier EDI or plant-to-ERP telemetry — to convert dormant entitlement into measurable runtime and prove the 50% cost-reduction thesis.
Pair every Joule Studio agent design with a corresponding Integration Suite API package so AI agents have governed, reusable endpoints rather than point-to-point access, preventing shadow integration as agent adoption scales.
Reconcile the overlapping Integration Suite entitlements under CPEA and LPR499 into a single consumption plan tied to a named integration roadmap, eliminating commercial duplication and creating accountability for activation.
Establish a Technology Foundation governance forum for Tenneco that tracks CPEA credit burn against architectural outcomes — APIs published, agents deployed, processes integrated — rather than against generic cloud spend.

Projected Consumed: $0 · Projected Budget: $13K · Projected ACV: $26K
Solution Insights and Actions
Customer Experience for Tenneco rests on a single pillar — SAP Cloud for Customer — meaning the LoB's sales and service execution is architecturally contracted, but with no surrounding CX products (Commerce, Marketing, CDC, CDP) the front office operates as an isolated CRM rather than an integrated customer engagement platform.
The absence of contract insights for C4C signals an adoption blind spot: without visibility into seat utilization, sales pipeline activity, or service ticket throughput, it is impossible to confirm whether Tenneco's field sales and aftermarket service teams are actually driving opportunity-to-order and case-to-resolution cycles through the platform.
With no customer data platform or identity product adjacent to C4C, account and contact data in Cloud for Customer is likely siloed from ERP and aftermarket channels, blocking the unified customer view that automotive OEM and aftermarket sales motions depend on.
Establish consumption telemetry for Cloud for Customer — active users, opportunities created, service tickets processed — to validate whether the LoB is realizing sales and service value or merely holding licenses.
Assess integration between C4C and Tenneco's ERP backbone to ensure quote-to-order and installed-base service data flows bidirectionally, since standalone CRM adoption rarely delivers automotive aftermarket outcomes.
Evaluate the case for extending the CX footprint with a customer data and marketing capability to convert C4C from a transactional sales tool into a full customer engagement architecture.

Solution Insights and Actions
The Finance and Spend Management footprint for Tenneco Automotive is architecturally thin — only a Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is contracted under FP&A, with no dedicated planning, close, or spend products visible, meaning the LoB has platform entitlement but no functional finance capability deployed on top of it.
The CPEA is a consumption-based credit pool intended to activate BTP services (planning extensions, integrations, analytics), and with no contract insights or consumption signal it is operating as latent capacity rather than a funded enabler of finance transformation.
Without Group Reporting, SAC Planning, or Ariba in the signal set, core FP&A outcomes — driver-based planning, consolidation, and spend visibility — are not being delivered by SAP for this LoB, regardless of any ERP financial posting layer underneath.
Validate whether the CPEA is funding any active BTP services for Finance (planning, integration, analytics) and reallocate or draw down the credits against a defined FP&A use case before renewal.
Conduct a finance capability gap assessment to determine whether SAC Planning, Group Reporting, or Ariba should be introduced to convert the platform entitlement into LoB outcomes.
Establish a consumption baseline and ownership for the CPEA within the Finance LoB so platform spend is tied to a measurable finance capability rather than sitting dormant.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
77.7%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$583K
YTD Budget$750K
YTD ACV: $927K
Projected Consumed: $1.4M · Projected Budget: $1.7M · Projected ACV: $2.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
Tenneco's HCM suite is architecturally complete but operationally hollowing out — Employee Central as the system of record is declining alongside every dependent talent module (Performance, Compensation, Career Development, Learning), meaning the integrated talent-to-pay value chain is degrading from the core outward rather than at the edges.
Onboarding is the broken entry point of the hire-to-productive journey — SF Onboarding sits at 12.4% attainment while Recruiting Management runs at a flat $11.4K/month plateau, so new hires sourced through Recruiting are not flowing into the Onboarding-to-EC handoff that should seed downstream talent, learning, and performance data.
The skills-driven talent architecture is effectively dormant — Career and Talent Development at ~$1K/month against a $74K ceiling means Learning completions and Performance outcomes are not being converted into skills profiles, career paths, or succession pipelines, collapsing the strategic talent layer into a transactional HRIS.
Preferred Success is over-consuming while the products it is meant to enable are under-consuming — 105% attainment on advisory entitlements against declining adoption across EC, Performance, Compensation, and Learning indicates success services are being burned without translating into measurable product engagement lift.
Commission an Employee Central core-HR diagnostic to isolate which foundational workflows (self-service, position management, workflows) are decaying and re-anchor the dependent talent modules to a healthy system of record before pursuing module-level adoption sprints.
Rebuild the Recruiting-to-Onboarding-to-EC handoff by routing all new-hire populations through SF Onboarding and activating cross-boarding, internal transition, and offboarding workflows to convert the $900/month footprint into the intended hire-to-productive pipeline.
Redirect Preferred Success SFSF entitlements away from generic cadence and toward targeted activation of Career Development, Performance, and Compensation, using EarlyWatch and expert sessions to diagnose the five-month consumption decline across the talent stack.
Launch a skills-data activation play that connects SF Learning completions and SF Performance outcomes into SF Career and Talent Development to operationalize the dormant succession, mentoring, and career pathing capabilities Tenneco has already contracted.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
65.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$9K
YTD Budget$14K
YTD ACV: $18K
Projected Consumed: $22K · Projected Budget: $34K · Projected ACV: $43K
Solution Insights and Actions
IBP is architecturally positioned as Tenneco's unified planning backbone spanning demand sensing, inventory optimization, S&OP, and supply response — but the frozen $1,826/month consumption plateau indicates only a narrow slice of planning areas is live, meaning the LoB is running point-solution planning rather than the integrated end-to-end process the platform was contracted to deliver.
The flat consumption against a rising $33,908 annual budget (64.1% May attainment, $4,752 cumulative gap, ~$12,281 projected unconsumed) signals that high-value modules like Demand Sensing, Inventory Optimization, and Control Tower are likely dormant, leaving Tenneco without AI-driven forecasting and multilevel supply response — the very capabilities that justify IBP over legacy APO-style planning.
With collaborative S&OP under-activated, commercial and supply chain functions at Tenneco remain architecturally disconnected from a single planning version of truth, blocking the cross-functional scenario modeling needed to respond to automotive demand volatility and tier-supplier disruptions.
Conduct an IBP planning-area activation audit to map which modules (Demand, Supply, S&OP, Inventory, Control Tower) are live versus dormant and rebuild a phased activation roadmap targeting the $4,752 YTD gap.
Launch a planner enablement and scenario-planning expansion program to break the $1,826/month plateau by onboarding additional user personas across demand, supply, and inventory workflows.
Prioritize activation of AI-powered demand forecasting and multilevel supply planning to unlock the differentiated IBP capabilities Tenneco is paying for but not consuming.
Establish a unified S&OP cadence with Tenneco's commercial and operations leaders to drive collaborative planning adoption and convert IBP from a forecasting tool into the integrated decision platform it is architected to be.
The Lubrizol Corporation
Chemicals
YTD Consumed: $935K
YTD Budget: $1.0M
YTD ACV: $1.3M
Projected Consumed: $2.2M
Projected Budget: $2.5M
Projected ACV: $3.1M
EA Insights and Actions
Lubrizol has activated the Integration Suite backbone (now running at $14K/month) but left the semantic and extensibility layers dormant — Datasphere at 13.8% YTD and SAP Build flat at $680/month — meaning the enterprise can move data between systems but cannot govern its meaning or turn it into business-facing applications, structurally blocking the AI-ready, clean-core S/4 destination across every L1 domain.
The customer's transactional spine is running hot while every upstream governance layer is decaying in parallel — Ariba Buying/Invoicing, Concur, and Business Network are in overrun while Ariba Sourcing (-43% MoM), Ariba Catalog (5.8%), Signavio Process Intelligence, and SF Onboarding are dormant — indicating Lubrizol is executing transactions at scale without the negotiated agreements, mined processes, or governed handoffs that protect savings, compliance, and quality outcomes.
Across HCM, CX, Supply Chain, and Business Transformation, Lubrizol has contracted full suites but is consuming only the single anchor module in each (Employee Central, Commerce Cloud, Business Network Commerce Automation, Process Management seats) — a consistent 'hub-without-spokes' pattern that leaves ~$370K+ of annual contracted capacity (notably ~$171K in CX alone) unmonetized and prevents any L1 domain from delivering its end-to-end value proposition.
SAC BI is consuming at 94% on top of a 13.8% Datasphere foundation while Joule Studio is overshooting 5.4x without FinOps guardrails — the AI and analytics narrative is being scaled on an ungoverned semantic base, meaning every insight, agent, and Joule scenario Lubrizol stands up will inherit trust and compliance risk by construction.
Convene a cross-domain enterprise architecture review that maps each L1 dormant capability (Datasphere, SAP Build, Signavio Intelligence, Ariba Catalog, SF Onboarding, CX adjacents) to the integration backbone already in production, and sequence activation so Integration Suite traffic compounds into governed business outcomes rather than ungoverned point flows.
Run a consolidated commercial true-up and rebaseline across the overrun stack (BTP EA, Integration Suite, Joule Studio, full Concur estate, Document & Reporting Compliance) against the underrun stack (Datasphere, SAP Build, Signavio, CX, Onboarding/Compensation/Career Development) to realign ~$370K+ of misallocated capacity with where demand is actually landing.
Stand up a clean-core and FinOps governance forum that places consumption ceilings, tagging, and workload attribution on BTP, Integration Suite, and Joule Studio before agentic AI scales, and mandates Datasphere as the semantic source for all new SAC BI and AI content.
Launch an integration-chain restoration program targeting the three broken cross-domain handoffs — Recruiting→Onboarding→Employee Central, Sourcing→Contracts→Buying→Catalog, and Process Intelligence→Process Management — to convert provisioned suites into the closed-loop processes Lubrizol contracted for.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
89.7%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$53K
YTD Budget$59K
YTD ACV: $66K
Projected Consumed: $128K · Projected Budget: $166K · Projected ACV: $159K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation at Lubrizol is architecturally inverted — SAC BI is consuming at ~94% of plan while Datasphere, the semantic layer that is supposed to feed it with trusted, business-context-preserving data, sits at 13.8% YTD attainment, meaning analytics are being delivered without the unified data fabric underneath and likely rely on direct or legacy sources that bypass the governed knowledge graph.
Datasphere adoption reflects a single steady-state workload ($1,044–$1,218/month) rather than the multi-domain data product platform it is designed to be, leaving federated integration, semantic modeling, and AI-ready data capabilities effectively dormant and putting the H2 budget ramp to $6,275+/month at serious risk of non-realization.
The sustained ~11% step-down in SAC BI consumption from April onward signals a discrete loss of analytics workload (retired content, offboarded users, or a broken refresh) rather than seasonal drift, which directly erodes the decision-making surface the LoB depends on and projects an $11,758 annual shortfall against the $126,088 plan.
Because SAC BI sits on a thin Datasphere layer, the 'analytics with full business context' and Joule/AI value propositions are structurally unattainable today — Lubrizol is consuming visualization capacity without the semantic backbone that makes those insights trustworthy and AI-ready.
Diagnose the April SAC BI step-down by auditing active users, scheduled stories, and data refresh jobs against the March baseline, and either restore the lost workload or onboard a replacement use case (e.g., finance or planning dashboard) sized to close the $11,758 projected annual gap.
Expand Datasphere beyond its single steady-state workload by onboarding S/4 replication flows and additional source systems into governed analytic models, repointing existing SAC BI content to consume from Datasphere so the semantic layer becomes the system of record for analytics.
Validate the H2 Datasphere ramp to $6,275–$6,614/month with Lubrizol's data team by committing named use cases (knowledge graph expansion, AI-ready data products, cross-domain modeling) — otherwise rebaseline the budget to reflect realistic adoption.
Establish Datasphere as the mandated data foundation for new SAC BI content so that future analytics expansion compounds adoption across both products rather than deepening the current architectural imbalance.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
177.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$108K
YTD Budget$61K
YTD ACV: $90K
Projected Consumed: $259K · Projected Budget: $177K · Projected ACV: $216K
Solution Insights and Actions
Lubrizol's integration backbone is the load-bearing architecture of this foundation — SAP Integration Suite went from dormant to full production in April ($752 to $13,989/month) while the underlying BTP Enterprise Agreement is simultaneously running 210% hot, confirming a major integration workload has gone live and is now the dominant consumer of platform capacity supporting AI-ready APIs and cross-system process orchestration.
The low-code extensibility layer is architecturally provisioned but functionally idle — SAP Build's flat $680/month plateau against a $23.9K annual envelope indicates a single steady-state workflow rather than a citizen-developer program, so Lubrizol owns the integration plumbing but is not building the apps, workflows, and process automations that would let the LoB actually consume those integrated services.
The Joule Studio footprint on BTP is structurally undersized at 5.4x overshoot on a sub-$2K budget, signaling early agentic/AI experimentation is happening without a consumption ceiling or FinOps guardrails — small in dollars today but architecturally significant as the entry point for AI agents that will depend on the same Integration Suite APIs already running hot.
Taken together, Lubrizol has invested in the 'integrate and automate' half of the BTP foundation but underinvested in the 'build and extend' half — the LoB can move data and expose APIs at scale but lacks the application surface area on SAP Build to turn those flows into user-facing business outcomes.
Open a consolidated commercial true-up across both Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement workloads (Integration Suite and Joule Studio) and SAP Integration Suite to right-size against the ~$90K combined projected overage before fiscal year-end billing exposure compounds.
Run a workload attribution review on SAP Integration Suite and the BTP EA to map which integration flows and services drove the April step-change, separating sustained production traffic from uncontrolled growth and establishing tagging, quotas, and FinOps controls.
Launch an SAP Build use-case expansion and citizen-developer enablement program to convert the dormant $15K+ of contracted Build capacity into apps and workflows that consume the integration backbone Lubrizol has already paid to scale.
Establish governance for the Joule Studio footprint on BTP — define which AI agent scenarios are in scope, attach them to the Integration Suite APIs they will depend on, and set a consumption ceiling before agentic adoption scales without architectural intent.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
57.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$20K
YTD Budget$35K
YTD ACV: $102K
Projected Consumed: $48K · Projected Budget: $121K · Projected ACV: $245K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Business Transformation Management stack at Lubrizol is architecturally inverted — Process Management (modeling/governance) and Process Intelligence (mining/analysis) are both provisioned, but neither is producing artifacts, meaning the LoB has bought the full discover-model-monitor loop yet is operating without a single governed process or mined investigation to transform.
Process Intelligence is functionally dormant despite ratable billing — the flat $1,227/month signal indicates no live process connectors or investigations are running, so the capability meant to expose O2C/P2P inefficiencies is delivering zero diagnostic value to transformation owners.
Process Management entitlement is scaling ahead of organizational readiness — ACV stepped to $48,031 with only 48.5% YTD attainment and no upward modeling trajectory, indicating seats and workspaces exist but a governed process portal and active modeler community do not, blocking the 'single source of process truth' the LoB contracted for.
The two Signavio products are being consumed as isolated licenses rather than as a connected transformation suite — without mined processes in Intelligence feeding target models in Management, Lubrizol cannot close the loop from as-is insight to to-be design, which is the entire premise of the Business Transformation Management investment.
Stand up a joint Process Intelligence + Process Management activation on one priority value stream (O2C or P2P) so mined event data in Intelligence directly informs the to-be models published in Management, establishing the end-to-end transformation loop.
Validate active modeler seats, published processes, and connected source systems against the escalating back-half budget curve, and if a single pilot dominates, formally expand process domain coverage before the September ramp in Management and the Q4 tranches in Intelligence.
Establish a process governance council with Lubrizol's transformation and process owners to assign accountable owners per modeled process and per mined investigation, converting provisioned entitlement into measurable modeling and mining activity.
Build a use-case backlog tied to named business outcomes (cycle time, touchless rate, working capital) so Signavio consumption is justified by transformation KPIs rather than license recognition.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
16.3%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$14K
YTD Budget$83K
YTD ACV: $161K
Projected Consumed: $32K · Projected Budget: $204K · Projected ACV: $387K
Solution Insights and Actions
Customer Experience at Lubrizol is architecturally single-threaded — SAP Commerce Cloud is the only CX product in play, meaning the LoB has a digital storefront but no surrounding capabilities (marketing, sales, service, or customer data) to convert traffic into orchestrated buyer journeys.
Commerce Cloud is operating as a static catalog rather than a unified commerce engine — the flat $2,556–$2,807 monthly band across five months indicates one narrow B2B use case is live while pricing, promotions, personalization, and AI-driven merchandising capabilities remain dormant, blocking the 'grow sales across every channel' outcome the platform was contracted to deliver.
The commercial gap is structural, not seasonal — $13,529 consumed against a $203,642 annual contract (6.6%) with $29,460 ACV already accrued reflects an architecture sized for multi-site, multi-segment B2B commerce that is being used as a low-volume transactional site, leaving ~$171K of capacity unmonetized.
Run a Commerce Cloud capability audit on the live tenant to inventory active sites, catalogs, B2B buyer accounts, and promotion/personalization usage, then map dormant capabilities to specific Lubrizol product lines or customer segments that could be onboarded.
Build a CX architecture expansion plan that pairs Commerce Cloud with adjacent SAP CX capabilities (Sales Cloud, Service Cloud, or Customer Data Platform) to convert the standalone storefront into a connected buyer journey and justify the contracted ceiling.
Convene a digital commerce roadmap workshop with Lubrizol's CX leadership to prioritize storefront expansion (new B2B segments, additional product catalogs, self-service reorder) targeting the $29,460 ACV gap and the flat $2,700/month consumption baseline.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
99.7%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$454K
YTD Budget$455K
YTD ACV: $528K
Projected Consumed: $1.1M · Projected Budget: $1.1M · Projected ACV: $1.3M
Solution Insights and Actions
Lubrizol's Procure-to-Pay spine is operating at architectural full-stack — Ariba Sourcing feeds Contracts, which governs Buying and Invoicing, and all four are actively consumed — but the upstream Sourcing engine is decaying (43% MoM drop, 36% of contract value at risk) while downstream Buying/Invoicing runs hot at 110%+, meaning new demand is flowing through purchasing without fresh negotiated agreements behind it, eroding the savings discipline the sourcing investment was meant to enforce.
Catalog-enabled guided buying is the missing link in Lubrizol's procurement architecture — Ariba Buying burns a metronomic $30,128/month at 100% attainment while Ariba Catalog is frozen at $160/month (5.8% of ceiling), which means buyers are transacting in Ariba but bypassing preferred-supplier, negotiated-price content, undermining both spend compliance and the cost-reduction outcome the Catalog module was contracted to deliver.
The Travel & Expense stack is structurally undersized across every component — Expense (105%), ExpenseIt (126%), Reporting Services (129%), Preferred Success (126%), and Concur-Other (295%) are all in overrun — indicating T&E is a fully embedded, high-velocity business process whose contractual envelope no longer reflects actual organizational scale, exposing Lubrizol to mid-year service disruption across receipt capture, reporting, and success enablement simultaneously.
Statutory compliance posture is healthy but capacity-constrained — Document & Reporting Compliance is in active production at 800%+ monthly attainment with only $124 of ACV headroom remaining, meaning Lubrizol's ability to file VAT and e-invoicing mandates across jurisdictions will hit a hard stop before year end unless capacity is expanded, a direct regulatory risk to the finance close.
Open a coordinated true-up across the Concur estate (Expense, ExpenseIt, Reporting Services, Preferred Success, Company Bill Statement, and Concur-Other) to right-size contracted entitlements against the demonstrated full-stack overrun rather than negotiating each SKU in isolation.
Launch a Catalog enablement and guided-buying program tied to Ariba Buying's existing $30,128/month transaction base, onboarding top indirect-spend suppliers as punch-out and hosted catalogs to redirect uncatalogued requisitions onto negotiated content.
Diagnose the Ariba Sourcing decline by reviewing event pipeline, active category managers, and Contracts-to-Sourcing handoff to determine whether sourcing demand has genuinely contracted or whether category teams are renewing supply outside the platform, then reconnect the Sourcing→Contracts→Buying chain.
Expand Document & Reporting Compliance capacity ahead of the $124 ACV ceiling breach by scoping additional country mandates and reporting entities into the contract to convert the persistent overrun into a justified uplift before filings are interrupted.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
92.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$280K
YTD Budget$301K
YTD ACV: $329K
Projected Consumed: $672K · Projected Budget: $731K · Projected ACV: $790K
Solution Insights and Actions
Lubrizol's HCM suite is architecturally complete — core HR (Employee Central), talent (Performance, Compensation, Career Development), Learning, Recruiting, and Onboarding are all contracted — but adoption is flat or declining across every talent module, meaning the integrated talent lifecycle the suite is designed to deliver is operating as disconnected systems of record rather than a connected employee journey.
SF Onboarding at ~31% attainment is the critical architectural break in the hire-to-productive-employee flow — Recruiting Management is steadily filling requisitions and Employee Central holds the resulting records, but the Onboarding handoff between them is being bypassed, so new hires are entering core HR without the compliance, provisioning, and activation steps the architecture is designed to enforce.
The Performance → Compensation → Career Development chain is degrading in lockstep, with all three modules showing month-over-month consumption decline — because Compensation depends on Performance data and Career Development depends on skills/performance signals, the eroding engagement in Performance and Goals is starving the downstream pay-for-performance and talent growth processes of the inputs they need.
Preferred Success SFSF is consuming at 116% of plan while the modules it is meant to accelerate (Onboarding, Compensation, Career Development) are underconsuming — the customer success investment is being absorbed but is not translating into adoption lift in the SuccessFactors modules it was contracted to enable.
Redirect Preferred Success SFSF engagement hours toward the three lowest-attainment modules — Onboarding, Career Development, and Compensation — and tie success metrics to module-level adoption rather than ticket throughput.
Run an end-to-end hire-to-onboard process review with Lubrizol HR to identify why Recruiting volume is not flowing into Onboarding, and reconfigure the Recruiting-to-Onboarding-to-Employee Central handoff as the default new-hire path.
Anchor a talent process re-launch on Lubrizol's upcoming compensation cycle that forces the Performance → Compensation → Career Development data chain to be used end-to-end, reversing the synchronized decline across the three modules.
Commission an Employee Central telemetry review to confirm whether the fixed $12,626 monthly burn reflects active self-service, position management, and workflow usage or a passive license draw, since every other HCM module depends on Employee Central being the live system of record.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
100.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$6K
YTD Budget$6K
YTD ACV: $6K
Projected Consumed: $14K · Projected Budget: $16K · Projected ACV: $14K
Solution Insights and Actions
Lubrizol's Supply Chain footprint on SAP is architecturally narrow — Business Network Commerce Automation is the sole active capability, meaning the buyer-supplier transaction layer (PO, confirmation, ship notice, invoice) is digitized but upstream sourcing, contracts, and supplier risk governance are not part of the operating model, leaving commerce automation as a point solution rather than an end-to-end procurement architecture.
The $1,193/month flat-line consumption pattern indicates Lubrizol has operationalized a fixed supplier community and document mix into recurring flows, which delivers compliance and AP automation benefits but signals the network is not expanding to new trading partners or document types that would compound the value of the Business Network investment.
The stepped Sep–Dec budget ramp creates an $1,328 (8.5%) capacity gap against current run-rate, meaning planned architectural headroom for supplier or transaction expansion exists but no adoption catalyst is visible in the signals to convert that capacity into realized supplier collaboration outcomes.
Confirm with Lubrizol procurement the supplier onboarding wave or document-type expansion (e.g., service entry sheets, quality notifications) underpinning the Q4 budget step-up so Business Network Commerce Automation consumption scales into the planned capacity.
Baseline the current Business Network transaction profile — supplier count, document types, spend categories covered — to identify untapped trading partners and process flows that can lift the steady-state run rate.
Position the architectural gap around sourcing and contract governance to Lubrizol, framing Business Network Commerce Automation as the transactional endpoint of a broader source-to-pay capability that is currently missing upstream controls.
Tropicana Products, Inc.
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $427K
YTD Budget: $515K
YTD ACV: $495K
Projected Consumed: $1.0M
Projected Budget: $1.3M
Projected ACV: $1.2M
EA Insights and Actions
Tropicana has contracted three separate BTP/CPEA platform entitlements — BDC Public (32% hot), Database Cloud ($0), and Finance CPEA ($0) — yet only one is consuming, revealing that the customer is buying platform foundations per-domain rather than operating BTP as a single enterprise fabric, which means every cross-domain scenario (FP&A-to-treasury, PLM-to-IBP, Process Intelligence-to-Process Management) has no shared runtime to land on and will continue to be stitched together manually outside the architecture.
Integration Suite's four-month decline ($2,730 → $2,349) is the single most consequential signal in the estate because it is the connective tissue beneath every other declining domain — T&E capture-to-analytics, Datasphere pipeline freshness, PLM-to-IBP handover, and Process Intelligence-to-Process Management closed-loop — meaning the synchronized decay across Finance, Data & AI, Supply Chain, and Transformation is not five independent problems but one integration-layer problem expressing itself five ways.
Across all five L1 domains Tropicana has fully funded the systems of record (IBP at 100%, Datasphere, Process Intelligence at $254K, Concur Expense, Multi Bank Connect) but is starving the systems of engagement and governance above them (SAC BI 50%, SAC Planning shortfall, Process Management $2.3K, Integrated Product Development 16%, ExpenseIt 57%), meaning the enterprise can capture and store process, financial, demand, and product data but cannot turn any of it into governed business decisions — the architecture is a data-rich, decision-poor estate.
With a combined ~$150K+ projected YTD shortfall concentrated in the consumption, planning, and extensibility layers (SAC BI, SAC Planning, Joule Studio, Process Intelligence, Integrated Product Development, Database Cloud) against fully-burning record-of-truth layers, Tropicana's FY26 architectural trajectory is regression toward an ERP-of-record posture rather than progression toward the AI-ready, closed-loop enterprise its contract portfolio was designed to deliver.
Convene a single enterprise architecture review covering all three dormant platform entitlements (BDC Public, Database Cloud, Finance CPEA) and BTP to define one shared platform operating model with named workloads from Finance, Supply Chain, and Data & AI rather than allowing each LoB to underutilize its own slice.
Commission an Integration Suite flow inventory and dependency map across T&E, Datasphere pipelines, PLM-to-IBP, and Process Intelligence-to-Process Management to identify which retired or quiesced integrations are causing the synchronized cross-domain decline and rebuild the integration backlog as one enterprise pipeline.
Launch a consumption-layer activation program pairing SAC BI with Datasphere, SAC Planning with FP&A driver models, Process Management with named process owners, and Integrated Product Development with R&D recipe workflows so the systems of engagement catch up to the systems of record they sit on.
Establish an enterprise adoption scorecard that reports record-layer vs. engagement-layer vs. platform-layer attainment across all L1 domains so Tropicana governs the SAP estate as one architecture and surfaces the bifurcation pattern before it consumes another fiscal year of contract value.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
96.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$113K
YTD Budget$118K
YTD ACV: $91K
Projected Consumed: $272K · Projected Budget: $294K · Projected ACV: $217K
Solution Insights and Actions
Tropicana's Data & AI stack is architecturally bifurcated — the BDC Public foundation (Datasphere + Data Intelligence Cloud) is ingesting and modeling data, but the consumption layer (SAC BI) is decaying toward 50% attainment, meaning the LoB is building an AI-ready semantic layer that no one is reading from.
Datasphere is functioning as the unified data layer as designed, but Data Intelligence Cloud's three-month decline (104.7% → 78.0% → 75.5%) signals the upstream integration and pipeline orchestration tier is weakening — without it, Datasphere's federated access and AI-ready promise will starve from lack of fresh, transformed inputs.
The Database Cloud Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement sitting at $0 while the BDC Public CPEA runs 32% hot reveals a one-sided platform adoption — Tropicana is consuming analytics and data services aggressively but has never activated the database/persistence foundation, leaving a structural gap beneath the Data & AI stack.
The BDC Public CPEA overrun pattern (three of five months above 120%) coexisting with declining attainment in DIC and SAC BI suggests consumption is concentrating in unmanaged infrastructure services rather than the business-outcome products, meaning Tropicana is paying for platform capacity that isn't translating into analytics or planning value.
Run a joint consumption-to-workload mapping for the BDC Public CPEA to determine whether the overrun is supporting Datasphere/DIC pipelines or unrelated workloads, and rebalance the burn against the $101,360 ceiling.
Launch a Data Intelligence Cloud re-engagement with Tropicana's data engineering team to restore pipeline activity feeding Datasphere, targeting recovery of the $2,126 YTD shortfall and reversing the April–May regression.
Pair an SAC BI adoption sprint with a Datasphere semantic model showcase so business users see governed, AI-ready data in dashboards, closing the $4,891 BI consumption gap by activating the semantic layer that already exists.
Open a platform activation workshop for the Database Cloud CPEA to identify a first persistence or data service workload, moving LPR868 off $0 and aligning the database foundation with the active BDC Public investment.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
64.7%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$17K
YTD Budget$27K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $42K · Projected Budget: $66K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
Tropicana's Technology Foundation rests entirely on a single BTP enterprise agreement that is fragmenting into three disconnected workload pockets — Integration Suite carrying the load at 84% YTD attainment, while Joule Studio (13.6%) and general platform services (76.5%) sit largely dormant — meaning the LoB has contracted a unified extension-and-integration platform but is operating it as an isolated iPaaS, forfeiting the composability that justifies an enterprise agreement.
Integration Suite consumption is in a sustained four-month decline from $2,730 to $2,349, which architecturally signals that integration flows are being retired or quiesced faster than new ones are onboarded — a leading indicator that downstream LoB processes dependent on SAP-to-SAP and SAP-to-third-party data exchange are losing their connective tissue.
Joule Studio's flat $156–$214 monthly burn confirms a single static proof-of-concept rather than active AI-agent development, meaning Tropicana has entitlement to build generative AI extensions across its SAP estate but is not architecturally positioned to deliver any Joule-powered LoB outcome this fiscal year.
With a combined projected shortfall of roughly $23,900 across the three BTP workstreams against a $65,730 envelope, the Technology Foundation is on track to underdeliver about a third of its contracted platform capacity — the architectural risk is not cost, but that extensions, automations, and AI use cases the broader LoB roadmap assumes will have no runtime home.
Convene a BTP architecture review with Tropicana to map active Integration Suite flows, identify the workloads driving the four-month decline, and rebuild a pipeline of integration scenarios that restores monthly consumption above the $3,300 budget line.
Launch a Joule Studio activation engagement anchored on two to three concrete LoB agent use cases so the entitlement transitions from a static sandbox into a productive AI extension layer on top of Tropicana's SAP core.
Run a Technology Foundation service discovery workshop to surface dormant BTP capabilities — extension runtime, data and analytics services, automation — that can absorb the $2,898 general-platform gap and convert unused entitlement into LoB capability.
Establish a single BTP adoption scorecard spanning Integration Suite, Joule Studio, and general platform services so Tropicana governs the enterprise agreement as one architectural fabric rather than three independent line items.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
52.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$55K
YTD Budget$105K
YTD ACV: $123K
Projected Consumed: $131K · Projected Budget: $257K · Projected ACV: $296K
Solution Insights and Actions
Tropicana's Business Transformation stack is architecturally inverted — Process Intelligence (the data-driven mining layer) is the dominant investment at $254K but stalling, while Process Management (the modeling and governance layer) sits at just $2.3K and runs in volatile bursts, meaning the LoB can see process behavior but has no durable model of the as-is or to-be to act on what it sees.
The transformation loop is broken at the handoff between discovery and design — Process Intelligence findings have nowhere to land because Process Management is used project-by-project rather than as the system of record for process ownership, so mining insights cannot be translated into governed process change at Tropicana.
Process Intelligence adoption has collapsed in May (-$18,530 reversal after four months of ~87–90% budget attainment) and is tracking to leave ~$125K of annual value unconsumed, signaling the customer has exhausted the initial use case and has no pipeline of follow-on processes to mine.
Process Management consumption volatility ($73 → $549 → $207 against a $188 monthly budget) confirms modeling is being done reactively by a small team rather than embedded into business-as-usual process ownership, which is why the $2,979 YTD ACV will likely leave headroom even as monthly spend overruns budget pacing.
Resolve the -$18,530 May Process Intelligence entry to confirm whether it is a billing reversal or a true usage collapse before committing to any expansion motion.
Scope a second Process Intelligence use case (e.g., order-to-cash or accounts payable) to lift monthly consumption above the $21K budget line and close the projected $125K annual gap.
Anchor Process Management to a named end-to-end process with assigned process owners so modeling becomes a recurring governance activity rather than project-driven bursts.
Establish a closed-loop operating model where Process Intelligence findings are routed into Process Management for redesign and approval, making the two products function as one transformation capability instead of two disconnected tools.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
63.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$37K
YTD Budget$59K
YTD ACV: $73K
Projected Consumed: $89K · Projected Budget: $152K · Projected ACV: $175K
Solution Insights and Actions
Tropicana's Finance and Spend Management architecture is fragmenting along its core process boundaries — Concur Expense, ExpenseIt, and Reporting Services are all declining in lockstep, meaning the T&E capability is collapsing as a whole rather than failing at one layer, and the LoB is losing closed-loop spend visibility from capture through analytics.
The treasury connectivity layer is contracted but architecturally inert — Multi Bank Connect is running as a single static pipe with a May reversal and flat $71/month baseline, so Tropicana owns a multi-bank hub but is operating it as a point-to-point connection, forfeiting the payment centralization and SWIFT gpi transparency the product is designed to deliver.
The planning and extensibility foundation of the FP&A stack is unbuilt — Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement sits at $0 consumed while SAC Planning has plateaued and is trending to a ~$6K annual shortfall, meaning Tropicana has no BTP runway to extend planning models, integrate operational data, or unify financial and operational planning as the architecture intends.
Concur ExpenseIt's declining attainment (83% → 57%) against a flat Expense user base indicates receipt-capture is decoupled from expense submission at Tropicana — submitters are bypassing the mobile capture layer, which removes the OCR and policy-enforcement entry point that makes downstream Expense and Reporting Services accurate and audit-ready.
Convene a T&E architecture review across Concur Expense, ExpenseIt, and Reporting Services to diagnose the common root cause of the synchronized decline and re-establish the capture-to-analytics flow as a single operational process rather than three independent tools.
Activate Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement against a named FP&A or treasury extension workload — such as an SAC Planning data integration or a Multi Bank Connect format extension — so the BTP layer begins underpinning the Finance capabilities it was contracted to enable.
Run a Multi Bank Connect expansion assessment with Tropicana's treasury team to onboard additional bank relationships, entities, and payment formats onto the hub, and resolve the May -$71 reversal to confirm the connectivity baseline is clean.
Launch an SAC Planning model-expansion engagement with FP&A to move beyond the current static footprint into workforce, sales, or driver-based operational planning, unifying financial and operational planning as the product is architected to deliver.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
99.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$205K
YTD Budget$207K
YTD ACV: $209K
Projected Consumed: $491K · Projected Budget: $496K · Projected ACV: $501K
Solution Insights and Actions
Tropicana's supply chain planning backbone is architecturally lopsided — IBP is fully operationalized as the demand-to-supply nerve center at 100% burn, but the upstream product development layer that should feed it with manufacturable BOMs, recipes, and cost-aligned designs is running at 16% attainment, meaning new product introductions are not flowing into the integrated planning process through the intended architecture.
IBP is delivering steady-state S&OP value but has zero expansion headroom — the flat $40,843 monthly burn against a fully consumed $490K contract means capabilities like demand sensing, inventory optimization, and additional planner tiers cannot be activated without a contract conversation, capping the LoB's ability to mature beyond baseline planning.
SAP Integrated Product Development is regressing from pilot rather than scaling — declining monthly consumption ($95 → $59) signals that recipe collaboration, enterprise BOM management, and PLM-to-manufacturing handover are not embedded in R&D daily behavior, leaving Tropicana exposed to design-to-supply misalignment on new juice and beverage SKUs.
The architectural bridge between product innovation and supply planning is broken — without active Integrated Product Development adoption, IBP planners are forecasting and balancing supply for products whose cost, BOM, and feasibility data never traversed the intended PLM-to-S/4-to-IBP pathway, forcing manual reconciliation outside the system of record.
Initiate an IBP capacity and expansion review with Tropicana's supply chain leadership to scope uplift for demand sensing, inventory optimization, or additional planner tiers before the contract ceiling blocks maturation of the planning footprint.
Launch a targeted adoption sprint for SAP Integrated Product Development centered on recipe development and enterprise BOM collaboration with R&D and product development leads to reverse the downward consumption trend and close the $4,658 ACV gap.
Validate IBP usage depth with Tropicana's planning team — confirm active planners, scenarios, and planning areas in use — to ensure the flat $40,843/month burn reflects genuine S&OP value rather than idle subscription cost.
Convene a joint R&D and supply chain planning workshop to design the end-to-end flow from Integrated Product Development into IBP, ensuring new product BOMs, recipes, and cost data feed the planning cycle through the intended architecture rather than offline handoffs.
VS Service Company, LLC
Professional services
YTD Consumed: $276K
YTD Budget: $299K
YTD ACV: $257K
Projected Consumed: $663K
Projected Budget: $711K
Projected ACV: $616K
EA Insights and Actions
VS Service is running production-grade AI, integration, and data fabric workloads (Joule Studio 133%, Integration Suite 109-133%, BDC 197%) on top of a dormant BTP foundation and a $0-consumed Finance CPEA, meaning the customer has effectively built a multi-domain digital core without the governance, identity, and extensibility substrate underneath it — every overrun is also a compounding compliance and clean-core debt event.
The enterprise has two simultaneous capacity cliffs (BDC $25K projected overrun, BAIP $26K combined Joule+Integration overrun) sitting next to ~$49K idle CPEA Public balance and a fully dormant Finance CPEA, exposing a structural contract-design problem where capacity is mis-allocated across L1s rather than a true demand shortage — the money to fund growth is already on paper but trapped in the wrong envelopes.
Integration Suite's structural overrun is the single common dependency linking Data & AI (BDC pipelines), Technology Foundation (Joule extensibility), and Finance (Ariba Sourcing-to-Buying handoff at 42%→87.8%) — its throttling will simultaneously break the data fabric, the AI use cases, and the source-to-pay continuity, making it the highest-leverage architectural chokepoint in the estate.
Ariba's fragmented Source-to-Pay flow (sourcing awards not feeding buying, $20K AP leakage) mirrors the BDC-without-BTP-services pattern in Data & AI — the customer consistently lights up the headline solution but skips the connective tissue, indicating an enterprise-wide procurement and adoption habit that buys destinations without buying the roads between them.
Convene a cross-L1 CPEA rebalancing review to redirect the $49K idle BDC Public and dormant Finance CPEA balances toward the BDC, Joule Studio, and Integration Suite overrun trajectories before negotiating net-new capacity uplift.
Run an enterprise integration architecture workshop centered on Integration Suite as the shared backbone for BDC data flows, Joule AI extensions, and Ariba source-to-pay continuity, with a prioritized activation plan for the governance and identity BTP services they all depend on.
Launch a clean-core and extensibility posture assessment to map where AI, integration, and procurement customizations are landing today, and establish BTP as the mandated extension layer before the Joule and Ariba footprints harden customizations into the core.
Establish a single monthly enterprise architecture checkpoint spanning BAIP, Data & AI, and Finance to govern the combined ~$50K projected overrun, surface broken cross-domain handoffs (Sourcing→Buying, BDC→CPEA services), and align contract envelopes to the customer's demonstrated AI-plus-integration-plus-S2P trajectory.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
86.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$34K
YTD Budget$39K
YTD ACV: $53K
Projected Consumed: $81K · Projected Budget: $96K · Projected ACV: $128K
Solution Insights and Actions
The business data fabric is the only Data & AI capability that has truly landed — SAP Business Data Cloud is running at 197% YTD attainment with mission-critical workloads on track to exhaust the $42,560 ACV ceiling before year-end, confirming that agentic AI, SAC, and Datasphere use cases are now structurally core to the LoB rather than experimental.
The broader cloud platform foundation underneath BDC is bifurcated — Database Cloud on CPEA is consistently 6-24% over budget showing real workload scale-out, while BDC Public on the same CPEA went silent after a single January burst, meaning the LoB is scaling persistence and data services but has not operationalized the complementary platform services that would extend BDC's reach.
The architectural risk is a capacity cliff colliding with an unused entitlement — the LoB will hit a hard BDC contract ceiling with ~2 months of FY runway while $49,122 of CPEA Public capacity sits idle, leaving production data and AI workloads exposed even though platform budget is available elsewhere in the estate.
Open a BDC capacity expansion and workload review with VS Service Company to secure additional ACV ahead of the $25,353 projected overrun and validate that the 197% consumption reflects high-value production use cases rather than unoptimized pipelines.
Diagnose the post-January drop-off on CPEA BDC Public, identify the services behind the initial $5,147 spike, and build an activation plan that converts the $49,122 unconsumed balance into recurring workloads aligned to the BDC data fabric strategy.
Right-size the CPEA Database Cloud envelope to match the $9,796 projected run rate and run a service-level consumption review to contain the $1,107 overage while preserving the scaled database footprint supporting BDC.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
120.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$64K
YTD Budget$53K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $154K · Projected Budget: $127K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation is architecturally bifurcated — Joule Studio (AI/extensibility) and Integration Suite (middleware) are both running 9–33% hot while the broader BTP platform shows $0 consumption, meaning VS Service is consuming BAIP narrowly for AI experimentation and integration flows rather than as the unified extension and data fabric it was contracted to be.
Integration Suite over-consumption signals that VS Service has made BAIP the de facto backbone for inter-system orchestration, and the sustained overrun ($23K cumulative, $10.9K projected annual gap) indicates integration volume is a structural feature of the LoB's landscape — not discretionary — so hitting the contract ceiling will directly throttle business process continuity, not just optional workloads.
Joule Studio consumption running at 133% of plan reveals the LoB is scaling generative AI use cases faster than the contract envelope anticipated, but with no corresponding activation of governance or platform services on the wider CPEA, the AI footprint is expanding without the architectural guardrails (identity, data governance, lifecycle) that BTP is designed to provide.
The $107 annual 'Other' BTP plan with a July step-up and zero May activity indicates planned-but-unscheduled platform foundation services that underpin Integration Suite and Joule Studio — if these foundational services remain dormant, the two hot workloads are effectively running on an incomplete platform stack, increasing operational and compliance risk.
Run a unified CPEA consumption and architecture review across Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and dormant BTP services to map which AI and integration workloads are driving the ~$26K projected annual overage and which foundational platform capabilities must be activated to support them safely.
Initiate a CPEA true-forward and resizing conversation that consolidates the Joule Studio ($15.9K) and Integration Suite ($10.9K) overage trajectories into a single capacity uplift aligned to VS Service's demonstrated AI-plus-integration adoption pattern.
Launch an activation plan for the dormant Technology Foundation services on LPR868 ahead of the July budget ramp, prioritizing the governance, identity, and data services that the active Integration Suite and Joule Studio workloads architecturally depend on.
Establish a monthly BAIP consumption and capability checkpoint with VS Service to govern workload growth, surface unmanaged AI/integration sprawl, and ensure the contract envelope tracks the LoB's actual platform trajectory.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
86.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$179K
YTD Budget$207K
YTD ACV: $203K
Projected Consumed: $428K · Projected Budget: $488K · Projected ACV: $488K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Source-to-Pay backbone is architecturally fragmented — Ariba Sourcing operates at 42% attainment with ad-hoc event activity while Ariba Buying and Invoicing decelerates to 87.8%, meaning strategic sourcing outcomes are not feeding downstream buying channels and the LoB is losing the contract-to-PO continuity that justifies the Ariba investment.
Ariba Buying and Invoicing is active but leaking — declining attainment and a $20,467 cumulative budget gap over Feb–May indicate requisitions and invoices are bypassing guided buying and three-way matching, eroding AP automation, compliance enforcement, and the savings capture the platform was sized to deliver.
Ariba Sourcing is deployed but not standardized — $5,880 consumed against $13,942 with zig-zag monthly usage shows RFx and e-auction capabilities are running as exceptions rather than the default channel, leaving spend awarded without competitive sourcing discipline or auditable supplier evaluation.
The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is a dormant extensibility layer — $0 consumed against $35 planned with no activation signals means the LoB has no platform foundation for Ariba integrations, custom workflows, or AP/sourcing extensions, capping the ceiling on what the procurement stack can be tailored to deliver.
Run a transaction-source audit on Ariba Buying and Invoicing to identify off-platform requisitions and non-PO invoices and route them back through guided buying and supplier channels to close the $20,467 budget gap.
Establish a sourcing threshold policy that routes all spend above a defined value through Ariba Sourcing, converting irregular event activity into a standardized strategic sourcing channel feeding downstream buying.
Connect Ariba Sourcing awards to Ariba Buying and Invoicing catalogs and supplier records so sourced agreements automatically govern requisition and invoice flows, restoring source-to-pay continuity.
Launch a kickoff and first-workload onboarding for the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement, prioritizing a procurement integration or extension use case to activate the dormant platform contract.

Projected ACV: $2.0M
Industry Insights
Integration Suite is the shared architectural fault line across the entire Agribusiness cohort — Land O'Lakes sits at zero consumption blocking all cross-application flows, Coop Regions is over-consuming at 107% on a single legacy use case, CHS is 125% over-consumed but feeding dormant downstream consumers, and Corteva runs at 143% without endpoint activation — meaning every customer owns the integration backbone but none has operationalized it as the enterprise connective tissue for closed-loop planning, AI extensibility, or unified supply chain visibility that agribusiness margin management demands.
Cargill leads the cohort on AI and data consumption intensity with Joule Studio and BDC Public actively overrunning in BAIP, while CHS, Corteva, and Land O'Lakes show zero or near-zero Joule activation and Coop Regions has $0 of $174 consumed — Cargill is the only customer converting platform investment into AI workloads, but it does so without the BTP extension fabric that would govern and scale those capabilities enterprise-wide.
CHS and Corteva share an inverted architecture pattern where infrastructure layers (Database Cloud at 2.2-2.5x for Corteva, Integration Suite at 125% for CHS) run hot while decision layers (SAC Planning at 7.6% for CHS, SAC BI at 0.2% for Corteva) are dormant — both cooperatives and agriscience companies are paying enterprise-scale compute costs without converting that spend into the predictive planning, demand sensing, and yield forecasting outcomes that differentiate winners in commodity-volatile agribusiness.
Land O'Lakes is the most architecturally exposed customer in the cohort — Integration Suite at zero, Fieldglass dark, Ariba Sourcing flatlined, and CPEA pools near-zero burn while transactional layers (Ariba Buying 108%, Concur over-consuming) execute without governance — creating the highest uncontrolled spend leakage risk in a CPG/agribusiness portfolio where margin discipline is existential.
Coop Regions demonstrates the strongest planning activation in the peer group with IBP at 100% and SAC Planning recovering to 111%, ahead of CHS (SAC Planning 7.6%, IBP dormant), Corteva (SAC Planning 55%, IBP 158% but isolated), and Land O'Lakes (no visible planning integration) — but Coop Regions operates these planning brains without execution feeds or Datasphere connectivity, meaning even the cohort leader runs plans in isolation from actuals.
Run a cohort-wide Integration Suite activation sprint positioning the backbone as the shared enabler for closed-loop agribusiness processes — Datasphere connectivity for Land O'Lakes, planning-to-execution flows for Coop Regions and Corteva, and downstream consumer activation for CHS — and use the workshop outputs to rebalance over-consumed versus dormant entitlements at each account.
Convene a cross-customer BTP platform governance forum to address the universal gap in extension, AI, and data fabric activation — consolidating Corteva's three dormant Enterprise Agreements, redirecting CHS's unused CPEA pools, activating Land O'Lakes' flatlined BDC and Database Cloud, and governing Cargill's Joule overrun — before each customer's renewal locks in misaligned architecture.
Establish Cargill as the peer reference for AI activation and facilitate a Joule Studio and BDC use-case exchange with CHS, Corteva, and Coop Regions, converting Cargill's consumption intensity into a replicable pattern while surfacing the BTP governance gaps that prevent the other four from following.
Prioritize Land O'Lakes for immediate Source-to-Pay architecture remediation to reactivate Fieldglass and expand Ariba Sourcing as upstream controls on the Buying overrun, then replicate the pattern to Cargill (Ariba Contracts at 33%) and CHS (Fieldglass at 39%) where procurement governance gaps create similar uncontrolled spend exposure.
Frame FY26 renewal narratives for the entire cohort around the dormant intelligence and AI layer — Datasphere, Joule, SAC Planning, and IBP execution feeds — positioning these as the missing predictive capability for agribusiness demand sensing, yield forecasting, and commodity volatility management rather than isolated platform entitlements at risk of forfeiture.

Tenneco Automotive
YTD Consumed: $596K
YTD Budget: $780K
YTD ACV: $966K
Projected Consumed: $1.4M
Projected Budget: $1.8M
Projected ACV: $2.3M
Industry Insights
Tenneco Automotive stands alone in this single-customer cohort with a critically dormant platform architecture — full CPEA entitlements across Data & AI, Integration Suite, Technology Foundation, Finance, and Joule Studio show zero consumption against $965K ACV, representing the most severe procurement-to-activation gap possible for an automotive supplier where EDI connectivity, plant telemetry, and supply chain responsiveness are existential capabilities.
Tenneco's Integration Suite dormancy is blocking three automotive-critical value streams simultaneously — aftermarket quote-to-order (C4C-to-ERP), demand-supply synchronization (IBP-to-S/4), and workforce continuity (Recruiting-to-Onboarding) — meaning a single middleware activation failure is compounding into multi-domain operational friction across the entire automotive value chain.
The only domain with active telemetry at Tenneco — HCM — shows a deteriorating system-of-record (Employee Central declining) while Preferred Success burns at 105%, indicating that even where SAP adoption exists, success investment is misallocated toward support rather than the integration and skills-data flows that would enable automotive workforce planning and plant staffing agility.
Tenneco's IBP deployment is plateaued at $1.8K/month with a projected $12K unconsumed gap, leaving the company without the demand sensing and supply response capabilities that automotive Tier-1 suppliers require to manage OEM schedule volatility and aftermarket channel complexity — a competitive disadvantage against peers who have operationalized planning.
With 38% consumption against budget ($595K consumed of $1.75M) and every platform entitlement dark, Tenneco's enterprise architecture posture is procurement-led rather than outcome-led, creating significant shelfware risk heading into FY26 renewal that will require executive intervention to remediate.
Convene an enterprise architecture review with Tenneco's CIO to consolidate duplicated Integration Suite entitlements and dormant CPEAs into a single platform activation roadmap anchored to automotive-specific use cases: supplier EDI, plant-floor telemetry, aftermarket order orchestration, and OEM demand signal processing.
Launch an integration-first activation sprint that treats Integration Suite activation as the prerequisite for every other domain expansion — IBP planning areas, C4C quote-to-order, SuccessFactors Onboarding handoff — to prevent further shadow integration accumulation as adoption eventually scales.
Instrument consumption telemetry across every dark entitlement (BDC Public, Database Cloud, Integration Suite, Joule Studio, FP&A CPEA, C4C) to establish an enterprise consumption baseline before any FY26 renewal conversation, converting shelfware risk into documented remediation targets.
Redirect Preferred Success and CSM capacity from HCM-only support cadence toward a cross-domain activation play targeting Integration Suite, IBP planning-area expansion, and C4C-to-ERP connectivity that collectively unblock Tenneco's automotive aftermarket velocity, supply resilience, and talent pipeline outcomes.

Industry Insights
All three chemicals customers share a critical BTP platform fragmentation pattern — H B Fuller has three separate dormant/declining BTP LoBs, Stepan has $0 Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreements blocking three domains, and Lubrizol has activated Integration Suite but left Datasphere at 13.8% and SAP Build flat — meaning no customer in this cohort has the unified extensibility and semantic foundation required for clean-core S/4 or AI readiness, a structural gap that will compound as industry pressure for real-time formulation analytics, batch traceability, and regulatory compliance automation intensifies.
Lubrizol leads this chemicals cohort on integration backbone maturity with Integration Suite running at $14K/month in production, while H B Fuller's Integration Suite is eroding ($13.5K→$11.7K, $62K shortfall) and Stepan's is declining -17% — however, Lubrizol's lead is hollow because Datasphere semantic governance sits at 13.8% and Joule Studio is overshooting 5.4x without FinOps controls, meaning integration traffic is scaling without the governed data layer chemicals companies require for product compliance, SDS management, and AI-driven process optimization.
Stepan is the most architecturally fragmented customer in the cohort — Ariba Catalog at $97 of $20K, SF Onboarding at 41%, BDC/Datasphere/SAC at zero, and declining Performance-to-Compensation chain — creating an enterprise pattern where transactional systems capture data but governance layers are bypassed, which for a specialty chemicals manufacturer means procurement spend leakage, workforce compliance gaps, and zero foundation for the predictive analytics increasingly demanded by downstream CPG and industrial customers.
H B Fuller is the only customer paying for Signavio Process Intelligence and Management (~$893K contracted) but with Intelligence reversing -$49K and Management at 29% consumption, the process mining and modeling investment that should be identifying batch yield variability, formulation changeover inefficiencies, and regulatory bottlenecks across 90+ country operations is instead generating advisory overhead without operational insight — Stepan and Lubrizol have no comparable process intelligence investment, leaving H B Fuller with an unrealized capability advantage.
Procure-to-pay governance is structurally incomplete across the cohort — H B Fuller runs Ariba Buying & Invoicing at 96% with $0 on Contracts, Stepan has Ariba Buying at $725K but Catalog at 0.5% utilization, and Lubrizol shows Ariba Sourcing declining -43% MoM while Buying/Invoicing overruns — meaning all three chemicals manufacturers are processing supplier transactions without the upstream contract compliance, catalog governance, or sourcing optimization that protects margins in a sector facing volatile feedstock costs and stringent REACH/TSCA regulatory requirements.
Run a cohort-wide BTP activation sprint with all three customers, treating Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement dormancy (Stepan), Integration Suite erosion (H B Fuller), and Datasphere/Build gaps (Lubrizol) as one coordinated intervention to establish the unified extensibility and semantic foundation required for chemicals-specific compliance automation, batch traceability extensions, and AI-ready clean-core architecture.
Position Lubrizol's Integration Suite production deployment as the peer reference architecture for H B Fuller and Stepan, while simultaneously engaging Lubrizol on Datasphere and FinOps governance remediation — this creates a reciprocal learning model where Lubrizol demonstrates integration backbone value and H B Fuller/Stepan see a tangible chemicals-industry peer path forward.
Launch an Ariba procurement governance remediation program across the cohort to activate Ariba Contracts at H B Fuller, restore Catalog utilization at Stepan, and reverse Sourcing decline at Lubrizol — framing the intervention around chemicals-specific value drivers including feedstock price volatility hedging, supplier compliance documentation for REACH/TSCA, and maverick-spend reduction in a high-volume raw materials procurement environment.
Convene an H B Fuller-specific Signavio value realization engagement to wire Process Intelligence outputs into Process Management modeling and Preferred Success advisory streams, converting the $893K dormant investment into discoverable process improvement use cases (batch cycle time, formulation changeover, regulatory filing bottlenecks) that can later serve as a lighthouse for Stepan and Lubrizol process mining adoption.
Reframe the Stepan FY26 renewal conversation as a single enterprise activation scorecard spanning Data & AI ($6,138), BTP ($7.9K shortfall), Ariba Catalog ($20K gap), and SF Onboarding (59% unused) — consolidating four domain renewals into one cross-domain value realization plan that addresses the master data and governance bypass pattern before it permanently compromises downstream analytics and AI ambitions.

Industry Insights
BTP platform dormancy is the defining architectural failure across this Consumer Products cohort — Lancaster Colony, Molson Coors, S.C. Johnson, Ingredion, and Tropicana all show $0 or near-zero consumption against combined platform entitlements exceeding $20K annually, meaning 45% of the portfolio has contracted for clean-core extensibility and AI-readiness but is operating disconnected SaaS islands with no integration fabric, no Joule agent runway, and no governed path to the closed-loop supply-demand-margin automation that CPG margin defense requires.
Kraft Heinz leads the cohort on contracted enterprise breadth ($1.6M+ across S/4, Ariba, IBP, Signavio, SAC, BDC, BTP) but trails on depth — Process Intelligence at $526K remaining, Business Network at $1.1M shortfall, SAC BI at $0, Ariba Sourcing at 35% — while Conagra and Mondelez show the inverse pattern with production workloads overrunning (Integration Suite 133%, BDC 145%, IBP 100%) against dormant complements, revealing two distinct failure modes: breadth-without-activation versus depth-without-governance.
Integration Suite is the most consistent stress point across the portfolio — Conagra (133%), Mondelez (143%), Kraft Heinz (+$11.7K projected overrun), and Domino's (99%) are all at or above capacity while Ingredion, Lancaster Colony, Molson Coors, S.C. Johnson, and Tropicana sit at $0 or declining, meaning the cohort is bifurcated between customers straining against integration ceilings with no platform substrate and customers with no integration backbone at all, both of which block the supplier collaboration, demand sensing, and trade-spend optimization workflows that define CPG competitiveness.
S.C. Johnson is the only customer demonstrating analytics production at scale (BDC at 158% projected attainment, $112K vs $71K plan), making it the cohort's capability leader on data consumption, yet this strength is architecturally orphaned — BTP at $0, Joule Studio stalled, Integrated Product Development at 5.7% — meaning the insight layer is generating value the execution layer cannot absorb, a pattern Kraft Heinz and Mondelez should study and avoid as they scale their own BDC and Datasphere investments.
Primary Products Ingredients Americas and Tate and Lyle Americas are architectural outliers operating SAP as single-domain T&E utilities rather than enterprise platforms, with no Supply Chain, Manufacturing, or core Finance activation visible — for primary ingredients businesses where sourcing volatility, batch traceability, and formulation compliance define margin, this posture exposes both to operational risk their peers have at least contracted to address.
Launch a coordinated BTP activation sprint across Lancaster Colony, Molson Coors, S.C. Johnson, Ingredion, and Tropicana — the five customers with $0 platform consumption — using a shared reference architecture that anchors Integration Suite, Joule Studio, and Database Cloud to one cross-domain use case per customer (e.g., Signavio-to-S/4 process automation, Ariba-to-Buying compliance, IBP-to-Business Network forecast sharing) to convert ~$25K+ of dormant entitlement into active consumption.
Convene enterprise BTP true-up and rebalancing reviews at Conagra, Kraft Heinz, and Mondelez to reconcile simultaneous overruns (Integration Suite, BDC Public) and dormancy (Database Cloud, Finance CPEA, Joule Studio) within the same Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreements, preventing mid-year consumption freezes and aligning contracted capacity to actual architectural posture before renewal.
Run an Integration Suite capacity and architecture workshop across the six customers at or near ceiling (Conagra, Mondelez, Kraft Heinz, Domino's) and the five at zero (Ingredion, Lancaster Colony, Molson Coors, S.C. Johnson, Tropicana) to establish a cohort-wide integration maturity model that sequences capacity uplift for over-consumers and activation sprints for non-consumers against named CPG workflows: supplier collaboration, trade promotion integration, and demand-to-supply signal flow.
Initiate an executive value-realization review with Kraft Heinz covering the $1.6M+ of under-absorbed ACV across Process Intelligence, Business Network, SAC BI, Ariba Sourcing, and Supplier Risk, positioning the conversation as renewal-risk mitigation and tying each dormant capability to a named CPG outcome (supplier resiliency, tail-spend reduction, integrated planning) that justifies the contracted footprint.
Commission enterprise architecture roadmap sessions with Primary Products Ingredients Americas and Tate and Lyle Americas to expand beyond T&E into core Supply Chain and Finance domains, using their ingredient-industry dependency on sourcing volatility and batch traceability as the business case for Ariba, IBP, and BTP activation against the current single-domain posture.
