Industry Insights
BTP activation is near-zero across all six customers—AbbVie ($0), Abbott (HANA Cloud 65%, Build 44%), Cardinal Health (Build 68% with no Integration Suite), Eli Lilly (Integration Suite stalled), Hill-Rom (no BTP signal), and Medtronic (~1%)—meaning this $17.8M ACV life sciences cohort is operating SAP estates as disconnected transactional silos without the integration, AI, and extensibility fabric required for GxP automation, regulatory traceability, and closed-loop compliance workflows.
Eli Lilly and Medtronic exhibit the most architecturally inverted stacks in the cohort: Lilly's Joule Studio runs at 172% and Medtronic's at 150% while their Integration Suite, BTP data services, and compliance layers remain dormant or declining—both are building AI agents faster than they can govern or connect them, creating agentic investments without trustworthy enterprise data context.
AbbVie leads the cohort on transactional layer execution (Concur T&E 115-135%, Ariba B&I 100%+, Traceability Hub active) but trails all peers on foundational platform activation (BTP $0, SSO $0, Batch Release Hub $0), making it the highest-risk GxP compliance exposure in the group given its pharma manufacturing profile and regulatory audit surface.
Abbott is the only customer with Integration Suite over-consuming (106%, +$44K overage), yet its downstream systems of record—Cloud for Customer at 53%, Supplier Risk at 26%, HANA Cloud at 65%—are starved, meaning Abbott is scaling middleware without the data fabric or system-of-record discipline required for FDA, ESG, and financial reporting integrity.
Cardinal Health and Hill-Rom are the least architecturally mature in the cohort—both operate single-product anchors per domain (Cardinal: Build alone, Commerce alone, Ariba B&I dominant; Hill-Rom: Commerce storefront and Buying/Invoicing in isolation) with no evidence of integration backbone or governance layers, leaving roughly $1.2M combined stranded across dormant Commerce, Ariba Contracts, and Supplier Risk entitlements.
Launch a cohort-wide BTP activation sprint targeting AbbVie, Medtronic, and Eli Lilly first—these three represent the largest ACV and the most acute platform dormancy—positioning a single cross-domain integration workload (e.g., Batch Release + Traceability for AbbVie, Joule + Integration Suite for Lilly/Medtronic) as the reference pattern for the remaining customers.
Convene a life sciences compliance architecture workshop across AbbVie, Eli Lilly, and Medtronic to wire dormant Batch Release Hub, Document & Reporting Compliance, Watchlist Screening, and Supplier Risk into active BTP integration flows, closing the GxP and third-party risk gaps regulators examine first.
Open portfolio rebalancing conversations at Abbott, Cardinal Health, and Hill-Rom to redirect stranded ACV from dormant strategic capabilities (Commerce Cloud, Ariba Contracts/SLP, Supplier Risk, HANA Cloud) toward over-consuming operational layers (Integration Suite, T&E, Payments, Preferred Success), aligning contract envelopes to actual consumption shape before renewal.
Establish a peer-learning forum pairing Abbott's Integration Suite scaling (106%) with Eli Lilly and Medtronic's Joule Studio momentum (150-172%) to cross-pollinate integration discipline into AI activation and AI ambition into integration investment, accelerating both toward a governed, AI-ready enterprise posture.

Industry Insights
Cleveland-Cliffs stands alone in this single-customer mining cohort with a fragmented architecture problem rather than an adoption maturity problem — four contracted L1 domains (Data & AI, Integration, CX, Supply Chain) operate as disconnected point solutions with Integration Suite at critically low 18.8% attainment, which for a steel manufacturer facing volatile commodity markets and complex mill-to-customer logistics means planning forecasts cannot trigger execution, analytics lack governed data pipelines, and customer signals remain isolated from supply chain decisions.
Integration Suite dormancy is the architectural root cause blocking consumption growth across Cleveland-Cliffs' entire $390K portfolio — the 18.8% attainment ($26/month static iFlow) starves IBP of execution handoffs, SAC of trusted data feeds, and C4C of order-to-service flows, making this the single highest-leverage activation target in the mining cohort.
IBP at 64% attainment and $19K/month flat consumption represents Cleveland-Cliffs' strongest domain adoption yet delivers diminished value as a standalone S&OP engine — without connected manufacturing execution, logistics network integration, or CX demand signals, the largest SAP investment produces siloed forecasts rather than the demand volatility response and supply chain resilience that differentiate winning steel producers.
Cleveland-Cliffs exhibits an identical adoption signature across all four domains — single product activation, flat consumption trajectory, no platform extensions — indicating absent enterprise architecture governance and positioning the account on a trajectory to leave six-figure annual commitments unrealized while remaining unprepared for AI/Joule enablement.
Prioritize Integration Suite activation immediately as the enterprise unlock for Cleveland-Cliffs — stand up API Management, Event Mesh, and Trading Partner Management with iFlows explicitly connecting IBP outputs to mill execution systems, SAC to governed operational data sources, and C4C to order and service backends.
Convene a cross-domain enterprise architecture review with Cleveland-Cliffs IT and LoB leadership to map integration dependencies and produce a single sequenced activation roadmap that replaces the current four parallel domain plans with a connected steel value-chain approach.
Commission a contract-to-architecture reconciliation across Cleveland-Cliffs' ~$390K annual footprint to identify dormant capabilities in SAC planning, IBP demand sensing, C4C service depth, and BTP integration modules that should be activated, rescoped, or consolidated before renewal.
Deliver a steel-industry value-chain reference architecture session positioning Digital Manufacturing Cloud, Business Network for logistics and supplier collaboration, and Joule AI as the execution and intelligence layers that would operationalize Cleveland-Cliffs' existing planning, analytics, and CX investments at enterprise scale.

Marathon Petroleum Company LP
YTD Consumed: $767K
YTD Budget: $805K
YTD ACV: $1.0M
Projected Consumed: $1.8M
Projected Budget: $2.0M
Projected ACV: $2.4M
Industry Insights
Marathon Petroleum stands alone in this single-customer cohort with a fundamentally inverted architectural posture — Integration Suite running at 95-108% proves runtime capability exists, yet every upstream governance layer (Preferred Success at 38%, Ariba Sourcing at 42%, Process Intelligence collapsing, Public BTP dormant) that should shape demand remains underutilized, creating a 'run hot, govern cold' pattern that is especially dangerous for a downstream refiner where uncontrolled spend and ungoverned process changes directly erode already thin refining margins.
Marathon has purchased the complete architectural fabric for an intelligent enterprise — BTP across Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance plus S/4HANA Private Cloud, Ariba, Signavio, and SAC Planning — but operates each domain as an isolated island, meaning the $1M+ ACV investment is not delivering the closed-loop 'mine-to-model-to-execute-to-measure' capability that midstream and downstream operators require to optimize crack spreads, turnaround scheduling, and feedstock procurement in volatile commodity markets.
The consumption pattern reveals Marathon is simultaneously overspending on AI and transactional capacity (Joule Studio at 9x budget, HANA Cloud at 13x, Ariba Buying at 126%) while stranding approximately $300K+ in dormant entitlement across Preferred Success, Ariba Sourcing, CPEA Public, and Tech Foundation Other — a dual-bleed condition where the company pays overage penalties and shelfware costs concurrently without a contract vehicle to rebalance.
Marathon's Source-to-Pay chain is architecturally broken: Ariba Catalog is declining, Process Intelligence has reversed, and SAC Planning CPEA sits dormant, meaning the refiner cannot close the loop between supplier sourcing decisions, contract compliance, invoice automation, and demand forecasting — a critical gap when crude differentials and logistics costs swing rapidly and procurement discipline directly protects gross margin.
Convene an enterprise architecture review with Marathon's cross-LoB stakeholders to establish BTP as the unified integration, extension, and AI activation platform across Procurement, Finance, ERP, and Process Excellence, with a single activation roadmap that converts dormant CPEA and Tech Foundation entitlement into sanctioned cross-domain workloads.
Propose a commercial true-up that caps Joule Studio and HANA Cloud overage exposure while redirecting stranded Public BTP, Preferred Success, and FP&A CPEA entitlement toward high-value use cases in refining margin optimization, turnaround planning, and feedstock procurement analytics.
Launch a Source-to-Pay-to-Plan architectural workstream that reconnects Ariba Sourcing → Catalog → Buying with Signavio Process Intelligence mining and SAC Planning forecasts, restoring the governance loop Marathon needs to control invoice volume growth and AI-driven automation scaling.
Reposition Preferred Success as Marathon's architectural assurance layer by scheduling Expert Sessions and CQCs against the highest-risk integration points — BTP extensibility, Ariba-S/4 settlement flows, and Signavio-S/4 process alignment — before the remaining 62% of advisory entitlement expires unutilized.

Industry Insights
VS Service Company, LLC is the sole customer in this Professional Services cohort and demonstrates advanced AI and integration adoption (Joule Studio 133%, Integration Suite 109-133%, BDC 197%) that would position them as a capability leader in a larger peer group, yet this maturity is architecturally inverted—production workloads are running without the foundational BTP governance, identity, and extensibility services underneath, creating compounding clean-core debt that exposes the firm to compliance risk particularly acute in a services industry where client data governance and auditability are table stakes.
The structural contract misalignment—$49K idle CPEA Public and fully dormant Finance CPEA sitting next to $50K+ projected overruns in BDC and BAIP—reveals a procurement pattern where VS Service buys point solutions without funding the connective infrastructure, a habit that in Professional Services undermines the cross-practice visibility, resource optimization, and real-time project margin analytics that drive competitive differentiation.
Integration Suite is the single highest-leverage architectural chokepoint in the VS Service estate: its capacity ceiling simultaneously constrains BDC data pipelines, Joule AI extensibility, and the broken Ariba Source-to-Pay handoff (42%→87.8% gap), meaning throttling this service will cascade failures across Finance, AI, and operational domains critical to services firms managing complex client engagements and vendor ecosystems.
The fragmented Ariba Source-to-Pay flow with $20K AP leakage and sourcing awards not feeding buying mirrors a broader enterprise pattern of activating headline solutions while skipping integration—a pattern that in Professional Services translates directly to margin erosion, delayed billing cycles, and diminished partner/subcontractor management efficiency.
Convene an immediate cross-L1 CPEA rebalancing session with VS Service to redirect the $49K idle BDC Public and dormant Finance CPEA balances toward the overrun trajectories in BDC, Joule Studio, and Integration Suite before any net-new capacity negotiation.
Deliver an enterprise integration architecture workshop positioning Integration Suite as the shared backbone for BDC data flows, Joule AI extensions, and Ariba S2P continuity, with a prioritized activation roadmap for the dormant BTP governance and identity services.
Execute a clean-core and extensibility posture assessment to catalog where AI, integration, and procurement customizations are landing today, establishing BTP as the mandated extension layer before Joule and Ariba footprints harden non-compliant customizations into the core.
Institute a monthly enterprise architecture checkpoint spanning BAIP, Data & AI, and Finance to govern the combined ~$50K overrun exposure, remediate broken cross-domain handoffs, and align contract envelopes to VS Service's demonstrated AI-plus-integration-plus-S2P growth trajectory.

Target Corporation
YTD Consumed: $2.6M
YTD Budget: $2.4M
YTD ACV: $3.1M
Projected Consumed: $6.2M
Projected Budget: $5.8M
Projected ACV: $7.5M
Industry Insights
Target Corporation stands alone in this single-customer Retail cohort, establishing the baseline for peer comparison, but against industry best practice they are architecturally inverted — transactional systems (Ariba Buying 111%, Concur 101-116%, Integration Suite 128.5%) are overconsuming while strategic governance and intelligence layers (BDC, SAC Planning, Ariba Sourcing/Contracts, Joule Studio, SAP Build) remain dormant, a pattern that in retail translates to high transaction velocity without the margin optimization, demand sensing, and spend governance capabilities that separate market leaders from laggards.
Target's dormant BTP extensibility surface (SAP Build at 17%, Joule Studio declining, CPEA Integration Suite at $0) represents a critical retail-specific risk: without AI and low-code capabilities activated, the retailer cannot build the real-time inventory visibility, dynamic pricing, and agentic supply chain workflows that define modern omnichannel retail — leaving them operationally dependent on SAP as a system of record rather than a competitive differentiator.
Target is systematically over-purchasing Preferred Success advisory across Ariba (118%), Concur (118%), and SCM (113%) while architectural gaps persist in those same domains, indicating they are buying guidance to compensate for incomplete solution deployment rather than to accelerate transformation — a consumption pattern that inflates cost-to-serve without producing the supply chain resilience and procurement intelligence outcomes that retail demands.
Target's ~$575K projected Ariba Buying overage against dormant Ariba Sourcing/Contracts and Multi Bank Connect capacity reveals a broken Source-to-Pay architecture where transactional spend flows uncontrolled through buying and invoicing without upstream strategic sourcing governance or treasury visibility — a structural gap that in retail's margin-compressed environment directly leaks procurement savings and working capital efficiency.
The $36K Data & AI YTD shortfall and decaying SAC BI consumption at Target signals that the retailer lacks the demand forecasting, assortment planning, and customer analytics foundation that peer retail leaders use to drive inventory turns and promotional effectiveness, positioning them behind industry trajectory on insight-driven merchandising.
Convene an enterprise architecture intervention with Target that treats BDC, CPEA Integration Suite, SAP Build, and Joule Studio as a unified retail platform activation bundle, mapping each to specific retail outcomes — demand sensing, dynamic markdown optimization, and supplier collaboration — to justify platform spend through domain value.
Execute a portfolio contract rebalancing with Target that redirects unconsumed BDC ($27,840), CPEA Integration Suite ($32,058), Multi Bank Connect, and dormant Ariba Sourcing/Contracts budget toward demonstrated overconsumption in Integration Suite, Ariba Buying, and Concur to align ACV shape with actual consumption center of gravity.
Facilitate a Source-to-Pay-to-Plan architecture workshop for Target connecting Ariba Sourcing/Contracts, SAC Planning, Multi Bank Connect, and BDC to establish the closed-loop procurement governance and treasury visibility that converts their high transactional volume into controlled strategic spend and improved supplier terms.
Restructure Target's Preferred Success consumption into outcome-linked scorecards requiring measurable adoption milestones — BDC data products live, Sourcing events executed, SAC planning models deployed — before advisory hours are released, converting advisory spend from architectural compensation into transformation acceleration.

Budget vs Consumed by Customer
Border States Industries, Inc.
YTD Consumed: $410K
YTD Budget: $454K
YTD ACV: $399K
Projected Consumed: $984K
Projected Budget: $1.1M
Projected ACV: $957K
Industry Insights
Border States Industries is the sole customer in this wholesale distribution cohort, establishing the benchmark by default, yet its architectural posture reveals a bifurcated maturity pattern — operationally strong in HCM (EC + Payroll live) and T&E (full capture-to-audit chain) but strategically dormant in Commerce Cloud (32% consumption) and workforce activation (SF Compensation at $0), a misalignment that undermines the omnichannel and talent agility imperatives critical for electrical wholesale distributors competing against digitally-native rivals.
Border States' BTP foundation is structurally undersized and over-consuming (~$139K projected vs. $102K budget) while serving as the sole integration backbone for Data & AI, T&E, HCM, and Commerce domains — a single-point-of-failure architecture that, if not right-sized, will throttle the real-time inventory visibility, customer self-service, and branch-level analytics that define competitive wholesale distribution operations.
The absence of named Datasphere, SAC, or AI Core SKUs despite 210% Joule Studio overrun signals that Border States is pursuing generative AI without a governed data product layer — an architecturally rootless approach that, for a distributor managing thousands of SKUs across 100+ branches, risks AI outputs disconnected from master data integrity and demand sensing accuracy.
Border States owns architecturally complete process suites in HCM and Finance/T&E but three critical integration bridges — SF Compensation↔Payroll, Concur Expense↔Finance posting (May $507 cliff), and Commerce Cloud↔Customer/Order data — remain broken or dark, meaning the customer has paid for end-to-end process automation but operates fragmented workflows that erode the speed-to-serve and working capital efficiency wholesale distributors require.
Approximately $120K of combined dormant entitlement across Commerce Cloud, Talent Intelligence Hub, and SF Compensation represents funded but unrealized transformation capacity — a commercial posture where Border States pays for digital distributor capabilities (omnichannel selling, workforce planning, variable pay optimization) while operating a transactional footprint typical of a prior-generation wholesaler.
Initiate a consolidated CPEA re-baselining engagement with Border States that treats over-consuming foundation/T&E layers and dormant Commerce/Talent/Compensation entitlements as a single commercial equation rather than isolated negotiations, converting budget imbalance into activation runway.
Convene a cross-domain integration architecture workshop targeting the three broken bridges (SF Compensation↔Payroll, Concur Expense↔Finance, Commerce Cloud↔Order/Customer data) to convert disconnected suites into closed-loop processes that deliver the order-to-cash velocity and workforce cost control wholesale distributors need.
Commission a BTP service-mix decomposition to establish which named Data & AI services are carrying workload and architect a governed data foundation (Datasphere, SAC) before Joule expansion outpaces master data integrity — critical for a distributor where AI-driven demand sensing and pricing optimization depend on trusted product and customer data.
Position the ~$120K dormant entitlement as funded runway for Border States' omnichannel and workforce growth agenda through a capability-activation workshop with CIO and LoB owners, reframing commercial overhang as strategic investment in counter-based selling, eCommerce expansion, and branch-level talent optimization.
